question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 44133/22-27, to detail (i) the number of staff who work exclusively on EA TV; and (ii) the number of staff who work as part of the Education Authority's wider communication and productions team.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of children's respite services.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the recent pavement repairs on Moira Main Street, outside of the doctor's surgery, meets adequate standards.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 39614/22-27, to detail the amount that was spent on pavement repairs on Moira Main Street, outside of the doctor's surgery.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail when anonymised regional data on incidents of bullying-type behaviour in schools will be published.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made on the impact that the classification of a young person as having a learning disability or learning difficulty has on their access to post-19 services.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any current pathways and supports in place for young people with additional needs, leaving a special school as they transition from children to adult services.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any tailored referral and treatment pathways that exist in Northern Ireland, for adults and young people with a learning disability and present with an eating disorder.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the delivery of the new Education Information Solutions system is (a) on schedule; and (b) within budget; and (ii) an update on progress to date.
To ask the Minister of Education whether Bright Start funding will continue for 2026-2027.
answer_text: The Executive has not yet agreed a budget for 2026-27; however, to provide clarity for affected organisations, I have agreed an Interim Resource Budget for the first three months of the 202627 financial year. As a result, the Department of Health has received confirmation of an interim three-month resource funding allocation in respect of the administration and delivery of the Bright Start School Age Childcare Grant Scheme from 1 April 2026 to 30 June 2026.Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount spent on (i) EA TV; and (ii) podcasts created by the Education Authority, in the last three years.
answer_text: DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATIONPaul Givan, MinisterRathgael House, Balloo RoadBangor, BT19 7PR	Michelle Guy MLAParliament Buildings BallymiscawStormont01/05/2026AQW 44133/22-27Michelle Guy MLA has asked:To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount spent on EA TV; and podcasts created by the Education Authority, in the last three years.ANSWEREA TV costs over the last 3 years can be broken down as follows:Cost categoryAmount (£)Staff Costs (including pension contributions)530,702Running Costs (video server and licence fees)2,752Podcast Costs (production and platform streaming costs)-Total EA TV Costs533,454There is no additional cost to the Education Authority for the production of podcasts as these are part of the service provision and scope of the EA TV service.Paul Givan MLAMinister of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 34399/22-27; (ii) AQW 42439/22-27; (iii) AQW 43004/22-27; and (iv) AQW 43250/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will request a further review of Pupil Allocation Numbers for non-statutory settings.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the next steps they will take to regenerate the Maze Long Kesh site.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 42344/22-27, to detail (i) the current percentage of pothole repairs checked for quality; and (ii) how this has changed over the last three years.
answer_text: As outlined in my answer to AQW 42344/22-27 defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor. On completion of the repair, my officials undertake a percentage check to assess the quality of the pothole repair and confirm works for payment if the repair meets the required threshold.Section Office staff aim to undertake a minimum 10% check of repairs, however these checks are not subjected to formal monitoring and as such the Department cannot comment on trend data over the last three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of elderly carers who are supporting adults with complex needs; and (ii) whether comprehensive data is collected on this.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 43130/22-27, to detail the number of placements in (i) primary; (ii) secondary; and (iii) special, schools that need to be created.

To ask the Minister of Health whether there is any policy or guidance in place to assist carers in planning for the future care of dependants with complex needs, particularly in the event of illness or death.
answer_text: A range of relevant Health and Social Care (HSC) support mechanisms and guidance are in place to support planning for the long-term care of people with complex needs and including the needs of their carers.Carers’ Assessments are provided under the Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002 and, which enable carers to discuss their own needs future and long care arrangements, and contingency planning  in the event  an emergency.The HSC ‘Care management, provision of Services and Charging Guidance’ Circular (2010) provides Trusts with guidance on the care management process of assessment and case management for short or long-term needs, including for people with complex needs. For those individuals who, due to the complexity of their needs require a varying range of supports, the provision of services will be planned and co-ordinated by an identified HSC professional within an individual case management approach. This guidance is currently being reviewed and updated by my Department.The Learning Disability Service Model (LDSM) in Northern Ireland has completed public consultation and is currently in the analysis and finalisation phase. The LDSM sets out ambitions for people with complex needs to receive lifelong, coordinated support involving their families and carers, that is tailored to individual needs and enables choice and control across their lives.The Department will continue to work with HSC Trusts and key stakeholders to ensure carers of people with complex needs are supported and are able to access appropriate advice and assistance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to identify and support families who may be struggling with caring responsibilities for adults with complex needs.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the important role families play in caring for adults with complex needs and is committed to ensuring that carers are appropriately identified and supported.Health and Social Care Trusts have a statutory responsibility to assess any adult who appears to require care or support. Adults or their families may request an assessment at any stage, either directly through their local Trust or via referral from a GP, hospital or community professional. Assessments are provided free of charge and consider functional ability, health and safety, risks and support needs, as well as the views of the adult and their support network.Planning and review of care arrangements are expected to take place at key intervals, including transition from children’s services, following hospital discharge, or where needs change, to help identify emerging pressures on families at an early stage.Carers can also request a carer’s assessment under the Carers and Direct Payments Act (Northern Ireland) 2002. This assessment considers the impact of caring on the individual, their ability to continue caring safely and the support required, including respite, practical assistance and/or emotional support.Where assessment of need indicates that additional support is required, Trusts determine eligibility for packages of care using regionally agreed criteria, with a focus on supporting individuals to remain living at home where possible. For those whose needs can no longer be met in the community, residential care may also be considered following assessment where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of special schools able to run summer schemes in summer 2026.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) anticipates that uptake for summer scheme provision will be similar to last year, when 33 Special Schools participated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support, outside of nursing provision, provided to special schools to ensure the safety of those taking part in summer schemes.
answer_text: The Education Authority, Department of Health and my department will continue to work closely in order to operationalise summer schemes, including identifying each child’s needs in advance and putting the right support in place; completing site risk assessments; ensuring appropriate staffing levels and training; and providing oversight of activities for children and young people throughout the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 34399/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when he was first made aware that there was an issue with nursing provision for special school summer schemes; (ii) when he last made contact with the Minister of Health regarding these issues.
answer_text: Officials indicated a number of weeks ago that challenges around inter alia, on-site nursing provision may have prevented summer schemes from proceeding in 2026.  Lines of communication remain open at all times between myself and the Minister of Health.  Following discussions on 27 March, we issued a joint statement confirming a solution to nursing provision which will enable special school summer schemes to proceed safely.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions that are being taken to ensure special school summer schemes can go ahead this year.
answer_text: I have tasked the Chief Nursing Officer to meet with the five regional HSC Trust nursing leaders to develop plans to meet the Education Authority’s request for a registered nurse to be present on each site to ensure the summer schemes in Special Schools can proceed this year. Some key data has been requested from EA to enable the HSC to fulfil this request and we await receipt.Whilst extremely challenging, my Department is committed to making every effort to source the identified additional nursing cover, involving appropriate staff with the right skills and knowledge including the appropriate children’s safeguarding training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any alternative provisions being put in place, should special school summer schemes not go ahead this year.
answer_text: Following my direction, officials in the Education Authority liaised with the Department of Health and concerns regarding on-site nursing provision to support the safe running of Special School Summer Schemes for 2026 have now been addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount it would cost to provide adequate nursing provision for special school summer schemes this year.
answer_text: Recognising the importance of these Summer Schemes to the children attending them and their families, I have tasked the Chief Nursing Officer to work with the nursing leaders across our five regional HSC Trusts to develop plans to meet the Education Authority’s request for the presence of a registered nurse at each SEN school summer scheme in 2026. Whilst this will be extremely challenging, my Department is committed to making every effort to source the identified additional nursing cover, involving appropriate staff with the right skills and knowledge including the appropriate children’s safeguarding training.   Until the plan is completed, however, it is not possible to estimate the cost of implementing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions being taken to ensure special school summer schemes can go ahead this year.
answer_text: Officials indicated a number of weeks ago that challenges around inter alia, on-site nursing provision may have prevented summer schemes from proceeding in 2026.  Lines of communication remain open at all times between myself and the Minister of Health.  Following discussions on 27 March, we issued a joint statement confirming a solution to nursing provision which will enable special school summer schemes to proceed safely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to expand the Women's Centre Childcare Fund.
answer_text: There are no plans for an expansion of the current provision of the Womens Child Care Centre Fund. This fund was established as an interim measure; Strategic Investment Board advised against expansion in advance of an agreed system wide childcare framework.My officials are currently working on proposals on the future of the funding in light of the Executive’s Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department monitors incidents of families being denied visiting access to children who are in Health and Social Care Trust-led disability residential care.

To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Minister of State for Care stating that the UK Government intend to improve visitation rights and end blanket bans on contact of relatives of people with with learning disabilities in care, whether any assessment has been made regarding action to be taken in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Guidance around appropriate arrangements for visiting across a range of health and social care settings was developed and enhanced throughout the pandemic period.  The current guidance, including the detail of the Care Partner scheme, is published on my Department’s website. The guidance is predicated on the principle that there should be no unreasonable restrictions placed on any patient or resident to prevent them from being able to receive visitors.Work is being planned to complete a light touch review of the visiting guidance, to broaden its focus and remove the more Covid specific references that remain throughout it.My Department remains committed to embedding the principles of person-centred visiting, co-production, and meaningful contact across all care settings. Officials will continue to work with families, providers, and partners to seek to maintain and strengthen the principles that made visiting guidance and Care Partner arrangements work well: flexibility, collaboration, compassion, and shared commitment to patients’ and residents’ wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the work his Department is doing as part of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.
answer_text: My department is actively driving forward Action 6 of the ‘Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework’ and Delivery Plan. Central to this is the development of a Healthy Relationships Action Plan, which will coordinate targeted interventions for children and young people and strengthen their understanding of healthy, respectful relationships. As part of this work, we are developing a suite of education led actions for schools and education settings. These will ensure that staff feel supported, confident and appropriately equipped with the resources they need to address these issues effectively, and within the limits of their professional role.Schools are at the forefront of this agenda and as Minister I am committed to ensuring they have the tools required to take this important conversation forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on placements for children with special educational needs for the 2026-2027 academic year.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that as of 27 March 2026, there is a shortfall of approximately 384 special education places for September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action being taken in partnership with the Minister of Health to provide early support for any parents who are struggling with teaching their children how to toilet themselves independently.
answer_text: Department officials have engaged with pre-school leaders who advised that the issue of children starting pre-school unable to toilet themselves independently, primarily relates to children with medical or special educational needs and/or penultimate aged children. The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy includes an action to, “make legislative changes, subject to consultation, to amend the age range for funded statutory pre-school provision, removing the current requirement to enrol children in their penultimate pre-school year.” Consultation on the draft Strategy closed on 24 March 2026. My officials are now analysing responses received with a view to preparing the final Strategy, which I intend to bring forward for Executive approval in due course. Implementation of the actions will be subject to the required budget being made available by the Executive. Early intervention across education and health services supports children to develop independence in toileting. The DE Sure Start programme, delivered in collaboration with DoH can signpost families to appropriate health related guidance and support.   The Getting Ready to Learn Programme also promotes parental involvement in early learning, including the development of toileting skills. Additionally, each pre-school setting has an allocated Health Visitor who can provide toileting advice, offer guidance to parents and where needed, targeted support. For school aged children, or children with physical or developmental needs, the School Nursing Service is available to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department's implementation of the recommendations in the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman report, reference 202000405.
answer_text: The Department has accepted and implemented all the recommendations set out in the Northern Ireland Public Services Ombudsman report, reference 202000405.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any assessment of concerns from school leaders and staff regarding the number of children with no specific medical or special educational needs starting pre-schools unable to toilet themselves independently.
answer_text: Department officials have engaged with pre-school leaders who advised that the issue of children starting pre-school unable to toilet themselves independently, primarily relates to children with medical or special educational needs and/or penultimate aged children. The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy includes an action to, “make legislative changes, subject to consultation, to amend the age range for funded statutory pre-school provision, removing the current requirement to enrol children in their penultimate pre-school year.” Consultation on the draft Strategy closed on 24 March 2026. My officials are now analysing responses received with a view to preparing the final Strategy, which I intend to bring forward for Executive approval in due course. Implementation of the actions will be subject to the required budget being made available by the Executive. Early intervention across education and health services supports children to develop independence in toileting. The DE Sure Start programme, delivered in collaboration with DoH can signpost families to appropriate health related guidance and support.   The Getting Ready to Learn Programme also promotes parental involvement in early learning, including the development of toileting skills. Additionally, each pre-school setting has an allocated Health Visitor who can provide toileting advice, offer guidance to parents and where needed, targeted support. For school aged children, or children with physical or developmental needs, the School Nursing Service is available to provide support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of parents who have completed positive parenting programmes, delivered or commissioned by Health and Social Care Trusts, Sure Start, or through Children and Young People's Strategic Partnership-supported Family Support Hubs, in each of the last five years, broken down by (i) programme type; and (ii) Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following the Minister of Health's comments that the Delivery Unit will be considering how to progress post-19 special educational needs provision, to outline any specific work that will be carried out in the remainder of the 2022-2027 mandate.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the work she is undertaking to increase investment in rural roads, given the condition of many roads and the impact on road safety, damage to vehicles and rural connectivity.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of maintaining and improving the condition of our road network, particularly in rural areas, given the impact on road safety, vehicle damage and connectivity.As part of my commitment to the maintenance of rural roads, an initial £5 million was allocated to the Roads Recovery Fund, which principally targets rural roads. As part of the December monitoring round, I was able to increase this the funding to the Roads Recovery Fund up to a total of £9 million pounds to deliver real benefits across the rural network.Further to this funding, a public consultation has now concluded on my draft Road Maintenance Strategy, which seeks to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, as a means of improving resilience. The Strategy proposes a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment, supported by a more sustainable maintenance regime.My officials are currently considering the consultation responses in detail, and I intend to finalise the Road Maintenance Strategy as soon as possible following publication of the consultation report.I have also committed £1.3 million to the provision of a groundbreaking, full digital survey of the North’s entire road network. This will provide a level of detailed, consistent data that will support more efficient, evidencebased decisions on maintenance interventions.The information from this digital stocktake will assist officials in taking a more targeted approach to routine maintenance activities, such as patching, helping to ensure the best possible use of limited resources across the network.These measures are designed to maximise the benefits of every pound invested in road maintenance, including on rural roads.However, I have been clear that these initiatives alone cannot address the consequences of many years of underinvestment. I will therefore continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure appropriate levels of funding to improve the quality of our roads and to support delivery of the priorities set out in the Programme for Government.In addition, I have invested heavily through the Roads Recovery Fund, a significant proportion of which is focused on addressing conditions on rural roads and supporting local connectivity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW40506/22-27 whether any assessment has been made on (i) the number of missed 3+ year reviews; (ii) the level of data on late reviews; and (iii) the information his Department have on the impact of missed reviews.
answer_text: The 3+ year review is not part of the current core Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF) dataset submitted by Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs). As a result, HSCTs do not routinely report performance on 3+ reviews, and no regional analysis is undertaken on completion, lateness, or impact. This limits the level of assessment, noted below, that can be made across points (i)–(iii).All HSCTs report that noncompletion of 3+ year reviews can occur for reasons such as parental refusal, absence, movement between preschool settings, or questionnaire returned without attendance. HSCTs have also highlighted a range of mitigations to limit the impact of noncompletion, including making repeated attempts to complete the review, undertaking followup by the named Health Visitor before transfer to the School Nursing service, and ensuring families continue to have access to the universal Health Visiting service should any concerns arise.Data on late 3+ year reviews is limited. The 3+ review may be completed at any point during the preschool year, meaning children may be aged either three or four depending on school entry eligibility. This wider timeframe makes it difficult to define lateness on a consistent regional basis. Only the Northern HSCT records late reviews; all other HSCTs report that this information is not held.No HSCT holds specific data on the impact of missed 3+ year reviews, as this review is not currently included in the core dataset submitted through the HCHF reporting process. The 3+ review is an important contact as it is the final formal Health Visiting review before a child enters school and provides an opportunity to identify any emerging needs. The refreshed HCHF framework, once implemented, will strengthen data capture and reporting by mandating standardised collection of 3+ review data across all HSCTs and enabling greater flexibility as to where the review can be completed to help reduce missed contacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in the absence of HMRC carrying out a local impact assessment on proposed changes to the wear and tear allowance for childminders, when his Department will review the potential impact this will have on the childcare sector.
answer_text: Taxation policy is a retained matter, and as such the Treasury and His Majesty’s Revenue and Customs (HMRC) have responsibility for tax policy and administration across the United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland. This includes decisions taken under their Making Tax Digital programme, and the removal of the 10 per cent wear-and-tear allowance previously available to childminders.Under established Executive protocols, the Department of Finance leads on engagement with Treasury and HMRC regarding tax matters.My Department has received an increasing number of representations from childminders and sector bodies outlining concerns about the proposed removal of the allowance. These concerns relate both to the potential financial implications and to the increased administrative burden associated with the new arrangements.In light of this, I have written to the Minister of Finance to:inform him of the volume of correspondence received on this issue;seek clarification on whether HMRC has undertaken any analysis of the potential impact on childminders in Northern Ireland; andrequest that the Department of Education be kept informed of any developments.I have also asked that I receive a copy of HMRC’s formal guidance once it becomes available, which I understand was expected in early 2026.My Department does not hold detailed financial information for individual childminders, the majority of whom operate as private commercial enterprises, and there is understandable reluctance within the sector to share business-sensitive data — a point also highlighted in the 2025 Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support report undertaken for the Department for the Economy. This limits our ability to undertake any robust local assessment of financial loss or individual impact.Looking ahead, and subject to Executive funding, the proposed Business Advisory Service within the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy may provide scope for more structured analysis of business pressures within the sector, including those arising from taxation or regulatory changes. My officials will monitor any impact the HMRC changes may have on childminder provision at a strategic level in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the proposed development of a business support scheme for childcare providers.
answer_text: My Department is progressing the development of a specialised business support service for childcare providers. The need for such a service was identified in the Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Market Research report. Officials are currently developing the specification of the proposed service and engaging with stakeholders both across and beyond Government to seek their input and determine the most appropriate delivery model. The childcare sector is complex and highly regulated. It is made up of a wide range of business models, from individual childminders to voluntary and community organisations and private providers. In addition, it is vital to allow parents, and particularly mothers to work. It is therefore essential that the Childcare Business Support Service reflects the sector’s diversity and is designed to meet those needs as effectively as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41503/22-27, to detail the number of times the Education Authority has invoked contract management controls, in the last five years, broken down by (a) requesting clarification from contractors; (b) issuing noncompliance notices; (c) requesting rework at no additional cost; (d) escalation to senior managers and the Head of Maintenance for contractual remedies.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Maintenance Service issues approximately 60,000 task orders each year to contractors for work across the EA school estate.The information you have requested, detailing the number of times the EA has invoked contract management controls in the last five years broken down by (a) requesting clarification from contractors; (b) issuing noncompliance notices; (c) requesting rework at no additional cost; (d) escalation to senior managers and the Head of Maintenance for contractual remedies is not readily available in the form requested and can only be collated at disproportionate cost. The EA has confirmed that the new Term Service Contracts will emphasise key performance indicators targeting poor contractor responses, completion times, workmanship quality and reducing repetitive site visits, with a stronger emphasis on achieving first-time site fixes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any work has been undertaken to develop a function within the online reporting portal, that would allow users to receive email updates on the progress of faults they have reported.
answer_text: My department receives approximately 90,000 road faults and 60,000 lighting faults annually.  The system for reporting such faults was first introduced by the Department in 2015, and is now available for members of the public to report instances directly using this online system Report a pothole or other surface defect | nidirect or via the NI Direct contact centre.When a fault is reported, an automated acknowledgement email is issued which provides an indication of the priority of the fault and a webpage link and reference number to facilitate tracking.The reference number can be used to track updates to the fault report via the dedicated NI Direct webpage Track progress of a reported road fault or issue | nidirect which will provide details of any investigation and where possible an indication of the timescale for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of times a payment to a contractor was withheld or delayed, as a result of failing to meet her Department’s quality standards during inspection, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department may withhold or delay payments to contractors pending resolution of queries or completion of necessary remedial works. While the Department's financial system does record details of disputed invoices, the information held is insufficient to separately identify payments withheld because of issues identified during inspection of works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the introduction of V-Levels in England.
answer_text: My Department is currently considering the potential implications of the reform of post-16 vocational pathways in England.  In particular, the potential impact on the range of vocational qualifications that will be available to Northern Ireland schools in future years. My officials have been engaging with officials in the Department for Education in England who are leading these reforms, as well as liaising with officials in the Department for the Economy who also have a policy interest in potential changes to the vocational qualifications landscape. That engagement will continue as details of the proposed changes emerge.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of pothole repairs, carried out each year, that are subject to a quality assurance inspection.
answer_text: Maintenance staff inspect and make-safe defects by working to address the highest priority defects as fast as possible, in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.Defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor. On completion of the repair, my officials undertake a percentage check to assess the quality of the pothole repair and confirm works for payment if the repair meets the required threshold.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) percentage, of pothole repairs that (a) failed to meet the required standards at first inspection; and (b) required further remedial work, broken down by Section Office, for each of the last five years.
answer_text: Maintenance staff inspect and make-safe defects by working to address the highest priority defects as fast as possible, in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.Defects are instructed for repair either through our internal or external contractor. On completion of the repair, my officials undertake a percentage check to assess the quality of the pothole repair and confirm works for payment if the repair meets the required threshold.If a repair fails to satisfy the required standards, officials will request that it be rectified and subjected to further inspection. This process will continue until the repair fully complies with all stipulated requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 39612/22-27.
answer_text: I provided a response to AQW 39612/22-27 on 16 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will continue this year.
answer_text: The continuation of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme was announced on 11 March: Education Minister announces continuation of Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme | Department of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, as part of his changes to AS and A Levels, whether pupils will have the choice to take part of the new three unit assessment at the end of year 13.
answer_text: Pupils will be able to take one unit of the new modular A level at the end of Year 13 or all three units at the end of year 14. CCEA will designate one of the three units of assessment for each A level subject that can be taken at the end of Year 13. The designated unit will be worth 30% with the remaining 70% of assessment being taken in year 14.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will conduct further consultation on extending A Levels over two years.
answer_text: Under my new Policy Framework for General Qualifications, CCEA A levels will continue to be delivered over two years. The new three-unit modular A level structure will allow pupils to be entered for one unit at the end of Year 13, with two units being taken in Year 14. Alternatively, all three units may be taken at the end of Year 14. The consultation on the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels ran from September 2025 until November 2025 and received over 3500 responses as well as almost 4500 from the young persons’ version.  In addition, my Department held several post-consultation events in January 2026 to seek further feedback from school leaders young people on the proposals before any decisions were made. Considering the extensive consultation process already conducted by my Department, there are no plans for further consultation on A levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he has taken to ensure more pupils have access to pre-vocational, and technical, education.
answer_text: Pupils in key stage 4 and post-16 are entitled to have access to both general and applied qualifications. Under the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006, provision is made which requires the Board of Governors of a grant-aided school to provide access to twenty-one qualifying courses of which not fewer than one third should be applied (vocational) courses and not fewer than one third shall be general (academic) courses.  A “qualifying course” is a course of study which leads to an external qualification and the Board of Governors of a grant-aided school can provide access to qualifying courses either in their school or through collaboration with another provider.A list of approved courses is maintained by the Council for Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment and this provides a good mix of both applied and general courses for pupils.  My Department continues to work with the Department for the Economy on the actions contained within the 14-19 Framework. This includes consideration of how more effective collaboration can be achieved between schools, colleges and training providers. High quality careers education and advice are also important in ensuring learners are aware of pathways and qualifications available to them.  Young people are supported through independent, impartial information and guidance about the range of pathways, including those vocational and technical routes available to them.  My officials are working with colleagues in the Department for the Economy to implement several actions that will increase opportunities for work-related learning and engagement with employers for both learners and their teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the cost of increasing the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to 20 per cent; and (ii) whether this increase will occur from April 2026, as suggested in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I have extended the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%, however, as the Budgets for 2026-27, including the final allocation for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, have not yet been set by the Executive  I am not in a position to provide any commitments to increase the percentage contribution for the NICSS.Any increase in the subsidy rate above 15% would require significant additional recurrent funding. Moving from 15% to 20% represents a one third uplift in the level of subsidy and would therefore require a broadly proportionate increase in the overall scheme budget.Any decision regarding future increases will be considered in the context of the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 41503/22-27, to detail the number of complaints received regarding maintenance work, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Further to the holding response that issued to you on 13 March 2026, the Education Authority (EA) has advised that there has been a total of 13 complaints regarding maintenance work since records commenced in December 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the timeline for; and (ii) who is involved in, the review of vocational qualifications.
answer_text: In the coming months my Department plans to review the range of vocational and applied qualifications that are currently approved for teaching in Northern Ireland schools. Planning for this review is at a very early stage as my primary focus, and that of my officials, has been on reforming GCSEs and A levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when special schools will receive confirmation of funding for summer schemes.
answer_text: It is expected that special schools will be notified about summer schemes before the end of March 2026.Any decision on running summer schemes in special schools is an operational one for the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support that her Department has made available to provide childcare support, for those in further and higher education.
answer_text: The Department has a number of student support schemes which provide childcare support to those in further and higher education.For those studying in Further Education the Care to Learn (NI) Scheme provides financial support towards registered childcare costs for students who are over compulsory school age and have not reached the age of 20 before the start of their course. For those Further Education students aged 18 and over, Further Education Grants and the Hardship Fund provide financial support with registered childcare costs.In respect of Higher Education, the Department provides a means-tested childcare grant to help towards the costs of registered or approved childcare for eligible students studying at Higher Education Providers. In addition, the Department makes available Student Support Funds (also known as Hardship Funds) through the local Higher Education Institutions. These provide assistance to those who without additional support may be unable to continue with their course, potentially including help with childcare costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to address the frequent flooding on Warren Park, Lisburn.
answer_text: My officials visited Warren Park on 27 February 2026 to assess the condition of the road gullies in the area when three of the four gullies were fully cleaned.  An issue was identified with the outlet pipe in the remaining gully, and arrangements are in place to have this addressed by the end of March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31462/22-27, for an update on when the review of delegated nursing tasks in relation to direct payment carers will be published.
answer_text: My Department commissioned the Northern Ireland Practice and Education Council for Nursing and Midwifery (NIPEC) to lead a review of the delegation of healthcare tasks, with the aim of gaining a comprehensive understanding of current practices and identifying viable pathways for safe and effective delegation in line with existing guidelines and legislation.Given the complexity of the issues involved, the review has taken time to progress through the necessary stages of evidence gathering, stakeholder input, and professional scrutiny. The work is now at an advanced stage and is expected to be finalised shortly.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline the plans for MLA induction in the next Assembly mandate.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail all funding spent by her Department from the Executive funding for Early Learning and Childcare, in the last two years
answer_text: My Department was allocated £415,000 from the Executive to fund a range of skills interventions to support Early Learning and Childcare. This funding supported the development of 12 accredited microcredential courses as well as research into higher level qualifications that will support individuals progression into management roles. Additionally, my Department spent £153,288 on the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the intended services of the Childcare Business Advisory Service, referenced in the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: My Department is leading on the development of a Childcare Business Support Service as part of the Executive’s Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy to strengthen the long-term financial sustainability of the childcare sector.  The service will provide targeted, specialist support to childcare providers, including childminders, voluntary and community settings, and private providers, aimed at helping them improve financial planning, resilience and operational sustainability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any engagement his Department has had with school leaders, regarding improving how maintenance procurement works; and (ii) whether alternative models have been considered, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for maintenance of controlled and maintained schools.  The EA has engaged regularly with school leaders in learning lessons from legacy Term Service Contracts (TSCs) and how maintenance contracts could be improved.  This engagement highlighted the following key areas for improvement:some poor contractor response/completion timesquality of workmanship.repetitive site visits by contractors.ineffective health & safety.cost certainty for Local Management of Schools (LMS) works.the need for improved management information systems.the need for a greater flexibility for schools to complete non-technical minor repairs outside that of the TSCs.As a result of this feedback, the EA has undertaken a new procurement model, comprising an 18-lot structure designed around the eleven council areas, with the Building and Mechanical & Electrical works procured separately within six procurement exercises.  The implementation of these procurements will lead to the delivery of significant changes including completion of task orders in compliance with specified Key Performance Indicators, including:quality of workmanship.adherence to health and safety requirements.live reporting/tracking of maintenance works.adherence to task order response and completion times.The new contracts include an LMS addendum for cost certainty, which means that the most requested maintenance activities have set prices fixed for the duration of the contract, to provide assurance and transparency to school leaders.There is also live integration with EA management information systems, resulting in live reporting on all task orders whilst providing early warning of contractual non-performance.Ten of the 18 lots have been successfully implemented across the schools’ estate, with the remaining contractual lots scheduled to commence from 1 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) options open to school leaders to complain about maintenance work carried out by Education Authority approved contractors; and (ii) protocols in place to handle those complaints.
answer_text: Although there is no formal complaints process, school leaders can report issues and concerns to the Education Authority (EA) via their Area Maintenance Officer and the Maintenance Help Desk in the first instance. When required, these concerns will be escalated to Senior Managers and Head of Maintenance.This process facilitates the reporting of poor workmanship, contractor performance and requests for follow-up inspections or rectification works. When a complaint is made about a contractor’s performance the following protocols are followed:EA tracking of the complaint so it can be followed up with the school leader.Review of the contractor’s work by EA Maintenance Service against the contractual Key Performance Indicators (KPI), with penalty action if required.Examination of job records, contractor submission documents, the Maintenance Audit regime or price lists.If the complaint is regarding the level of workmanship, the EA invokes standard contract management controls that include the following:Requesting clarifications from contractors.Issuing noncompliance notices.Requesting rework at no additional cost.Escalating to Senior Managers and the Head of Maintenance for contractual remedies.Going forward with the new Term Service Contracts, stakeholder engagement meetings will be held with school leaders as a formal mechanism to:Gather feedback from schools, principals, contractors and internal maintenance teams.Review service performance, KPIs, customer satisfaction and areas for improvement.Provide updates on contracts, operational delivery, statutory compliance, and upcoming changes.Ensure alignment between Maintenance Service, schools, contractors, and senior leadership

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has made representations to the UK Government regarding children born in July and August losing out on Tax Free Childcare support, due school year differences between Great Britain and Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Minister for Education wrote to me on 23 February to highlight this specific issue and to request that my Department raise it with the Treasury, my officials have raised this matter with Treasury Officials.Supporting families through affordable childcare provision is essential to improving labour market participation and driving economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 40510/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any policy in relation to the completion of the autism medical questionnaire required for the Education Authority to process alternative transport arrangements for school pupils, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: There is no separate “autism-specific” questionnaire policy in relation to HSC staff completing Special Educational Needs (SEN) assessment forms, autism is included under long-term medical needs. Trust clinicians complete the Education Authority (EA) SEN medical form when clinically appropriate. The EA SEN process requires a medical questionnaire, completed by a Consultant, confirming how a child’s presenting needs, not their autism diagnosis, impacts a child’s ability to use standard transport.  The SEN Code of Practice is an important guidance document that all statutory educational settings/schools must follow when making decisions about identifying, assessing and making provision for children/young people who have, or may have, special educational needs. No Health & Social Care Trust has its own policy relating to SEN.The Code explicitly requires co-operation between health and social services and education providers during identification, assessment and provision for children with SEN. This includes:Sharing relevant health information (where appropriate and with consent)Participating in multi-disciplinary decision-makingAdvising schools or the EA when a pupil has needs that may affect learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) who is represented on the task and finish group for restraint and seclusion; and (ii) the dates of any meetings, since the group was established in July 2025.
answer_text: Invitations to the join the Restraint and Seclusion Task and Finish Group were issued to selected relevant stakeholders in November 2024, and the first meeting of those who accepted the invitation was held in December 2024. (i) Membership of Restraint and Seclusion Task and Finish Group is as follows:Hill Croft School Clarawood School Lakewood School Brownlow Integrated College Belfast Boys Model SchoolTullygally Primary SchoolCastle Gardens Primary School (withdrew from the group on the 29 August 2025 due to competing priorities and time constraints)Loughshore EOTAS Treetops EOTAS Education Authority Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People (Resigned from the group on the 13 August 2025)Children’s Law Centre (Resigned from the group on the 13 August 2025)Public Health Agency (PHA)Royal College of Nursing (Resigned from the group on the 27 February 2025)(ii) The Restraint and Seclusion Task and Finish Group met on the following dates:5 December 2024 – initial meeting.9 January 2025 – meeting between DE officials and education representatives from the Task and Finish Group. 20 February 2025 – meeting between DE officials, education representatives from the Task and Finish Group, NICCY, and CLC.10 April 2025 – full meeting of the Task and Finish Group. PHA did not attend.16 June 2025 – DE officials convened a health and education multi-agency workshop, which included the Task and Finish Group, along with wider stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when he will publish the findings from the review of Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12; and (ii) any reasons for the delay in publishing this.
answer_text: My department will be publishing the consultation response document in the coming weeks.While the consultation was carried out in 2025, carrying out an analysis of the 495 responses received from a wide range of stakeholders with contrasting views has regrettably taken longer than expected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to bring forward legislation to establish a compulsory register of all children not in school, including those who have never been registered with a school, similar to the provisions in the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill currently progressing through Westminster, in order to strengthen safeguarding and ensure that every child is receiving a suitable education.
answer_text: I am aware of the Children’s Wellbeing and Schools Bill which is progressing through the various stages at Westminster. My officials are considering the implications of the Bill recognising the distinct legislative context in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Education Authority Transition Co-ordinators currently employed; and (ii) the number of vacancies in the team.
answer_text: There are 10 Permanent Transition Coordinators currently employed in the Service.There are currently no Transition Coordinator vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current membership of the working group on Restrictive Practices; and (ii) the number of times the group has met, in the last two years.
answer_text: (i) Membership of the Restraint and Seclusion Working Group is as follows:The Department of EducationThe Education & Training Inspectorate (ETI) The Department of Health The Department of Justice The Education Authority The Public Health Agency (ii) In the last two years the Restraint and Seclusion Working Group met on the following dates to receive updates on the progress to develop draft guidance:15 April 2025 – Joint meeting with the Restraint and Seclusion Reference Group (Neither the Department of Health nor the Public Health Agency attended this meeting)10 May 2024 – Joint meeting with the Restraint and Seclusion Reference Group

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why DFI Eastern Division is no longer accepting correspondence from elected representatives and, is instead, directing all enquiries to the online portal, except in cases that are deemed as complicated issues; (ii) any criteria used to determine what constitutes a complicated issue; and (iii) any assessment her Department has been made of the impact of this approach on constituency casework and timely issue resolution.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether a Rural Needs Impact Assessment has been carried out in relation to DFI Eastern Division’s decision to direct elected representatives to use the online portal for enquiries, given concerns that the mapping system does not reliably identify or locate particularly rural roads in search functions; and (ii) to detail any steps her Department will take to ensure that rural constituents are not disadvantaged by this system.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current clinical pathways available for individuals diagnosed with Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome.
answer_text: Elhers-Danlos patients are currently referred by GP to Rheumatology services across the five Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland for multidisciplinary support for joint issues, physiotherapy and pain management. There are currently no plans to commission a dedicated specialist clinic for Elhers-Danlos Syndrome in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to develop adult safeguarding guidance and policy for the education sector.
answer_text: Officials are currently working on a refreshed and strengthened ‘Safeguarding in Education Guidance’ document, and it is intended that this will set out clearly expectations for adult safeguarding in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any resource provided to the Children in Entertainment and Employment Team functions, in terms of its statutory requirement.
answer_text: The Children in Entertainment and Employment Team functions fall under the Education Authority’s (EA) Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) which is a core EA service, and the resourcing of this team is a matter for the EA when considering its block grant allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy notes that consideration has been given to capping fees, to detail the level of scoping that was completed by his Department in respect of this.
answer_text: As part of the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, my Department undertook significant scoping and analysis of how fee capping models operate in practice, including the funding, regulatory and delivery mechanisms typically required to support them. Officials also engaged with counterparts in other jurisdictions, including the Republic of Ireland, to understand how different approaches to managing parental fees operate and the wider system supports that accompany them.Alongside this, the Department considered relevant independent evidence and research and drew on engagement with childcare providers and sector representatives through the ELC Stakeholder Engagement Forum and wider strategy development activity.This scoping informed the position reflected in the draft Strategy, recognising that there is significant fiscal, regulatory and operational implications associated with such an approach. For these reasons, the capping of fees is not included within the draft Strategy but will be kept under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to introduce or commission a dedicated specialist clinic for Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome.
answer_text: Elhers-Danlos patients are currently referred by GP to Rheumatology services across the five Health and Social Care Trusts in Northern Ireland for multidisciplinary support for joint issues, physiotherapy and pain management. There are currently no plans to commission a dedicated specialist clinic for Elhers-Danlos Syndrome in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with other Departments, regarding the impact of the Adult Protection Bill on educational institutions.
answer_text: While the Bill is primarily health led and focused on health and Social Care Trust functions, officials are liaising with colleagues from the Department of Health regarding the Adult Protection Bill and are actively considering its potential implications for the education sector.  The department has not engaged with any department other than Health at this time in respect of the Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) employment permits; and (ii) performance licenses, issued by the Education Authority, in each of  the last five years.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any consideration his Department has given to the implications of the Adult Protection Bill for educational settings.
answer_text: While the Bill is primarily health led and focused on health and Social Care Trust functions, officials are liaising with colleagues from the Department of Health regarding the Adult Protection Bill and are actively considering its potential implications for the education sector.  The department has not engaged with any department other than Health at this time in respect of the Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39515/22-27, to detail a breakdown of the type of support requested from the Child Protection Support Service.
answer_text: The Education Authority advises that the support required in each Helpline call varies accordingly to the level of risk and the specific circumstances of each child, family, and school. Each call is triaged by the receiving Designated Officer (DO), who assesses the concern and identifies the appropriate next steps. Support may include advising on immediate safeguarding actions, determining whether referrals to statutory agencies are necessary, guiding schools through referral processes, escalating practice issues to external partners, and advising on relevant lines of enquiry with children, parents, staff or other parties.Through the helpline, DOs also support school safeguarding teams to ensure their practice is aligned to legislation, Department of Education Circulars, and policy. They frequently provide mentoring, coaching, and emotional support to safeguarding staff, recognising the pressures and potential for vicarious trauma associated with the role.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 39515/22-27, for his assessment of the increase in calls to the Child Protection Support Service.
answer_text: The Education Authority Child Protection Support Service (EA CPSS) Helpline ensures access to timely and specialist safeguarding advice from Designated Officers.The EA has advised that the increased demand on the service reflects emerging safeguarding and child protection issues and complexity, including: Impact of poverty and neglect on pupilsOperation Encompass (increase in domestic violence, including information being shared with schools by PSNI)Online safety and abuse including artificially generated images and deepfakesImpact of social media/TrollingRacist behaviour and community unrest impacting on pupils, school communitiesHarmful Sexual Behaviour displayed in school by young people Allegations against staffChild Sexual Exploitation and Child Criminal ExploitationBullyingSubstance misuse (drugs and alcohol)My officials are working closely with the CPSS officers to ensure that the department’s guidance and operational support for schools reflect this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of rising childcare costs.
answer_text: Childcare fees in Northern Ireland are set by individual providers, most of whom operate as private businesses. As a result, there is a wide variation in fees across the market, reflecting differences in provision, location and operating costs.Analysis of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) data shows that childcare fees have increased by approximately 12% on average, between September 2024 and April 2025. This analysis is based on daycare settings, which represent 83% of the total subsidy payments made since April 2025.This increase was not unexpected given that providers increase their fees annually, however, wider inflationary and wage pressures across the childcare sector from April 2025 are likely to also have had an impact. In anticipation of these fee increases I increased the NICSS monthly subsidy cap for the scheme by 10% in April 2025 and raised the administrative payments to providers. While increases in fees are determined by individual childcare providers, the net cost paid by parents will vary depending on their individual circumstances and eligibility to existing financial support schemes, such as Universal Credit, Tax Free Childcare and the NICSS.  However, I acknowledge that childcare costs for some families remain unacceptably high and that is why the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, currently issued for public consultation, sets out further proposals to improve affordability for working parents while supporting a sustainable childcare sector. Through the NICSS, my Department will continue to monitor the costs of childcare to inform policy decisions going forward.

answer_text: The table below presents the available data from the South Eastern, Northern, Southern, and Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) for one-year reviews recorded as Contact Late in 2024-25. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of why 1,467 One Year Health Reviews were recorded as No Contact in 2024-25.
answer_text: While it is not possible to confirm the specific reason in every case, the most common reasons why a one-year health review may not have been completed include:•	Family relocation - movement out of Trust / NI•	Parental Choice to decline the review •	Repeated non engagement despite follow up attempts•	Vacant Caseloads – Staff capacityQuarterly reports identify outstanding year 1 health reviews, and a range of follow up processes are in place.  Additional weekly/ weekend clinics are offered to families of children with outstanding year 1 health reviews to minimise the risk of children being overlooked.  Where there is parental decline or persistent non engagement, appropriate safeguarding policies and procedures are followed in line with Trust & regional guidance.  Health Visitors will also liaise with GP where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place to develop an adult safeguarding policy within his Department.
answer_text: Officials are developing a refreshed and strengthened ‘Safeguarding in Education Guidance’ document, and it is intended that this will set out clear expectations for adult safeguarding in education. The Education Authority are also undertaking operational policy development in respect of adult safeguarding and officials will continue to engage with officers in respect of the outworkings of how schools are supported to implement this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the quality of pothole repairs in recent months; and (ii) to detail any quality inspection that is carried out to ensure repairs do not degrade quickly.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 39446 / 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any ways, Emergency Departments are supported to appropriately assess and manage patients, with rare or multi-system conditions.
answer_text: All Emergency Departments (EDs) are designed and equipped to assess and manage a broad range of presenting conditions, including complex, multisystem and rare conditions. ED clinicians are trained to identify undifferentiated and atypical presentations and to escalate care appropriately.ED teams have access to senior decisionmakers, specialty advice, regional clinical pathways and diagnostic support to assist in the management of patients with complex needs. Digital health records and established referral mechanisms enable clinicians to access relevant information and specialist input where required.My Department does not mandate specific additional systems or facilities for the assessment of patients with rare or multisystem conditions within EDs. Instead, ED services are structured to provide flexible, responsive care that can accommodate the full range of patient presentations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the care pathway and clinical oversight available, to patients with complex multi-system conditions.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the legislation to prohibit the overtaking of school buses while they are stopped to drop-off or pick-up passengers.
answer_text: I have established a Safer Journeys to School Programme within the Department to examine options to improve safety for school journeys, including legislative options. This work is evidence-led to ensure interventions are effective and proportionate.The Department remains committed to improving road safety and will continue to engage with stakeholders to deliver the most effective measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps that are being taken to ensure that patients with complex presentations are not repeatedly passed between specialties without a coordinated clinical lead.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason why non-statutory pre-school providers have a different route to refer children to Education Authority Local Impact Teams.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Education and Library Board SEN services were originally designed and scoped to only support grant-maintained preschool settings where the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice (CoP) applies fully.  As non-statutory providers do not uniformly apply the SEN CoP, their route to access EA SEN support services has remained predominantly via Community Paediatrics/Allied Health Professional referral to the Educational Psychology Service who then refer into the Local Impact Teams (LITs).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason why non-statutory pre-school providers can not accept children with additional needs as supernumerary, similar to statutory providers.
answer_text: The Education Authority makes individual arrangements for children for whom statements are maintained under Article 16 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, including, where appropriate, naming the most appropriate pre-school placement for them in that statement. This applies to placement in both statutory and non-statutory pre-school settings.Legislation requires the Department to determine an admissions number and enrolment number for each statutory school, including statutory pre-schools. “Supernumerary” placement refers to a placement that is not counted within the   statutory admissions or enrolment numbers for a statutory school.  As non-statutory pre-schools do not have statutory admissions or enrolment numbers, “supernumerary” placement is not applicable. Placements in these settings are made under Articles 10 and 12 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any options childminders, and childcare providers, have to refer children who have additional needs, for support.
answer_text: As per my Department’s Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care, if a registered childcare provider identifies a child in their care as possibly having additional needs their observations should be sensitively discussed with the child’s parents and the parents should be encouraged to seek relevant help. The childcare provider also has a responsibility to ensure that the physical environment is suitable for each child.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to stop speech and language therapy provision ahead of children entering pre-school.
answer_text: Accessing speech and language therapy in the early years of childhood is extremely important in determining the future success of a child. It is therefore vital that we work closely with our education colleagues to support children who may be struggling with their speech and communication.There are no plans to stop speech and language therapy provision ahead of children entering pre-school

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of non-statutory pre-school providers that have had their Pupil Allocation Numbers increased for the 2026-2027 intake.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) has responsibility for the allocation of Pupil Admission Numbers (PANs) for non-statutory pre-school settings participating in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  The EA wrote to all settings on 4 November 2025 to advise them of their provisional PAN allocation for the 2026/27 academic year.The 2026/27 pre-school admissions process remains ongoing at this time and the EA will continue to review local provision in all areas throughout the process and may adjust PANs for settings where it is appropriate to do so to meet demand.  The EA has advised that as of 10 February a total of 35 non-statutory settings have had their PAN increased for the 2026-2027 academic year.  Final PAN allocations for settings will not be determined until the close of Stage Two of the admissions process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment made of the postal services currently utilised by Health and Social Care Trusts for issuing patient correspondence, including appointment letters; and (ii) whether the use of economy postal products is meeting the needs of patients, particularly in light of reports of missed or late appointments due to delayed delivery.
answer_text: The Department has not carried out an assessment of the postal service currently utilised by the Health and Social Care Trusts and no assessment has been made as to whether use of economy postal products is meeting the needs of patients. However, to minimise any patient inconvenience, Trusts have implemented a dual notification system for appointment reminders. In addition to sending appointment letters to patients, Trusts also use text message reminders to notify patients of their upcoming appointments in most services. Furthermore, the Encompass system is now fully operational across Northern Ireland. All patients and service users will be able to securely access a subset of their health and social care record via the My Care application, which provides secure online access to personal health information such as appointment letters, clinic letters, medication details, and some test results. They can also view appointments and track the progress of their treatment

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce specific legislation regarding the use of e-scooters.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have no plans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI. The Department is exploring measures to raise awareness around the dangers associated with e-scooters. The Department is also monitoring the progress of a pilot scheme for rental e-scooters in Britain, as well as the impact of the legislative changes in the south which made the use of e-scooters legal and regulated their use on public roads from May 2024. This evidence base can help inform the department of what steps to take in relation to the registration and regulation of e-scooters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his efforts to improve environmental governance in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Improving environmental governance remains a priority, and following the independent review, which brought forward the key recommendation to establish and Independent Environment Protection Agency in Northern Ireland, I brought a paper to the Executive seeking agreement in principle to move forward with establishing the new body. I am not alone in finding this hugely frustrating when we consider the costs of inaction and the overwhelming support for change that would benefit everyone. I would urge my Executive colleagues to provide the support needed so this important reform can advance. In parallel, officials are considering the wider recommendations falling within DAERA’s remit, noting where elements align with existing programmes of work. This includes ongoing activity to improve transparency around environmental information and to strengthen internal systems and processes. This approach enables my Department to maintain focus on areas within its direct control, pending an Executive decision on the broader governance reforms.Environmental governance is fundamental to protecting our environment, building public trust, and ensuring Northern Ireland meets its obligations. I remain committed to driving this agenda forward and ensuring that the reforms identified by the Panel are taken forward in a structured, evidencebased and timely way.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the reason for the delay in taking forward the proposal for an Independent Commission into a sustainable school estate.
answer_text: I accepted the Independent Review of Education (IRE) panel’s recommendation (no. 20) to reconfigure the network of schools ‘in principle’. The panel determined that Northern Ireland currently has too many non-viable small schools and too many small sixth forms.  To tackle this problem – which has both educational and financial dimensions – the Panel stated that we need a new approach to Area Planning.  As part of the considerations that lead to recommendation 20 the IRE panel proposed the establishment of an Independent Planning Commission to take the lead in developing a Northern Ireland wide plan for a new network of schools.  Current plans to reconfigure the network of schools, in line with my Department’s priorities, are detailed in the Strategic Area Plan and the Education Authority’s published Operational Plans. These plans and associated actions will run through to August 2027. My Department has set out its proposals for a Five-Year Education Budget Strategy.  Consultation on the strategy closes on 3 April 2026.  The strategy includes proposals to restructure the school estate and within that context a Commission may be appointed to lead on this work.  I will make a decision on the establishment of a Commission after the responses to the consultation have been considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the special educational needs budget for schools will be set, as referenced in his written statement on a Five-Year Education Budget Strategy.
answer_text: The Five-Year Education Budget Strategy is currently out for public consultation and responses will need to be considered before budgets can be set.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any planned process for appointing an Independent Commission, to review the sustainability of the school estate.
answer_text: My Department has set out its proposals for a Five-Year Education Budget Strategy.  Consultation on the strategy closes on 3 April 2026.
