The strategy includes proposals to restructure the school estate and within that context an Independent Commission may be appointed to lead on this work.  I will make a decision on the establishment of an Independent Commission and process for doing so, if applicable, after the responses to the consultation have been considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any process used for the review of eligibility criteria for transport assistance, proposed in his written statement on a Five-Year Education Budget Strategy.
answer_text: At this stage, the reforms outlined in the five-year education budget strategy remain consultation proposals. The reforms will be finalised once the budget position is confirmed, taking account of the outcome of the public consultation. Formal governance structures, overseen by the Permanent Secretary, will ensure robust oversight as the reform programme develops. Individual reform projects will be taken forward in key areas with any reviews focussed on supporting evidence-based policy decisions to ensure that vital services remain fit for purpose, sustainable and meet the needs of pupils and families.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her officials are having to ensure drainage, utility and resurfacing work, will be coordinated with planned public realm schemes, undertaken in Hillsborough and Moira.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working in partnership with local councils and other stakeholders to support the delivery of high quality public realm schemes including those in  Hillsborough and Moira.Where schemes impact on adopted roads, my officials engage closely during the planning and design stages to assess the condition of the carriageway and associated infrastructure, including drainage systems. Whilst efforts are made to align these improvement works, it is not always possible, particularly where funding constraints exist or where the road network does not meet the necessary condition thresholds. My Department continues to operate a rolling programme of maintenance works and seeks to coordinate wherever practical to support town centre regeneration and ensure long-term benefits for road users and pedestrians.My officials will continue to engage with their local government counterparts to identify opportunities for joint delivery and ensure that statutory responsibilities for the public road are upheld throughout the development and implementation of public realm schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of pavement defects that have been fixed on Main Street, Moira, in the last four months; and (ii) any work her Department has scheduled, given the dangerous condition of the pavements.
answer_text: In the last four months, a total of 5 defects have been recorded and repaired on Main Street, Moira.In addition, sections of footpath along Main Street have been identified for patching and this work is expected to be completed by summer 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount his Department spent on legal fees on (i) the appeal against the 2022 High Court decision on the teaching of Religious Education in schools; and (ii) the subsequent Supreme Court case.
answer_text: Total legal fees to date incurred by the Department in relation to JR87 is £131,711.89, however it should be noted that total costs have not yet been finalised.   This encompasses both the appeal against the 2022 High Court decision and the subsequent Supreme Court proceedings (July 2022 to February 2026).Further costs are expected but not yet confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the compensation claims, paid by her Department for damage caused to vehicles due to road defects, broken down by constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025.Table 4.2 provides details of concluded claims in which compensation was paid over the last five full financial years, broken down by Section Office and claim type. Please note that my Department maintains information on public liability claims by Roads Section Office areas, which broadly align with Council areas. In addition, claims received in a particular financial year are not always concluded in the same year. Therefore, the concluded claims figures for a given financial year will usually include claims that were received in previous financial years.Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients who have died while on a waiting list for an initial consultant appointment, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data in a format that would allow me to report this information. There is currently no routine reporting of deaths occurring while patients are on a waiting list for an initial consultant appointment, nor is there a regional agreement on how such events should be defined or recorded. Capturing this information would require a standardised definition and consistent reporting processes across all Health and Social Care Trusts, which are not currently in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action being taken to reduce gynaecological appointments and surgeries, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Significant work is underway across the health and social care system to reduce waiting times for gynaecology outpatient appointments and surgery, and to standardise and strengthen pathways for gynaecology conditions in every Trust.Most referrals begin in primary care, and new pathways reflect this. Where patients cannot be managed by their GP, they may be referred to the Gynaecology Enhanced Primary Care Service or to secondary care. Primary and secondary care teams are working jointly to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.The Enhanced Primary Care Service is supporting this by managing pessary fitting, HRT, heavy menstrual bleeding and contraception locally. By 30 September 2025, it had delivered over 2,100 appointments. When fully operational, it is expected to manage around 6,000 patient contacts per year. In secondary care, Trusts are implementing action plans, including Getting it Right (GIRFT) recommendations, to reduce long waits. Between April and December 2025, £3.9m of additional funding supported waiting list initiatives, clinical validation and onestop clinics.  From October 2025 alone, there has been a 52% reduction in patients waiting over three years for an outpatient appointment and a 58% reduction for inpatient/day case waits. Further updates will be provided by March 2026.A regional Gynaecology Provider Collaborative was established in February 2025. The Collaborative (Trusts and SPPG) are focused on standardising pathways and maximising capacity to tackle the longest waiting patients while developing a sustainable, regionally consistent service model in Gynaecology.Additional funding through the Elective Care Framework is addressing service gaps, and work continues to improve pathways for women with gynaecological conditions. Some improvements will require investment; others can be delivered through modernisation. However, the current financial position does limit progress for nonurgent conditions such as endometriosis, unless clinically indicated.I hope this is helpful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any additional resources allocated to the Child Protection Support Service in response to demand, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) is a core EA service, and the decision in respect of resourcing would be a matter for the EA when considering its block grant allocation. EA has advised that it has not allocated additional core resources to the CPSS during the last five years. Since 2022/23, however, the department has secured an additional £660k from the Department of Justice’s Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy Fund to support EA CPSS in progressing both the pilot phase and wider rollout of Operation Encompass across education settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of calls received by the Child Protection Support Service, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the number of calls received in each of the last 5 years by the Child Protection Support Service is as follows:2020/21		4,7652021/22		6,6282022/23		8,1122023/24		9,1012024/25		11,086

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department will take, following the flooding at The Old Mill, Culcavy.
answer_text: Storm Chandra brought high winds and significant levels of rainfall in a relatively short period of time, resulting in a number of incidents of flooding across our road network including at the Old Mill, Culcavy.Given the extent of the disruption across the road network, it will take time for staff to undertake investigatory work in relation to the flooding as we prioritise work to clear water and debris from our road network.My officials will consider the cause of the flooding and identify if any remedial works can be undertaken to alleviate this problem in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department will take, following the flooding caused by Strom Chandra at the entrance to Bridge Road from Lany Road, close to Meadow Bridge Primary School, Hillsborough.
answer_text: Storm Chandra brought high winds and significant levels of rainfall in a relatively short period of time, resulting in a number of incidents of flooding across our road network including the Hillsborough area at the location mentioned.Given the extent of the disruption across the road network, it will take time for staff to undertake investigatory work in relation to the flooding as we prioritise work to clear water and debris from our road network.My officials will consider the cause of the flooding and identify if any remedial works can be undertaken to alleviate this problem in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any urgent action her Department will take, to ensure flooding underneath the railway bridge on the A26 Moira roundabout, does not continue to occur.
answer_text: Storm Chandra was a multi-weather event which brought wind and unprecedented rain, causing widespread flooding issues. Alongside our multi-agency partners, officials worked in difficult conditions to respond and minimise disruption. I am advised that the road gullies at the Moira Roundabout on the A26 drain into open channels situated on adjacent land. At the start of January 2026, the gullies and open drains within the vicinity of this location were cleaned however due to heavy rainfall in the weeks leading up to Storm Chandra, the River Lagan was at a very high level already, which contributed to the flooding.Inspection and maintenance of gullies and drains are critical components of rural infrastructure management and officials will continue to monitor these and arrange regular cleaning to mitigate the effects of such weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the recommendations from the Education Authority and his Department's review into school closure procedures, following Storm Amy, were applied for Storm Chandra.
answer_text: The implementation of the recommendations arising from the Storm Amy review remains a key priority for my Departmental officials and working closely with Education Authority (EA) colleagues, continue to make good progress. The Report highlighted several issues relating to communication with schools, including the need for all future EA messaging to be agreed with the Department and to clearly set out the nature of any closure and the required actions. These recommendations were implemented in practice during Storm Chandra, when the requirement for the EA to seek approval for all communications through the Department’s Press Office ensured consistent, timely and clear messaging regarding the emergency school closures.A small number of recommendations remain in progress, and these are being taken forward as a priority. This includes the development of an Education Civil Contingencies Protocol, which is anticipated to be finalised within the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department will undertake an urgent review to determine how the M1 west bound, between junction six, Saintfield Road, and junction eight at Blaris, became flooded during Storm Chandra; and (ii) to detail any preventative work that can take place to stop it happening again.
answer_text: Storm Chandra brought high winds and significant levels of rainfall in a relatively short period of time, resulting in a number of incidents of flooding across our road network, including on the M1 west bound.Given the extent of the disruption across the road network, it will take time for staff to undertake investigatory work in relation to the flooding as we prioritise work to clear water and debris from our road network.This particular stretch of the M1 is managed on our behalf through a Public Private Partnership (PPP) contract.  My officials will work with the contractor to determine the cause of the flooding and identify if any remedial works can be undertaken to alleviate this problem in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools with school crossing patrols, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) scheme and for the employment of SCP officers. It has advised that schools should contact their local EA transport office to discuss options to cover a vacant SCP post on a temporary basis. It is important that even where a school has identified an individual who could fill an SCP role on a temporary basis, they must first seek EA advice and approval. For an individual to be legally permitted to stop traffic under the SCP scheme, they must be appointed by the EA as an SCP and must be operating at an EA approved location with the statutory uniform and signage. An individual attempting to stop traffic without EA authority is not covered or indemnified by the EA for any legal consequences arising from unauthorised schoolcrossing activities and may be committing an offence.The EA has advised that it currently employs 345 SCPs and has provided information below detailing the number of SCPs employed in each of the last five financial years.Financial Year2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Number of SCPs449431388365329

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any alternative routes available to schools wishing to maintain a school crossing patrol, such as training volunteers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation of the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) scheme and for the employment of SCP officers. It has advised that schools should contact their local EA transport office to discuss options to cover a vacant SCP post on a temporary basis. It is important that even where a school has identified an individual who could fill an SCP role on a temporary basis, they must first seek EA advice and approval. For an individual to be legally permitted to stop traffic under the SCP scheme, they must be appointed by the EA as an SCP and must be operating at an EA approved location with the statutory uniform and signage. An individual attempting to stop traffic without EA authority is not covered or indemnified by the EA for any legal consequences arising from unauthorised schoolcrossing activities and may be committing an offence.The EA has advised that it currently employs 345 SCPs and has provided information below detailing the number of SCPs employed in each of the last five financial years.Financial Year2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Number of SCPs449431388365329

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any monitoring of school preparedness currently taking place in (i) pre-schools; and (ii) primary schools.
answer_text: DE does not assess or monitor 'school preparedness'.  The 3+ Review, conducted by Health Visitors in pre-school settings, is designed to assess children's overall development before they enter formal education. It also helps to identify any developmental needs; facilitating early provision of support where required or onward referral if needed and assists the pre-school in planning for the whole class, or for individual children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how each Health and Social Care Trust coordinates adult day centre placements.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) advise on receiving a referral for a day centre placement, an assessment of the individuals needs is undertaken by the Trust. Following this assessment of need, the Trust will work in partnership with the individual and their family representatives/carers as appropriate, to co-ordinate appropriate support and service provision, aligned to assessed need. Due to demand for day centre placements, HSCTS may on occasion operate a waiting list. HSCTs may consider alternatives to daycentre, based on assessed need, including day opportunities, community and voluntary services, home care provision or direct payments.  HSCTs undertake Reviews of individuals’ needs periodically to ensure service provision continues to meet assessed need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work underway to achieve the recommendation in A Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, to recognise the changing levels of development of children arriving in pre-school and school and address this through targeted funding to pre-school and foundation stage settings to pay for evidence-based interventions through higher staff pupil ratios, professional development and/or para-professionals.
answer_text: I have included in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a range of proposed actions to support children and early years settings.  This includes a commitment to introduce an integrated Early Years Curriculum Framework for children aged 0–6 that provides a learning continuum for children below compulsory school age and through the transition to formal education.  This action flowed from a recommendation contained in the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum and was also raised by key early learning and childcare stakeholders during the strategy development process.  It also includes actions in relation to the expansion of services for children facing disadvantage through my Department’s Sure Start Programme and Pathway Fund,  and the expansion of developmental provision for children aged 2–3 (in their pre-pre school year) as well as building the sustainability and capacity of the ELC sector and enhance the early years infrastructure through a skilled and valued workforce.I also secured funding of £4.5m in 2025/26 from the ELC budget for the Pre-school Inclusion Fund to support DE funded pre-school education providers to meet the needs of children in their settings who have or may have special educational needs requiring early additional support.  The Fund provides flexibility to those settings to secure additional staffing hours or specific training and/ or source external supports.The draft Strategy was published for public consultation on 17th December 2025 and I welcome all comments on it. Decisions on the implementation and timing of the draft actions within the draft Strategy will largely depend on the outcome of public consultation and the availability of funding from the Northern Ireland Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is transport support available to adults attending day centres outside of their Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) report day centre attendance and transport provision may be provided within Trust areas following assessment and in accordance with assessed need. HSCTs provided the following response in respect of transport to day centres outside their Trust geographical boundary.Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT):The NHSCT advise that they do not typically provide transport to day centres outside of their Trust area. The Trust further advised the exception to this is if the Trust resident had been assessed as having complex needs that could only be met by a service outside of the Trust.Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT):The SHSCT advise that they will provide transport to Day Centres outside of the Trust boundary if required by the assessed needs of the individual.Western Health and Social Care Trust (WHSCT):The WHSCT advise that they do not provide transport outside of their Trust boundary. Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT):The SEHSCT advise the Trust only provides transport to day centres within their Trust area only.Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT):The BHSCT advise that they do not place individuals outside of the BHSCT Trust boundary, with transport then provided within the Trust geographical area

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any work that is ongoing to ensure schools are getting value for money through maintenance contracts and are not having to pay excessive amounts.
To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31465/22-27, whether he has plans to develop legislation to ensure all children must be registered with the Education Authority.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the legal responsibility for ensuring that a child receives an education rests with their parents, who may choose to provide that education at home. Currently, there is no legislation in Northern Ireland requiring home educating parents to register their child with the Education Authority or the Department of Education and this reflects the current position in other UK jurisdictions.As part of the ‘Attendance Matters Strategy’, my Department is developing a policy around children missing education which will be refined following the Attendance Matters consultation. This work will consider the legislative position and developments in other jurisdictions and may involve exploring the case for introducing a mandatory register of children who are not registered in the formal grant aided school system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils being electively home educated, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the information below in respect of the number of children it was informed of who were being electively home educated in each of the last five years.  Whilst the EA is aware of children who have attended and subsequently been deregistered from a school, there is no duty on parents to inform the Department of Education or the EA if they have chosen to educate their children at home. Thus, there is no complete record of children being electively home educated in Northern Ireland. YearNumber of Pupils recorded as EHE2025-2026 (as at Jan 2026)11502024/202512202023/202410602022/20239072021/2022808

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education and Training Inspectorate will have a role in checking mobile phone policies are being implemented within schools.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) already considers a school’s policy in relation to restricting the use of mobile phones under the broader heading of ‘Health, Wellbeing and Keeping Safe’. Inspectors scrutinise associated policy documentation, coupled with direct observation of learning and teaching in the classroom to evaluate the effectiveness of the policies in practice, rather than compliance alone. Any areas requiring action will be referenced explicitly in the inspection report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when he will make a decision on Downshire Primary School's development proposal to integrated status.
answer_text: Development Proposal (DP) 735 which proposes that Downshire Primary School will transform to Controlled Integrated Status, with effect from 1 September 2025, or as soon as possible thereafter, was published by the Education Authority on 11 September 2024.A report on the proposal has recently been submitted to me for consideration by my officials.The assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires careful consideration of all evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all children and to ensure they have access to high quality education that is viable and sustainable in the long term.I am aware of the need for clarity and certainty for the school and its community; therefore, I will endeavour to provide that clarity and certainty as soon as practically possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering a recruitment drive to improve waiting times for ADHD assessments.
answer_text: The Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability in an extremely challenging financial environment.The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like. Consideration of future staffing requirements has formed an integral part of the needs assessment.The final ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025.  The report is currently under consideration, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to improve waiting times for ADHD assessments.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting times across Trusts. In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, with the purpose of informing options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, aimed to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs.  The final needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025 (in line with the revised agreed timeframe for completion) and subsequently published on 12 February 2026, noting that any decisions on future commissioning of ADHD services would be in the context of overall affordability.Officials have now commenced an options appraisal process to identify what options could potentially be implemented in the short to medium term to help address the need for ADHD services.The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. Any decision to commission services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a criteria for GPs to refer patients to a tissue viability nurses.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely commission set criteria for referral but would expect clinicians to consider recognised best practice guidance in determining the most appropriate pathways for their patient.  Primary care referrals to secondary care can be informed or supported by criteria or advice from secondary care colleagues but a patient’s GP can always make a referral to a hospital Trust if they consider this to be the most appropriate route or in the best clinical interests of the patient.  Within South Eastern Trust, the Tissue Viability (TV) Team has five tissue viability nurses and a specialist tissue viability lead nurse. They provide specialist wound management and preventative advice for patients with complex aetiologies/ comorbidities and psychosocial issues. The TV team also provides practical support and education in wound management in the clinical setting.All patients with wounds or who are at risk of tissue damage and require specialist advice from the tissue viability team, will be assessed and treated in a manner, which considers all aspects of the individual’s health care needs to improve patient experience and outcomes.Referrals to the service can be made via Primary Care Connect via GPs, community nurses, pharmacists and other authorised clinicians. The service operates Monday to Friday: 9.00am-5.00pm (excluding bank holidays), Tel: 02895 988000.I hope this overview of the HSC arrangements in your constituency addresses any concerns you may have.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 36768/22-27, to detail (i) how GPs can request a case review by an Effective Use of Resources (EUR) panel; and (ii) whether the EUR panel is independent of the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: An appeals process can be developed whereby a consultant or GP can make an appeal on behalf of the patient. The appeal panel will be led by the SPPG and will have clinical input from Trusts and the PHA. An appeal can be made if the patient does not meet the criteria in the EUR policy commissioning position but the consultant/GP believes that there are exceptional clinical circumstances that mean the patient should undergo the required procedure/intervention.  The panel will consider the appeals and ensure that the decision making processes are fair, equitable and ethical.  The consultant/GP must:Provide details as to what extent, if at all, the patient meets the criteria;Describe why the consultant/GP considers the patient to be clinically exceptional.The definition of exceptionality on which appeals will be considered is:The patient is significantly different to the general population of patients with the condition in question.  ANDIs likely to gain significantly more benefit from the intervention than might normally be expected for patients with that condition.A small panel will be convened, comprising SPPG, Public Health Agency (PHA) and HSC Trust representation (none of whom to have any previous involvement in the individual case) to assess the exceptional clinical circumstances. Every appeal shall be considered on its own merits, although particular care will be taken to identify and consider any exceptional circumstances that might demand or permit a departure from established policy and practice.Appeals should only be submitted to SPPG via a GP or Consultant and emailed via eur@hscnni.net

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether her Department has an up-to-date register of salt and grit bin locations; and (ii) how often her Department updates this register.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department keeps an up-to-date register of salt and grit bin locations which is updated when salt bins are added to the network through application and successful assessment or when removed by request or through non usage.  The most recent totals are shown in the table below:Salt Bin LocationCountEastern DivisionBelfast North                                     373Belfast South                                     204Lisburn and Castlereagh416Eastern Total993Northern DivisionAntrim and Newtownabbey389Causeway Coast and Glens East167Causeway Coast and Glens West522Mid and East Antrim400Northern Total1478Southern DivisionArds and North Down                               425Armagh Banbridge and Craigavon East477Armagh Banbridge and Craigavon West326Newry Mourne and Down West384Newry Mourne and Down East405Southern Total2017Western DivisionFermanagh and Omagh East                          219Fermanagh and Omagh West222Londonderry and Strabane424Mid Ulster North250Mid Ulster South260Western Total1375Departmental Total5863

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given the requirement in paragraph six of TNC2011/2 on tackling violence/abuse against teachers in schools which states the Employing Authority should also collate all information received on incidents of violence or abusive behaviour against teachers and submit an annual report to the Teachers‘ Negotiating Committee (TNC), for information, to detail (i) the number of reports provided to the TNC, broken down by employing authority, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the number of incidents reported in each yearly report, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Assaults on school staff are not reported to my Department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).Paragraph 6 of TNC 2011/2 details that all incidents of abusive behaviour should be reported to the principal of the school or other designated teacher who shall then record and report the incident to their Employing Authority.The Employing Authority is required then to assess the incident and determine if it is reportable under the provisions of the Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations Northern Ireland, 1997.As you state in your question, the Employing Authority should provide an annual report to the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) for their information.While we are aware that collation of these incidents is being maintained by the employing authority, we are only aware of one report being provided to members of TNC by the Education Authority (EA) on behalf of the EA and CCMS in the past five years.The EA has been maintaining the number of incidents involving assaults against staff and provided the following data in response to previous Assembly Questions, which details reported assaults against staff within both Controlled Schools and Catholic Maintained Schools in each of the past 5 years:Year 20202021202220232024TOTALNumber of reported assaults against staff79012731578208035799300These figures have also been broken down into school type:School Type20202021202220232024Grand TotalNursery02013823Other2814442189Post Primary8579635Primary3935113119288594Special74112231444189532568559Grand Total79012731578208035799300

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether GPs can refer to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust specialist regional service for severe varicose veins.
answer_text: The vascular varicose vein outpatient service is delivered by Belfast Health and Social Care Trust, providing a specialist regional service within Northern Ireland.  There are currently two ways in which a patient can access vascular services within Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; either from a referral by an Emergency Department or by referral by a GP or another clinician.Due to the current pressures on the Vascular Service across Northern Ireland, a decision was taken in 2023 that varicose vein assessment and treatment could only be provided to patients with severe varicose veins.  Patients are now assessed and reviewed under these criteria.  New referrals which do not meet these criteria will not be offered an outpatient appointment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his next actions in terms of children missing in education.
answer_text: Work on the development of policy in this area continues with Children Missing Education included as one six priorities in the draft ‘Attendance Matters’ strategy, which is currently out for public consultation.Once the consultation responses have been collated and considered then officials will seek to consult and engage with stakeholders more widely on the draft Children Missing Education policy framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether (i) he; or (ii) his Department, has contacted Ofcom with regards to the implementation of the Online Safety Act 2023.
answer_text: I wrote to the Director of Ofcom in Northern Ireland following the publication of the Ofcom ‘Protection of Children Codes and Guidance’ (May 2025) highlighting the importance of its work in creating a safer life online through the implementation of the Online Safety Act 2023, particularly in relation to children and young people. My officials continue to engage with Ofcom representatives as part of the work of the Online Safety Strategy Cross Departmental Implementation Group chaired by the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy 2024-2039, specifically in preventing families being made homeless from the private rental sector.

To ask the Minister of Education whether he has written to any UK Government (i) Ministers; or (ii) Departments, regarding concerns about online safety of children and young people, in the 2022- 27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: While I have not written to other UK Government Ministers or Departments regarding this, during my formal engagements with other Ministers we have discussed digital literacy, AI and the importance of effective regulation for its safe use.My officials continue to work closely with colleagues and key stakeholders, across Northern Ireland departments, to ensure delivery of the objectives set out in the Northern Ireland Executive’s cross-departmental Online Safety Strategy Action Plan 2025-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has written to any UK Government Ministers or Departments in relation to online safety of children and young people, in the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Whilst I have not directly written to any UK Government Ministers or Departments in relation to online safety, my department maintains effective links with the wider UK online safety landscape through its sponsorship of the Executive’s Online Safety Strategy and the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI). This includes ongoing engagement with Ofcom in its role as the independent regulator for Online Safety.  As the central body with responsibility for online safety in Northern Ireland, SBNI also participates in the UK Council for Internet Safety (UKCIS) Early Warnings Group, which supports timely information sharing and collaboration on the development of resources to strengthen online safety practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has contacted Ofcom in relation to the implementation of the Online Safety Act 2023.
answer_text: The Department of Health sponsors the Executives’ Online Safety Strategy and, as such, I receive regular updates from Ofcom regarding their implementation of the Online Safety Act. As part of this ongoing engagement, I met with Ofcom officials in October 2024 to discuss their work to deliver the requirements of the Act. In addition, at a recent meeting of the Online Safety Strategy Cross-Departmental Implementation Group (CDIG), Bimpe Archer from Ofcom provided members with an update on their ongoing work in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work that has been progressed to provide schools with greater flexibility, in relation to procurement spend, through existing frameworks.
answer_text: The EA is considering the options available for providing schools with flexibility on the use of frameworks established for use by schools. This includes a potential pilot project that will provide schools with greater autonomy in purchasing low-value consumables from their delegated budget and the choice to use EA frameworks or alternative suppliers. The objective will be to assess the potential benefits and impact of removing the mandate to use framework arrangements.  In addition, the pilot will assess the level of risk attached to schools undertaking procurements below the £10,000 threshold (i.e. the absence of the EA’s CoPE support and expertise).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any engagement his Department has had with HMRC to assess the impact of the removal of the 10 per cent wear-and-tear tax deduction for childminders, as part of the move to Making Tax Digital for Income Tax, on (i) childminders in Northern Ireland; (ii) the sustainability of the childminding sector; and (iii) the (a) affordability; and (b) availability, of childcare for local families.
answer_text: My officials have raised this issue with HMRC, which is responsible for the administration of income tax. My officials pressed on what engagement HMRC has had, or is planning to have, with the local sector and what assessments have been carried out to gauge the impact of the proposed changes. HMRC has indicated that it has carried out an assessment of the impact for childminders, and it has found no devolution-specific issues.  HMRC has also indicated that they expect the number of childminders impacted to be low and those that are impacted can continue to claim tax relief for wear and tear by deducting the actual cost of buying, repairing or replacing items. Childminders can also deduct the cost of business expenses such as utilities, cleaning and equipment. HMRC has indicated that this will allow childminders to receive tax relief for all of the costs that they incur in relation to their childminding business.HMRC has also stated that it is currently engaging with sector bodies, including with the NI Childminding Association (NICMA). My officials have pressed HMRC on the importance of recognising and acting on any concerns from the sector ahead of the proposed changes coming into effect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the membership of the TransformED Local Academics Group.
answer_text: Membership of the TransformED Academics’ Panel is comprised of representatives from the following organisations listed below:Jannette Elwood Queen's University Belfast Tony GallagherQueen's University Belfast Daniel Muijs Queen's University Belfast Gavin DuffyQueen's University Belfast Martin Hagan St Mary's University College Geraldine Magennis-Clarke St Mary's University College William KitchenStranmillis University CollegePatricia EatonStranmillis University CollegeUna O'Connor Bones Ulster University Kelsey Shanks Ulster University Paul Connolly Ulster University It should be noted that Geraldine Magennis-Clarke is currently on a one-year secondment to the Department of Education to support work on literacy development and will resume her position on the panel when her secondment is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement his Department has had with HMRC to assess the impact of the removal of the 10 per cent wear-and-tear tax deduction for childminders, as part of the move to Making Tax Digital for Income Tax, on (i) childminders in Northern Ireland; (ii) the sustainability of the childminding sector; and (iii) the (a) affordability; and (b) availability, of childcare for local families.
answer_text: HMRC is responsible for tax policy and administration across the UK, including Northern Ireland. There has been no engagement by HMRC with my Department on the plans to replace the flat-rate 10% deduction for wear-and-tear with a system based on actual costs. It is my understanding that HMRC will publish guidance on the transition in early 2026.My officials will monitor any impact on childminder provision at a strategic level. The draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, currently out for public consultation, includes actions to support the affordability and accessibility of childcare for families and the sustainability for providers, including childminders.Following the consultation process, implementation of agreed actions will be subject to Executive agreement and the availability of the budget required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the cost of expanding the mobile phone pouch trial to all schools, and (ii) whether other options are being explored to implement the same policy, in a more financially sustainable way.
answer_text: The Department is currently running a pilot in nine post-primary schools across Northern Ireland to test the effectiveness of using lockable pouches as an approach to reducing mobile phone usage during the school day.  The pilot is running from September 2025 until March 2026, with over 8000 pupils participating in the pilot.An external evaluation of the pilot is being conducted by King’s College London, and the findings will help to inform future policy decisions, including any consideration of a wider roll-out.  At this stage, no decision has been taken to expand beyond the current pilot and therefore a full cost model has not been developed. The Department is mindful of the need for any potential future approach to be provide value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 37727/22-27, to detail whether he will use the power of direction to ensure all children have a place for September 2026.
answer_text: The Department of Education and Education Authority are currently considering all options to ensure placements for all children with statements of Special Education Needs for 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review her Department’s policy on the provision of grit boxes in residential areas, in light of reports from residents of these boxes being removed or not provided where they are required.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.Equally, residents and proprietors can request the removal of a salt bin, and in some instances, a bin may be removed from a location due to lack of use. In which case, the bin is re-located.The current criteria for the provision of salt bins is considered appropriate, and therefore there are no plans to review it at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the new You Matter pilot project for women and young men leaving Hydebank Wood College.
answer_text: ANSWER  While most people leaving prison will have a place to live, for some, leaving prison will be an uncertain time and can mean they have nowhere to stay. Homelessness can be a major barrier to reintegration, and this initiative aims to break that cycle by providing practical support and a sense of dignity at a critical point in their lives.   The “You Matter Bag” project was created in response to feedback from women and young men in Hydebank Wood who shared the emotional and practical challenges of leaving custody, particularly when facing homelessness, social disconnection or lack of immediate support. By listening to those with lived experience, the project ensures that solutions are not only effective but also respectful and person-centred.  The Northern Ireland Prison Service teamed up with Start 360 and Ardoyne Youth Club to support people leaving prison by providing each person, who was known to be homeless, with a ‘You Matter’ bag.    The bags were designed to provide practical tools for that first day such as toiletries including toothpaste, shower gel and antiperspirant, as well as water and cuppa soup, alongside information about where to go for food, shelter and help.  The bags were financed through a donation to Start360 from People’s Kitchen and were presented to Hydebank Wood College by Ardoyne Youth Club during Homeless Week which ran from 1 to 7 December 2025.  The project is a great example of partnership working, bringing together Start360 and Hydebank as well as the incredible young people from Ardoyne Youth Club, whose creativity and commitment has been instrumental in shaping this work.  This highlights the power of community collaboration in tackling some of the most complex social challenges.  As a pilot, the project does not claim to solve systemic issues.  It hopes to restore dignity, comfort and provide clarity on what comes next.  The ambition is to build on its success and secure ongoing funding, so that every person leaving prison has the respect, care, and opportunities they deserve.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on school placements for those with special educational needs and disabilities; and (ii) the number of additional places which still need to be created for September 2026.
answer_text: Ensuring every child with a statement of special educational needs has an appropriate school placement remains a primary focus for my department and the Education Authority.Since 2020, circa 6,350 additional SEN places have been created in total. 273 schools, approximately 26% of all schools in Northern Ireland, currently offer specialist provision. There are now over 700 specialist provision classes operating across a range of key stages. The EA's early planning for September 2026 identified a need for approximately 950 additional places.  EA has been actively working with schools and has already initiated projects estimated to accommodate approximately 350 of these placements across specialist provisions and special schools, reducing the cost and length of home to school transport, whilst enhancing positive integration across our school communities. Throughout November and December, the EA held engagement sessions both in person and online inviting over 300 schools in high-pressure areas to explore the development of specialist provisions within their school community, approximately 90 schools attended. EA is working with those schools that expressed an interest at the events to identify solutions.My department and the EA remain committed to the vision that every child with SEN has access to a placement, in their local community and with their peers.This will require a fundamental shift towards a system in which all schools offer specialist provision classes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any protocols in place to address instances where a childcare provider that is part of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is deemed to have increased fees at an inappropriate or excessive rate.
answer_text: There are currently no protocols in place to address instances where a childcare provider participating in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is deemed to have increased fees at an inappropriate or excessive rate. The NICSS is a voluntary scheme to reduce childcare bills for working parents.  My Department does not regulate childcare fees or have authority to impose price controls on providers, most of whom operate as private businesses.Determining whether a fee increase is inappropriate or excessive would require a detailed understanding of each provider’s business model and financial position, including whether the increase was necessary to maintain sustainability. This level of assessment is outside the scope of NICSS and outside the remit of my Department.The Department does, however, monitor overall fee trends through NICSS data to inform policy development under the draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy. Proposals within the draft Strategy, currently out for public consultation, aim to improve transparency for families by requiring providers to publish their fees, and to support providers to operate effectively and manage cost pressures through a Childcare Business Advisory Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department's road gritting policy considers (i) preventing school closures; and (ii) ensuring children can travel to school safely.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. Route treatment is primarily based on traffic volume not road classification.Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. This Secondary Salting Network currently gives priority to 80 rural schools across the North.In addition to this, all schools which have requested a salt bin or grit pile has been provided with a box on either the public road frontage or a grit pile on the shortest route connecting the school to the salting network.It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed.The decision to close a school during adverse weather is taken on an individual basis by the school’s Principal / Governing body, taking many factors into account, aside from whether it is located on the salted network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any coordination between her Department, the Department of Education and the Education Authority, in regards to adapting the gritting policy to ensure as many schools as possible can remain open during periods of cold weather. over the last three years.
answer_text: The current winter service policy and procedures operated by my Department utilises the widely accepted practice of targeting limited resources for this service on the higher trafficked main through routes and can be viewed on the Department’s website below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted. Route treatment is primarily based on traffic volume not road classification.Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. This Secondary Salting Network currently gives priority to 80 rural schools across the North.In addition to this, all schools which have requested a salt bin or grit pile has been provided with a box on either the public road frontage or a grit pile on the shortest route connecting the school to the salting network.It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed.The decision to close a school is taken on an individual basis by the school’s Principal / Governing body, taking many factors into account, in addition to whether it is located on the salted network and my Department does not contribute to this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount her Department (i) budgeted for; and (ii) spent on, road gritting, over the last three years.
answer_text: The amount my Department budgeted for and spent on the provision of a Winter service for the last three years is detailed in the table below. Financial YearExpenditure/Budget£'m2022-238.72023-247.72024-259.6

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the proposal within the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy to require all ELC providers, funded by Government, to pay their staff at least the Real Living Wage will also include those offering the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The proposal in the draft ELC Strategy does not extend to those offering the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS).  Within the current parameters of the NICSS, funding is not provided to support the sustainability of participating childcare providers, many of which are private businesses, and therefore we are unable to impose any requirement to pay their staff the Real Living Wage (RLW). Following finalisation of the consultation process, I will work with my Executive colleagues, including the Economy Minister, in finalising actions to promote the RLW with all early learning and childcare employing organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36441/22-27, to detail any pilot schemes currently being funded by (i) the Education Authority; and (ii) other non- departmental public bodies funded by his Department.
answer_text: Under the Education Authority’s (EA) Home to School Transport Flexible Fleet pilot, the EA invested in a single seven-seater vehicle during 2023/24 to trial the use of such vehicles in transport operations. The initial pilot produced positive outcomes, leading to an expansion in 2024/25 with the purchase of ten additional vehicles. The Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) is currently developing a digital adaptive assessment tool for literacy and numeracy. Computer Adaptive Assessments (CAA) is a voluntary pilot available to all schools and provides free, online adaptive assessments that generate formative and diagnostic data, enabling schools to target literacy and numeracy support effectively at both pupil and class level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 35254/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 35254/22-27 on 19 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the Charity Commission for Northern Ireland's response to reports of safeguarding issues within the Presbyterian Church in Ireland; and (ii) his next steps regarding this.
answer_text: The Charity Commission for Northern Ireland (CCNI) has operational independence as the statutory regulator of the charity sector in Northern Ireland. While my Department sponsors CCNI, it has no direct role in the delivery of the CCNI’s statutory functions.  I am aware that the Charity Commissioners have decided to open a statutory inquiry into the Presbyterian Church in Ireland; they have authority to examine whether there has been misconduct or mismanagement in the administration of the charity.I am satisfied that the Charity Commission is taking necessary action under its authority as the statutory regulator.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether he has considered any discretionary payments for carers, in addition to Carer's Allowance.
answer_text: I am currently considering what additional support can be provided to carers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement the Education Authority (i) has had; or (ii) plans to have, with the Presbyterian Church of Ireland regarding ongoing safeguarding investigations.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that it has not been in contact with the Presbyterian Church of Ireland regarding ongoing safeguarding investigations. Such matters are for the PSNI to advise on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the principles governing the use and retrospective application of rationing healthcare rules, including how such cases are assessed and counted.
answer_text: The Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy sets out referral criteria for a range of procedures. The policy was reviewed and updated to reflect emerging clinical evidence from NICE and similar bodies, ensuring decisions are based on best practice and fairness.Implemented in 2023, the updated EUR policy supports transparency and consistency in decision making. GPs must confirm patients meet the defined clinical criteria before referral to secondary care, while Trusts are responsible for compliance with commissioning positions.When an assessment is made that the patient does not meet EUR criteria, the referral will either be returned to the GP (if at point of referral), or the patient will be discharged (if at the point of outpatient assessment). There are however instances where it will be clinically appropriate to proceed on a case by case basis.  It would be anticipated that an EUR panel would consider such cases, rather than individual Consultant oversight/ decision making.Work is underway to explore how future monitoring of restricted procedures can be effectively integrated into the new digital infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration been given to the potential increase in GP treatment room time resulting from changes to rationing rules around the treatment of vascular varicose veins.
answer_text: The treatment of vascular varicose vein within Primary Care remains unchanged following the Department of Health updating the Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy.  In line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guidance, if varicose veins are not bleeding, patients within Primary Care should be given verbal and written information on varicose veins, as well as self-care advice, to relieve symptoms and reduce the risk of complications. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust deliver a specialist regional service within Northern Ireland for those patients with severe varicose veins requiring an outpatient appointment for varicose vein assessment and treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department undertook any consultation or impact assessment before deciding to apply elements of the 2023 guidance on rationing healthcare to earlier referrals.
answer_text: The Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy was reviewed and updated in 2021 and sets out referral criteria for a range of procedures. The policy was reviewed and updated to reflect emerging clinical evidence from National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) and similar bodies, ensuring decisions are based on best practice and fairness.The SPPG (former HSCB) worked with the Equality Unit to undertake an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) to understand any cumulative impacts of the combined changes.  These assessments confirmed that the proposed changes will have only a minor impact on service users. The SPPG (former HSCB) assessed the impact of the proposed changes on rural service users and no significant implications were identified.The updated criteria were informed by a range of stakeholders including Consultants, Nurses, Trust Managers, GPs, Royal College of Surgeons representatives, Personal and Public Involvement leads, Public Health and HSCB staff. In addition to this there was a pubic consultation launched in October 2021 and lasted for a period of 13 weeks. The consultation process offered an opportunity for all stakeholders to consider and comment on the proposed service model for the Commissioning Position for Effective Use of Resources for the population of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36442/22-27, to detail (i) any selection criteria used for each of the subject working groups; (ii) the number of people who (a) applied; and (b) were accepted, broken down by each working group, and the type of school they were from; and (iii) the finalised guidance which has been issued to members of the working groups.
answer_text: An open Expressions of Interest process was launched on 16 September for teachers to join Subject Working Groups (SWGs) as part of the curriculum reform project.  Selection to working groups was based on information provided within application forms on the following four areas:Subject expertise.Curriculum design and pedagogical understanding.Communication skills – clarity and effectiveness in written responses.Vision.There was significant interest in these roles with a total of 366 applications received.  To date, 75 teachers and lecturers were selected to work within the SWGs.  Membership of these groups is subject to change should any gaps in expertise or experience be identified, or should individuals not be able to take up post.  Should gaps arise further invitations to join the SWGs will issue to previous applicants.  The expression of interest process ensured representatives across various school types and further and higher education, as follows:TypeNumber PrimaryMaintained (11)Controlled (8)Integrated (1)Other (1)21Post-PrimaryVoluntary (19)Catholic Maintained Secondary (6)Controlled Secondary (6)Grant Maintained Integrated Secondary (3)Controlled Grammar (2)Controlled Integrated Secondary (2)38Special2Further and Higher Education14The operating guidance for subject working groups is not currently publicly available, as it is a living, iterative document and forms part of ongoing policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any action her Department is taking to reduce the number of young people, not in education, employment or training.
answer_text: The increasing number of young people here who are not in education, employment or training is of concern. This is a complex issue involving a diverse range of young people aged between 16 and 24 with complex factors that necessitates a multi-agency approach. My Department is working very closely with the key government departments, including the Department of Education on the implementation of the 14-19 Education and Training Framework. The Framework includes a wide range of actions to ensure that every young person is on the right pathway.As Minister I have had a focus on Inclusion and supporting those individuals with lower qualifications. Many of my department’s programmes are aimed at supporting our young people to thrive in training or employment. These include entry level programmes such as Skills for Life and Work and Step Up, with FE colleges offering flexible delivery to accommodate diverse learner needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had, regarding proposed curriculum changes from the Department of Education, given the impact changes could have on skills development.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with the Department of Education on the proposed curriculum changes, recognising the impact changes could have on skills development. As part of this engagement, officials from my Department met with Lucy Crehan during the course of the Curriculum Review. Ongoing engagement is supported through the Curriculum and Qualifications Thematic Group, which operates within the joint 14–19 structures. This provides an important forum for collaboration between both Departments on curriculum matters.In addition, at the recent Cross Departmental Ministerial Meeting with Minister Givan, we discussed the Curriculum Review, including engagement with the newly established Curriculum Taskforce, particularly in relation to issues affecting Further Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will update the school uniform guidelines to include the right for girls to have the choice to wear trousers as part of the school uniform.
answer_text: There is no barrier in either the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill or the associated guidelines to girls wearing trousers as part of their school uniform.  I have consistently stated my view that there is a balance to be struck between ensuring school uniform policy requirements are affordable, comfortable, practical for pupils and their parents and respecting the autonomy of schools to determine their individual uniform, in line with the guidelines.  The Bill and the guidelines require schools to consult with their pupils and parents about the uniform policy requirements, which provides the opportunity for these requirements to be agreed.  I am content the School Uniforms Guidelines strike the appropriate balance as they are currently drafted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has met with the Minister for Health to discuss the number of training places for speech and language therapists needed to meet the demand for services within education.
answer_text: I have not met the Minister of Health in this specific topic. However, we continue to engage on Special Education Needs matters as do Officials from both departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned works to improve the safety of the Saintfield Road, at the junction with Old Ballynahinch Road, following a number of recent serious collisions at this location.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.I can advise that my officials are developing a preliminary design to change the layout of this junction.The development of this scheme will involve detailed design, accommodation works, land acquisition, funding and a separate procurement process. We are also considering additional traffic management measures that could be introduced to improve road safety in the short term.Concerns relating to driver behaviour should be reported to the PSNI using their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the October 2024 report Enabling Safe Quality Midwifery Services and Care In Northern Ireland report, to detail any progress on a consolidated regional action plan for maternity services.
answer_text: To progress this work, a Maternity and Neonatal Partnership is required.  There is a funding requirement associated with this. As you are aware, my Department is currently facing a significant funding deficit. While it has not been possible to allocate all of the additional funding originally requested to establish this Partnership, I have asked my officials to develop a scaled-back, more affordable option.I have been advised that this new Partnership will be established in the coming weeks.  Once established, work will commence at pace on the development of a consolidated regional action plan for Maternity services.   This action plan will outline short, medium and long term actions.  An Oversight Group/Board will also be established as part of the Governance arrangements.  The Oversight Group will be responsible for approving the action plan and its agreed implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the establishment of the Maternity and Neonatal Partnership, including (i) the number of times the Partnership has met, since October 2024; and (ii) the membership of the Partnership.
answer_text: There is a funding requirement associated with establishing a Maternity and Neonatal Partnership. As you are aware, my Department is currently facing a significant funding deficit. While it has not been possible to allocate all of the additional funding originally requested to establish this Partnership, I have asked my officials to develop a scaled-back, more affordable option.(i) I have been advised that this new partnership will be established in the coming weeks.(ii) Membership of the Partnership will include representation from HSC Trust, DoH Professional and Policy leads, the Public Health Agency, Clinical, Nursing, Midwifery and AHP leads and Service Users representation. Membership will be kept under review and refreshed as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether any other companies were considered when awarding a Direct Award Contract to InnerDrive, for the Online Teacher Continued Professional Development Academy for post-primary schools.
answer_text: In May 2025, the Department undertook a review of potential providers in relation to the proposed online Continued Professional Development (CPD) programme on the Science of Learning for post-primary schools. This review concluded that InnerDrive was the only supplier that was able to deliver the comprehensive package required within the timescales set out in the TransformED Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34283/22-27, to detail (i) the number of defects identified; (ii) the date when they are due to be repaired; and (iii) when more significant works will be carried out to improve the condition of the pavements.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.Whilst there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including those in Moira village, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes completed in 2024/25 and planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council.One NI Water Utility lid defect was identified during the inspection on 4 November 2025which was referred to NI Water for action. I am advised that this utility lid has now been replaced.Subsequent to this inspection three further defects were noted on Main Street, Moira between 11 November 2025 and 5 December 2025.  One has been completed and the other two are programmed with our internal contractor.A further defect was noted close to the junction of Station Road and Main Street, Moira on 5 December 2025 and this has been repaired.A routine highway inspection was completed in Main Street, Moira over 16 and 17 December 2025. One defect was identified and this has been issued to the contractor for repair.In addition, works are planned to repair an area of flagged footway close to the doctor’s surgery. This work is expected to be completed in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the resurfacing works carried out in Moira, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.Whilst there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including those in Moira village, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes completed in 2024/25 and planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council.One NI Water Utility lid defect was identified during the inspection on 4 November 2025which was referred to NI Water for action. I am advised that this utility lid has now been replaced.Subsequent to this inspection three further defects were noted on Main Street, Moira between 11 November 2025 and 5 December 2025.  One has been completed and the other two are programmed with our internal contractor.A further defect was noted close to the junction of Station Road and Main Street, Moira on 5 December 2025 and this has been repaired.A routine highway inspection was completed in Main Street, Moira over 16 and 17 December 2025. One defect was identified and this has been issued to the contractor for repair.In addition, works are planned to repair an area of flagged footway close to the doctor’s surgery. This work is expected to be completed in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is aware of any issue with the online portal for reporting road defects, including potholes, where the report is removed without the pothole being repaired.
answer_text: I am not aware of any issues with regard to the online portal for reporting potholes, however I would advise that when a pothole is reported, officials will undertake a review of the site and determine if the defect is within the Department’s policy to deem it an actionable defect.  If the defect meets the required threshold, it will be marked and reported for repair.  If the defect does not meet the current threshold of my Department’s Limited Service policy, the report is closed on the system and marked as a “non-actionable” defect.  On 2nd December, I was pleased to launch the new Road Maintenance Strategy which is now open for public consultation and in support of the new approach I have also commissioned a full digital road survey of all roads in the North.We are committed to raising the standard of maintenance across the network, ensuring that interventions are timely, durable, and delivered to consistently high specifications. This will reduce long-term costs, improve user experience, and enhance safety.Whilst the strategy and survey will provide greater detail to inform strategic decision making, this will also continue to be supported by the reporting of potholes and surface defects via the online portal.  Maintenance staff will continue to inspect and make-safe defects in accordance with our policy to ensure the safety of the travelling public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail all pilot programmes currently ongoing, funded by his Department.
answer_text: There are four pilots currently ongoing, funded by my department namely:the Mobile Phone Inhibiting Devices pilot;the Belfast Wide Early Years (BWEY) pilot; the Pre-School Inclusion Fund (PSIF) pilot; andthe South Belfast Sure Start (SBSS) Roma pilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 35784/22-27, to detail all (i) independent reviews of Department of Education programmes; (ii) Independent Assessors; and (iii) Ministerial Advisory Panels, without associated budget.
