answer_text: The details on all (i) independent reviews of Department of Education programmes; (ii) Independent Assessors; and (iii) Ministerial Advisory Panels, without associated budget are set out below:Consultation event on the barriers and solutions in respect of childcare; Early Learning and Childcare Strategy consultation;Education Authority Landscape Review;Evaluation of DE-funded Nurture Provision;Independent Review of Education Panel in Northern Ireland;Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum;Review of DE Targeted Early Years Interventions;Review of Sure Start Health Professionals;Review of Childcare Services in Northern Ireland;Review of Standardisation of the Pre-school Education Programme;Review of Childcare Costs and Assumptions; andNorthern Ireland Childcare Survey.Appointment of independent assessors in the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations & Assessment.Appointment of International Ministerial Advisory panel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether Religious Education will be part of the ongoing review of the curriculum.
answer_text: I will set out the process for reviewing the RE syllabus over coming weeks

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of curriculum taskforce working groups which have been set up; including (ii) their membership; and (iii) the chairs, of each group.
answer_text: The Curriculum Taskforce has established thirteen subject working groups to support curriculum reform.Membership of these groups is still being finalised and remains fluid as work progresses, and any gaps are identified.  The groups consist largely of practising teachers who participate voluntarily, supported by lead drafters and members of the Curriculum Taskforce Advisory Committee.In line with the requirements of the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR), it would not be appropriate to provide lists of memberships, as this would involve the disclosure of personal data.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work being done to establish citizens' assemblies, in line with the commitment made in the New Decade, New Approach deal.
answer_text: We recognise and fully support the role that civic society plays in supporting policy making, through co-design and co-production approaches.  Consideration continues to be given to a range of matters in relation to the re-establishment of the Civic Forum, and the new arrangements will be brought to the Assembly for its agreement in due course.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the registration of Parliament Buildings' defibrillators to the network to ensure maximum effect when required.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he is taking, following the the recommendation in the Independent Review of Education, that all learners, starting from age 14, should explore a wider range of educational pathways, including pre-vocational and technical education and learning about the world of work, and to achieve this, schools need to collaborate more effectively, while also working closely with the further education sector, to provide increased choice and flexibility.
answer_text: Through the Entitlement Framework, schools are required to offer access to a minimum of 21 courses at Key Stage 4 and Post-16, of which at least one third must be general and one third applied.  This aims to ensure that schools provide access for pupils to a broadly based and balanced range of subjects and qualifications which meet the interests and progression needs of their pupils, whether that is in to training, employment, further or higher education opportunities. These courses may be delivered in collaboration with Further Education colleges, training providers or other schools.  My Department is continuing to work in collaboration with the Department for the Economy on the actions contained within the 14-19 Framework. This work includes consideration of how more effective collaboration can be achieved between schools, colleges and training providers. High quality careers education in schools is crucial in ensuring learners are aware of their options and the available pathways.  All teachers, including careers teachers, have a role to play in ensuring that learners are aware of the range of qualifications and progression pathways available through those qualifications. Young people are further supported through independent, impartial information and guidance about the range of pathways, including vocational and technical routes available to learners.Through the joint careers action plan, my officials are working with colleagues in the Department for the Economy to implement several actions that will increase opportunities for work related learning and engagement with employers for both learners and their teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when the Redwood facility will be open; and (ii) the reasons for its delay.
answer_text: (i) The South Eastern HSC Trust is working with the Regulation Quality Improvement Agency (RQIA) with a view to having the Redwood facility fully registered and opened by Monday 1st December 2025. (ii) The short delay with the opening of the Redwood facility has been attributed to the completion of estates works, successful recruitment of the staff team and meeting the requirements by the RQIA following their pre-registration visit to the home on Monday 10th November. The South Eastern HSC Trust has completed the specified requirements, and is awaiting an imminent response from the RQIA to progress opening.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures considered by her Department to ease congestion, caused by those turning right from the Hillhall Road onto Largymore Link, during peak times.
answer_text: I can advise that officials have previously considered the removal of the right turn at this junction following similar requests, however the consequential impact of this action may result in increased traffic volumes using Cromwell’s Highway to travel to the Saintfield Road roundabout. Cromwell’s Highway has been the subject of complaints from residents about through traffic and speeding and the removal of the right turn at the Hillhall Road may further encourage rat running to avoid the congestion between Sloan Street and the M1 Saintfield Road roundabout. Whilst the Hillhall Road carries significant volumes of traffic and the right turning manoeuvre into Largymore Link can cause some delays at peak times, the overall journey time between Largymore Link and Milltown Road is unlikely to be greatly affected by any queuing at this junction as a result of the provision of the right turn facility. Officials will continue to keep this location under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34786/22-27, for a breakdown of all independent reviews of his Department's programmes and Independent Assessors, that make up the table line entitled, Numerous independent reviews of DE programmes and costs in relation to Independent Assessors and Ministerial Advisory Panel, broken down (i) over the last three years; and (ii) related costs for each one.
answer_text: I refer you to AQW 34786/22-27 where you will note that I included a rationale for the consolidation of the information in the table line entitled “Numerous independent reviews of DE programmes and costs in relation to Independent Assessors and Ministerial Advisory Panel”, as follows:The costs of reviews of DE programmes, independent assessors and the International Ministerial Advisory Panel have been consolidated, as providing this information for these individually could lead to the release of personal and commercially sensitive information.As such, the Department is unable to provide a detailed list or breakdown of the costs relating to all independent reviews of DE programmes and Independent Assessors by financial year, as releasing this would breach Section 43 (2) of the Freedom of Information Act 2000 and data protection legislation as it could lead to the identification of the remuneration of individuals.Under the UK General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR) and the Data Protection Act 2018, personal data is defined as any information relating to an identifiable natural person. If statistical data can be linked to an individual—either directly or indirectly—it is considered personal data and must be protected accordingly.  Where appropriate, this encourages organisations to anonymise or suppress data to avoid identification and remain compliant with the legislation. This is often referred to as the principle of integrity and confidentiality, which requires the implementation of appropriate technical or organisational measures to safeguard personal data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any transformation work currently ongoing, following the publication of the latest School Population Projections, showing that pupil numbers are projected to decline by 12.7 per cent across all school phases by the 2033-2034 academic year.
answer_text: Area Planning is the process of strategic planning of primary and post-primary education provision. The Department requires the statutory planning authorities (the Education Authority and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools), working with the managing authorities and sectoral support bodies to identify future education needs and develop solutions to meet those needs on an area basis. School population projections are one of a range of data sets used to help identify future need. Actions are and will continue to be taken to reconfigure the network of schools, in line with my Department’s priorities, through the Area Planning process.  These changes are detailed in the Strategic Area Plan and associated Operational Plans and are brought forward through the statutory Development Proposal process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what the safety works at Broadwater Reservoir, will entail.
answer_text: Broadwater Reservoir is a Controlled Reservoir under the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015. My Department, as Reservoir Manager, oversees monitoring and maintenance. The improvement scheme is currently at design stage with this phase scheduled for completion by Spring 2026. The “National Protocol for the Handling, Transmission and Storage of Reservoir Information and Flood Maps (UK Reservoir Safety Liaison Group, 2018)”, prevents the release of specific details of the works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the reservoir safety works at the Broadwater Reservoir.
answer_text: Broadwater Reservoir is a Controlled Reservoir under the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015. My Department, as Reservoir Manager, oversees monitoring and maintenance. The improvement scheme is currently at design stage with this phase scheduled for completion by Spring 2026. The “National Protocol for the Handling, Transmission and Storage of Reservoir Information and Flood Maps (UK Reservoir Safety Liaison Group, 2018)”, prevents the release of specific details of the works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rational for a Direct Award Contract being used for the online Continued Professional Development Academy.
answer_text: I would refer the member to the answer to AQW 34775/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 14 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is a published agreement between Oxford Brookes University, Amira Learning and the Department of Education, regarding the Reading with AI Research Project.
answer_text: A contract in place between Department of Education and Oxford Brookes University in relation to the Reading with AI Research Project however, this is not a publicly available document.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Reading with AI Research Project became part of the RAISE Programme.
answer_text: The Reading with AI Research Project is one of a number of Cross-Cutting Initiatives progressing as part of the RAISE Programme. Cross Cutting Initiatives span across RAISE and other areas to address common issues and supplement locality led projects by - responding in a strategic way to cross-cutting priorities;bringing additional opportunities to communities around key themes e.g. literacy /numeracy support, improving school attendance and so on; andproviding opportunities to test the effectiveness of a range of initiatives in tackling educational disadvantage across all types of education setting both within RAISE localities and beyond.To that end officials provided a series of options for my consideration, which included the Reading with AI Research Study.  The project provides an opportunity to test how AI tools can support literacy, aligned with research-informed approaches to reading it can help to inform future evidence based policy.The Reading with AI Research Study aligns with one of RAISE intermediary benefits - Raising standards with a focus on increasing literacy, numeracy and digital skill outcomes for all pupils.  Furthermore, the need to improve literacy levels has also been highlighted across localities throughout the engagement phase of the programme’s development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that currently have pupil numbers under the sustainable enrolment thresholds for established schools.
answer_text: The Sustainable Schools Policy adopts the enrolment thresholds as recommended in the Bain Report. The report recommends that, as a minimum, an urban primary school should have 140 pupils, and rural primary schools should have 105 pupils (excluding supernumerary) in recognition of population levels in rural areas.In relation to post-primary schools a minimum enrolment of 500 pupils for Years 8-12 recommended, and 6th Forms should have a minimum of 100 pupils (excluding supernumerary).  There are 245 primary schools below their respective recommended enrolment (202 are rural primary schools and 43 are urban primary schools).In relation to post-primary schools there are 58 schools below the recommended minimum of 500 pupils for Years 8-12, and there are 35 schools below the recommended minimum of 100 pupils for post-16. This does not include those schools which do not provide post-16 provision (32).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions her Department is taking to address the road condition on Main Street, Moira, specifically the increasingly uneven section close to the entrance to St John's Park.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I have asked my officials to assess Main Street, Moira in the vicinity of the St John’s Park junction for possible inclusion in a future resurfacing programme.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the costs associated with the TransformED agenda, including a breakdown of (i) budget spent on actions and projects within the agenda; and (ii) costs associated with (a) events; (b) travel; and (c) other project expenses.
answer_text: The TransformED Agenda represents much needed investment in core education delivery across curriculum, assessment and the education workforce.  Overall, there are 30 separate actions within the TransformED Delivery Plan. The investment to date, across these 30 actions, is estimated at  £5,422,503.  The vast majority of this investment relates to Teacher Professional Learning (£5,027,000) and has been allocated directly to school budgets.The remaining expenditure to date relates to a variety of actions including Independent Review of Assessment (£27k), Qualifications Reform (£180k), Curriculum Reform (£12k) and Events, such as the TransformED school leaders conference, two-day curriculum conference for teachers and nine regional TransformED engagement events for school teachers and leaders (£108k).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority has met with community transport operators, to explore how they might be able to support with school transport for children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has engaged with Community Transport Hubs and other voluntary transport providers such as Disability Action, to assess their capacity to support home to school transport for pupils with Special Educational Needs (SEN). These groups provide a vital service in local communities; however, the transport they provide is different in nature to that of the school transport service and it hasn’t been feasible for community transport operators to provide the regular and consistent home to school transport services required. The EA informs me that this is due to a range of challenges, such as licensing requirements, funding structures, staffing limitations and the difficulty in allocating limited resources to daily scheduled services. The EA has advised me that it is continuing to liaise with community transport operators and will explore any opportunities to develop solutions to support school transport for pupils with SEN that align with its statutory obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 34658/22-27, to detail whether all of the children listed on tracer letters were accounted for following this measure.
answer_text: Not all children have been located.  It is important to note that the total number of cases includes children from other jurisdictions (i.e. remainder of UK and/or RoI) and in some cases it may simply be that the family has moved to the Republic of Ireland.  In such cases EA undertake routine checks to confirm this. The EA has established a Children Missing in Education post within the Education Welfare Service, funded by the Department of Education, as part of the EWS Transformation Programme (October 2025).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of any impact that price increases to school meals will have on children who are just outside the threshold for free school meals.
answer_text: In taking its decision to raise the price of a school meal for those not entitled to free school meals, the Education Authority (EA) recognised that children and young people having access to a nutritious lunch positively impacted both their educational and health outcomes.The EA acknowledged that if a child is no longer able to access a healthy lunch, it could result in a negative impact on these outcomes for any such children. However, a family not entitled to free school meals retains the choice of whether to purchase a school meal or to make their own arrangements, such as providing a packed lunch. To support parents in this, the Public Health Agency (PHA) publication “Are you packing a healthy lunch?” provides advice and guidance to parents to ensure their child is able to access an affordable, healthy and nutritionally balanced packed lunch.Consequently, if a family decides not to pay for a school meal, the availability of this guidance on preparing a healthy packed lunch means that all parents should be able to ensure that children are able to access an affordable, healthy lunch, either through the canteen or a packed lunch, without it having an adverse impact on their educational and health outcomes.The PHA publication is available at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/are-you-packing-healthy-lunch-english-and-irish-translation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any departmental resources that have been put towards the work on children missing in education, in each of the last 3 years.
answer_text: In addition to the resources addressing policy development provided by members of my Department’s Tackling Educational Disadvantage Team, funding has been provided to allow the Education Authority’s Education Welfare Service to create a range of specialist posts as part of a five year programme which commenced in the 2024/25 financial year.One of those posts will specifically seek to address the issue of Children Missing Education.A successful candidate has been appointed and is now in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has carried out (i) an equality impact assessment to determine any negative impact on children with special educational needs or disabilities; and (ii) a rural impact assessment to determine any impact on children and young people in rural areas, on the cuts to services announced by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that as a public authority, under Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, the mainstreaming of their Section 75 equality and good relations duties, as well as duties under the Rural Needs (Northern Ireland) Act 2016, has been embedded into their policy/decision-making processes.This includes consideration of any negative impact on children with special educational needs or disabilities and children and young people in rural areas for proposed Savings Measures 2025-26.The planned savings measures are being taken forward individually for implementation and those that have been finalised have had equality and rural needs screening/impact assessment completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of meetings officials from his Department have had regarding children missing in education.
answer_text: From the start of my tenure in February 2024 and in the month previous my officials in Tackling Educational Disadvantage Team hold a record of 9 meetings having taken place at which Children Missing Education was a topic for discussion.  These are listed below.06 October 2025 – DE/EA/NICCY Strategic Meeting - CME was an agenda item26 August 2025 – Working Group Meeting to discuss Children Missing Education, DE / EA officials 04 March 2025 – Meeting between Minister & NICCY – briefing provided on CME.19 February 2025 – Joint Health and Education Committee – briefing provided on CME. 17 February – Meeting between DE officials from TED Team and NISRA Team re CME14 November 2024 – Meeting between TED Team and NISRA Team06 November 2024 – Meeting between NISRA officials regarding data collection for CME24 October 2024 – Working Group Meeting to discuss Children Missing Education, DE / EA officials.January 2024 – DE Top Management Group – CME on the agenda for discussionIn addition, a further ten meetings have taken place involving officials responsible for Elective Home Education.These meetings have involved external stakeholders, DE officials and in certain cases, colleagues in DfE England to hear about progress of the Children’s Wellbeing Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any measures he plans to introduce to ensure children facing educational disadvantage will not be excluded from music lessons, following cuts to the Education Authority Music Service.
answer_text: My Department recognises the vital role of music education in supporting children’s development and engagement in learning. Music remains a compulsory element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum in both primary and post-primary levels.The Education Authority (EA) has advised my Department that it does not plan to make cuts to its Music Service. However, due to significant financial pressures and direction from the EA Board, the Music Service is reviewing its charging policy to increase income from its activities.A remissions policy is already in place to significantly reduce the charges for pupils entitled to Free School Meals who participate in Music Service activities, and it is expected that this support will continue. As part of its ongoing service transformation process, EA Music is exploring options to remove barriers to participation, including targeted support and partnership approaches, to ensure that music remains accessible to as many pupils as possible.My Department also provides Targeting Social Need funding and the Extended Schools Programme which can provide schools with flexibility to support pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds. These programmes can be used to deliver enrichment activities, including music-based programmes, tailored to local needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any engagement he has had with the Finance Minister, to secure additional funding for the Education Authority.
answer_text: I have consistently placed on record with the Finance Minister and the Department of Finance (DoF) that Education requires substantially more funding.From the outset of the 2025-26 budget process, even before the final budget allocation was known, my Department had engaged with DoF as part of an information gathering exercise to identify the funding requirement for 2025-26. At that time, using the initial planning budget provided by DoF, my Department advised DoF of a funding shortfall for Education of c£700m for all resource pressures and submitted resource bids of c£660m for additional funding for 2025-26, in respect of inescapable pressures only.Upon publication of the draft budget in December, in which my Department was allocated additional funding, my Department was still forecasting a significant budget shortfall for Resource DEL.  This budget shortfall, and the implications for the Education sector, were clearly communicated directly to the Finance Minister in my response to his Draft Budget 2025-26 Paper.  Upon finalisation of the Opening 2025-26 Budget my Department was forecasting a Resource DEL shortfall, after taking account of indicative June monitoring allocations of £32.9m, of c£310m.  Again, the implications of this shortfall for Education were communicated to the Finance Minister in my response to his Draft Final Budget Paper.Subsequent to this I, along with my officials, have continuously stressed the quantum and inescapable nature of the financial pressures facing Education in all budget correspondence, monitoring rounds and monthly forecast outturn returns to DoF.To date the only opportunity for my Department to bid for and receive additional funding was the June monitoring round. As part of June Monitoring,  my Department re-assessed previously identified pressures  and submitted Resource bids totalling £347m and Capital bids totalling £91.6m.  Additional Resource funding of £53.3m (which included the previously indicated allocation of £32.9m) and £12.3m Capital was secured at this time, together with £55m of Executive funding in respect of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.The Finance Minister has provided a commitment to my Department for first call on additional funding available in December monitoring, up to £37m in respect of Teachers’ Pay.  However, whilst this commitment is welcome, we have also been advised by DoF that it is unlikely that any further funding will be available at December monitoring.Whilst I welcome the additional funding that has been allocated to my Department to date, I remain committed to advocating for the required level of investment in Education.  Latest forecasts indicate my Department will overspend by c£288m for 2025-26. However, given the scale of pressures faced by the Northern Ireland Block Grant, the Executive also needs to continue to make the case to HM Treasury for additional funding for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any initiatives and decisions funded by his Department's discretionary budget, including a breakdown of costs, in each of the last 3 years.
answer_text: In response to the Department’s request for clarification, the member has clarified that the question relates specifically to the Department’s budget that it spends and excludes the funding allocated to the Education Authority, schools, or any of the Department’s other Non-Departmental Public Bodies. A detailed breakdown of my Department’s budgets over the last three years is as per the table below. Please note that my Department does not operate a discretionary budget. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of times he has met with the Minister of Education to discuss specialist educational needs provision and the relevant workforce, since coming into office.
answer_text: Special Educational Needs (SEN) provision is a matter that has been raised across the Executive on a number of occasions. I am fully committed to working with Minister Givan and welcome the ongoing engagement via the Executive and the priority in the Programme for Government in relation to SEN. My Department is fully committed to the SEN Reform Agenda and accompanying Delivery Plan, which were published earlier this year, and considerable engagement continues between Health and Education with senior Health Officials represented on a number of structures within the Governance Framework as well as the Joint Health/Education Oversight Group. Most recently the DE Permanent Secretary has written to my Department seeking Allied Health Profession and nursing input, a meeting has been scheduled to take place to consider the workforce implications between DE, PHA and DoH.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of Specialist Provision classes, by the number of pupils in each classroom.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Specialist Provision classes are needs based and capacity measurements will be more complex than in a mainstream setting. EA works closely with schools to ensure that children with special educational needs (SEN) are placed in the most appropriate provision to meet their individual needs.There are currently 273 schools with operational specialist provisions across the education estate.140 schools have specialist provision types where pupil numbers are aligned with or exceed the typical capacity range — reflecting a high level of demand and appropriate placement decisions. Of these, 75 schools have provision types that are currently operating at or above their usual capacity range.163 schools have provision types where pupil numbers are currently below the typical capacity range, which may reflect a change in pupil profile or a strategic decision to maintain smaller class sizes to support wellbeing and effective learning.The October 2024 census is the most recently published dataset. This data provides enrolment figures for Specialist Provision at school level but does not include a breakdown by individual classroom capacity or pupil distribution across classrooms within each Specialist Provision unit, as this is not routinely collected or published in the standard census returns. The school enrolment data at school level for 2024/25 can be accessed at: School enrolment - school level data 2024/25 | Department of Education One of the core design elements of Specialist Provisions is to allow pupils to integrate with Mainstream Classes and reverse integrate into specialist provisions in line with their identified and changing needs. This means that schools can decide how best to utilise their specialist provision to meet the needs of their school community. As such, if more detailed information about classroom organisation within specific Specialist Provision units is required, this would need to be requested directly from individual schools, as this operational detail sits outside the scope of the EA's centrally held data.

answer_text: The details on i) all paid external advisor roles in the Department and the (ii) amount spent in each of the last three years is set out in the table below.Type of Advice2024-252023-242022-23£000£000£000Legal Advice in respect of the Covid enquiry5747-Advice in relation to contract tendering, business cases, due diligence works on the contract tenderer, consultancy services and tax advice639222185Education Infrastructure Advice and Site evaluations6525851,909Legal Advice on capital schemes, leases and site purchases and disposals443358304Recruitment Competitions for Departmental Board members271-Professional support provided in relation to the RAISE Programme70--		Numerous independent reviews of DE programmes and costs in relation to Independent Assessors and Ministerial Advisory Panel*715297385Total2,6031,5102,783*The costs of reviews of DE programmes, independent assessors and the International Ministerial Advisory Panel have been consolidated, as providing this information for these individually could lead to the release of personal and commercially sensitive information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether Health and Social Care Trusts are engaging with the Education Authority's Local IMPACT Teams to consider merging services.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) and Department of Education (DE) are represented on the Children’s Disability Work Stream 5, which sits under the Reform of Children’s Social Care Programme Board.  Both EA and DE have been actively engaged in the Early Help / Family Support Task & Finish group.  The initial phase of this work has been completed and a draft paper has been compiled to include a scoping of existing peer supports and training across the region.  A number of recommendations have been made to enhance service provision and address unmet need, and the next stage of this work will be the development of a regional implementation plan.  This will involve Education Authority colleagues and will take forward agreed actions relating to more joined up and collaborative working.  A presentation on the SEN Reform was delivered to the Workstream on 23rd Sept, and areas for collaboration and working together were identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions his Department is taking to ensure there are appropriate screening tools, and diagnostic assessments, in Irish to ensure early identification and timely intervention for Irish-medium pupils with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that Educational Psychology Service (EPS) support, including planning, consultation, assessment, intervention and training, to the Irish Medium sector is calculated based on the needs across Northern Ireland schools, via an equitable allocation formula.  Each school has a named Educational Psychologist who offers a range of services, from which a school can choose according to their needs.  Efforts are made during school allocation to match Educational Psychologists interested in Irish/bilingualism with Irish Medium settings.  An internal EA EPS Working Group continues to meet regularly and is focused on identifying ways of supporting Irish Medium schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her consideration in relation to the installation of a right-handed arrow, painted on the road to provide a right turn lane, from the A26 Glenavy Road onto Station Road, Moira.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department has considered a range of options to provide a right turn lane on the A26 Glenavy Road into Station Road. These include the realignment of Magheramesk Lane and alteration to the Station Road junction to provide right turning pockets into Station Road or to both Magheramesk Lane and Station Road. These different layouts require the purchase of agricultural land and the realignment of public utility equipment.  There are also a number of substandard design features, which include a substandard alternative route using the Maghaberry Road.Due to concerns regarding road safety the scheme has not been progressed, and costs to complete such a scheme would be significant compared with the benefits that would be realised.My officials will continue to keep this junction layout under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any provisions being made within Local IMPACT Teams to ensure support can be provided to Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:The SEND Local IMPACT Team (LIT) model was based on the total staffing resource which spanned the pre-existing services. The existing contractual terms and conditions for all pre-existing roles also had to be respected during the reconfiguration. As a result, the arrangements for supporting Irish medium schools remains the same in terms of staff with Irish language proficiency being deployed to support Irish medium schools to the greatest extent possible. Irish language proficiency can and will be considered in terms of the profile of any staffing expansion which is ultimately approved across the LIT network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the recommendation within A Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum that his Department and the Department of Education jointly release guidance for parents on the amount of screentime that is healthy for children and to support parents with alternative ideas for supporting children’s play through Sure Start and Health Visits.
answer_text: I am advised by the Department of Education that while the Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum notes the issue of screen time, no recommendations were contained within the report on this issue. The Department of Education’s Sure Start programme is delivered in collaboration with the Department of Health. Sure Start engages parents in enhancing the education, health and social development of their children and the promotion and provision of stimulating play is an important aspect of the programme. Sure Start Screen Time guidance already presents key messages for digital usage by both parents and young children, which are reinforced by regular engagement with parents.The UK CMOs published 2019 guidance on 'Screen-based activities and children and young people’s mental health and psychosocial wellbeing'. There are no plans to update this guidance and this Department is not currently considering producing specific guidance on screen time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the time that special schools have Community Children's Nursing provision available to them determined.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33447/22-27, to detail (i) the number of hours per week the Community Children's Nurse spent at Knockevin school, prior to removal; and (ii) the number of children in the school the nurse was responsible for.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether school governors have to undergo Access NI checks.
answer_text: The Department's circular 2025/06 issued in March 2025 sets out arrangements for Pre-Employment / AccessNI check requirements and safer recruitment practices for staff and volunteers working in or providing a service for schools/education settings.In the event a governor performs activities or functions that may fall within the definition of ‘regulated activity,’ it is expected that schools and BoGs, in their safeguarding and child-protection role, make a local assessment on whether an AccessNI Enhanced Disclosure Certificate is required. The option to request an AccessNI check, without a check of the barred list, is permissible under the legislation and schools/employing authorities may complete such checks if they have cause to use them as an additional safeguarding measure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of tracer letters that have been sent out by the Education Authority, for children missing from school, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Education Welfare Service will conduct an internal check of the CAPITA database, which hosts the School Information Management System, to see if there any records of the child. If there is no record, a tracer will be sent out to all Northern Ireland schools to ascertain school registration.A total of 36 Tracer Letters have been sent to schools by the Education Authority over the last five years. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33447/22-27, to detail the number of special schools that have had the hours of Community Children's Nurse reduced, in the last two years.
answer_text: The Community Children’s Nursing (CCN) service supports children and young people in special schools whose care cannot be delegated to Classroom Assistants. The hours are determined by the individual needs of each child. The recently published PHA Review has identified that the delivery model for the provision of this support varies from school to school across the entire region.  A change in the delivery model in the Knockevin Special School in Downpatrick has reduced the on-site presence of a Community Children’s Nurse to two half days per week; however, the service remains available to the School should there be any queries or concerns, outside of these hours.  All Special Education Needs (SEN) schools across Northern Ireland continue to have provision available to manage the identified healthcare needs of the pupils attending them.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any urgent action her Department is taking to improve the safety of the footpaths on Moira Main Street, given the extensive cracking and uneven surfaces.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential responsive maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all actionable defects are noted and programmed for repair.An inspection of the footways along Main Street, Moira took place on 4 November 2025. A number of defects were identified and these have been issued for repair.

department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a stop in Glenavy or Crumlin will form part of the late-night public transport pilot.
answer_text: In addition to existing late-night services to Derry, Newry and the Airport, Translink plan to run late night bus services on key Metro routes as well as on 4 key Ulsterbus corridors that have no rail alternative.  These are Belfast to Dungannon, Newcastle, Downpatrick and Crumlin.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 33446/22-27, to detail any support in place to increase the number of active companies led by women.
answer_text: A range of initiatives is currently in place to support female entrepreneurship in the North. Invest NI leads with programmes such as Ambition to Grow Supporting Women, which has provided £1.2 million in grants to 39 women-led businesses, creating 78 jobs and generating £2.3 million in salaries, with further calls planned. It also funds Techstart’s female-focused Proof of Concept grants, awarding £595,000 to 17 entrepreneurs and facilitating £1.6 million in follow-on investment, alongside annual calls through 2029. Additionally, Invest NI supports innovation-driven enterprises through its Founder Labs Accelerator and Pre-Accelerator programmes, which have engaged 38 startups since mid-2024, 14 of which were female-led.	 	InterTradeIreland delivers the €30 million Shared Island Enterprise Scheme, allocating €8 million to female entrepreneurship through thematic programmes such as W.I.R.ED, Shared Growth, Scale Together, and Future Focused Tech. These aim to support up to 642 women directly, with at least 30% participation from the North. 		At Council level Go Succeed is exceeding its 50% female participation target, offering female-only masterclasses, peer networks, and outreach, with plans to introduce tailored support for high-growth female-led startups in Autumn 2025. 		Other measures include engagement with the Invest in Women Taskforce, Enterprise Funding for Growth programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she is considering the introduction of a default part-time 20mph speed limit zone outside every school, with the option for schools to opt out.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.At present, my Department is prioritising the introduction of part-time 20mph zones at those schools where such measures are most appropriate, effective, and beneficial. This approach takes into account factors such as the location of some schools on roads where a part-time 20mph zone would not be suitable, as well as the limited resources and funding available. Therefore, we are focusing our efforts on delivering priority tranches, with a significant number of schools already benefitting from these interventions.My Department is actively progressing a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools, bringing the total to 273 schools since 2020/21. This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis. The three tranches equate to an investment of £5m by my Department.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking and cycling.My Department is also carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33227/22-27, to detail (i) whether his Department holds information on what is required to be reported as an assault; and (ii) any actions his Department is taking to support staff, and students, given the increase in reported assaults.
answer_text: In relation to what is required to be reported as an assault my department is aware of what is required to be reported as an assault. My department adheres to the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland’s (HSENI’s) definition of work related violence is “Any incident in which a person is abused, threatened or assaulted in circumstances relating to their work”.Through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) a Policy Statement on Tackling Violence and Abuse Behaviour Against Teachers, TNC 2011/2 has been agreed and this policy explains the responsibilities of Employing Authorities, Boards of Governors, Principals and Teachers regarding assaults and reporting requirements.We work closely with all the education employers to ensure that all incidents of physical assaults are recorded and managed effectively.There is an obligation on all schools, to report incidents where either a staff member or pupil is physically assaulted resulting in injury.Incidents where there is no injury are recorded and managed within each school.In relation to supporting staff and students, my department has already responded to a previous question from the member (AQW 32023/22-27 refers) detailing a Violence at Work Policy that the Education Authority has developed, along with associated guidance documents, designed to minimise risk from aggressive or violent behaviour and to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace. They also provide a framework for good practice including the steps to take and the support available to victims in an educational setting that have been subjected to an assault.   This Policy has been finalised, and discussions are currently underway with all education sectors and trade unions regarding its rollout and implementation.Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools have statutory duties under Articles 17 and 18 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to promote and safeguard the welfare of all their pupils, including keeping them safe from harm when required. While the department is not involved in individual school arrangements, we work with the Education Authority Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) who engage with schools where a concern has been raised in order to ensure guidance is being followed and to seek wider assurance. Any form of assault may constitute a criminal matter and need to be referred to the PSNI.All educational settings should put in place a Safety and Support Plan which considers the physical and emotional safety of any child who has allegedly been harmed.  The child, parents/carers, school staff, Social Services, the PSNI and external support agencies should be included in the development of the Safety and Support Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the evidential threshold needed for a death to be recorded as caused by an eating disorder; and (ii) what dedicated eating disorder services are focused on prevention.
answer_text: The Deaths and Births Registration (Northern Ireland) Order 1976 states that a Medical Certificate of Cause of Death must be completed by a registered medical practitioner who must state ‘to the best of his knowledge and belief the cause of death’. This is also reflected in Departmental guidance related to Death Certification.The clinical judgement and experience of the doctor who treated a deceased person is relied upon to give the most accurate and complete record of the cause of death and any contributory factors.Eating Disorder Specialist Teams across the five HSC Trusts have developed community based psychological therapy services using evidence-based treatments in line with the National Institute for Clinical Excellence (NICE) guidance. Teams also work to the Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders (2016). Individuals are referred to these services for treatment when an eating disorder is evident. As such, these services focus on treatment rather than prevention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33227/22-27, whether there is an issue of underreporting in secondary schools.
answer_text: As reported in earlier correspondence with the member, my Department does not hold information on assaults on staff or pupils in its schools.Reporting information is maintained by the Education Authority (EA), and my officials have asked them to provide a response for this AQW.The EA utilises an On-line Accident Reporting System for schools to digitally report accidents. While the EA does not hold any data on levels of under-reporting at post primary school level, it is aware that 89% of controlled and maintained post primary schools currently have active accounts on the on-line accident reporting system. There is an active work stream within EA Health and Safety Service to address the remaining 11% of post primary schools to create accounts and report accidents via the on-line accident reporting system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount her Department spent on the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research report.
answer_text: My Department spent £153,288 on the Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research.  The cost was met by the Department for Education from the £25m allocated by the Executive for early learning and childcare in 2024/25.
