question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) anyone consulted as part of the 2024 review of the GCSE equivalency of Level 2 Essential Skills qualifications; and (ii) the rational why Level 2 Essential Skills Literacy, Numeracy and ICT qualifications have been changed to carry a value equivalent to 0.25 of a GCSE.
answer_text: In relation to part (i) of your question, last year, my officials consulted with schools on proposed changes to how qualifications with different guided learning hours, levels and grading scales are counted to produce school system level performance measures such as the proportion of learners achieving 5 GCSEs (including equivalents) at A* - C. The consultation was not specifically about Essential Skills.  As the changes were about school performance measures, engagement was targeted towards school leaders and their TUS representatives. We also advised the Education Committee of the content and scope of the consultation.In relation to part (ii) of your question, it is important to note that performance points are solely intended to support the measurement of school system performance. They do not reflect how qualifications should be viewed by employers, further education institutions, or universities. This distinction was clearly outlined in both the consultation document and in DE Circular Number 2025/01 – Approval of Qualifications for Delivery by Post-Primary Schools in Northern Ireland.Within the school performance framework, Essential Skills qualifications accrue performance points which reflect the number of guided learning hours (GLH) associated with these qualifications. GCSEs have 120 GLH, whereas Essential Skills have between 40 to 45 guided learning hours.   Previously Essential Skills qualifications with 45 GLH were allocated 0.5 points, and those with 40 GLH received 0.25.  Following the consultation all Essential Skills qualifications are allocated performance points of 0.25 as they fall into the bracket of requiring between 30 and 59 GLH to deliver the course.It is also important to note that my department expects that almost all young people in schools will be given the opportunity to follow GCSE courses in English and mathematics, as these qualifications provide the most comprehensive and widely recognised foundation in literacy and numeracy.  GCSEs are designed with a high level of rigour and depth, and they are aligned with progression routes into further education, training, and employment.  For the vast majority of learners GCSEs offer the best preparation for future success. However, the Department recognises that some learners may not be able to access GCSE courses due to specific educational needs or circumstances. In such cases, Essential Skills in Communication and Application of Number provide a valuable and credible alternative. Data shows that this is reflected in practice with the vast majority of learners in schools following GCSE courses in English and mathematics. In 2023/24, before the consultation on performance points, 23,874 took GCSE English and 385 were recorded as taking level 2 Essential skills Communication. The figures for GCSE mathematics and Essential Skills level 2 Application of number were 26,507 and 180 respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33229/22-27, in cases where the Evidential Test is satisfied, whether the Education Authority robustly and consistently considers and applies the Public Interest Test.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) including its in house legal function, EA Solicitors, is not covered by the Code for Prosecutors issued by the Director of Public Prosecutions under Section 37 of the Justice Act (Norther Ireland) 2002 and which details the Evidential Test and the Public Interest Test.   EA will only consider prosecuting a parent under Article 45 and Schedule 13 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 (for failing to ensure their child receives efficient, full-time education suitable to their age, ability, aptitude, and any special educational needs either by regular attendance at school or otherwise) after thoroughly reviewing all relevant circumstances. Prosecution is normally pursued only as a last resort once all other strategies and interventions have been exhausted including consideration of whether it would be more appropriate, either instead of or in addition to prosecution, to apply for an Education Supervision Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the amount of the £55 million early learning and childcare funding being allocated to her Department; and (ii) the amount her Department has budgeted on spending by the end of the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department secured £415,000 for childcare skills pathways and is on target to spend the full amount by the end of the financial year.		Separately, the Education Minister has allocated £2 million for Childcare Sector Business Support actions arising from the Targeted Business Support Market Research. DfE is leading on recommendation 1 of that Research: "the creation of a specialised ongoing childcare business advisor role or service”.	My officials are working closely with Invest NI to develop a costed delivery plan, with a view to launching a pilot service in this financial year. 			The funding required from the Department of Education for this financial year to deliver the childcare business advisory service will be informed by the costed plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when changes will be finalised following the consultation on proposals to amend the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12.
answer_text: My officials have been analysing all 495 responses to the consultation exercise, and I will be taking decisions on proposed next steps in the coming weeks.   If it is considered that any urgent changes should be made to the Minimum Standards in advance of the completion of the wider review, these changes will be communicated through a Departmental Press Release, a Circular to HSC Trusts and a letter to all childcare providers.The full consultation response document will also be published on the Departmental website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33229/22-27, to outline, when a decision has been made to prosecute, whether the Education Authority takes into account any changes in circumstances that occur as the case proceeds.
answer_text: All cases are reviewed in accordance with court sittings in both Magistrates and Family Proceedings Courts. Education Welfare (EA) work on the premise of protection, paramountcy and partnership and all matters impacting young persons are assessed and reviewed to assist with interventions in all settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any engagement he has had with the UK Treasury ahead of the Chancellor’s Budget on 26 November 2025.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to prepare schools on preventing and responding to Artificial Intelligence-Generated image exploitation.
answer_text: My department is currently working with education partners to develop guidance for schools on the use of Generative Artificial Intelligence. Alongside this, officials are liaising closely with the Education Authority to ensure that the guidance is published in tandem with the release of AI resources to schools through the Education Information Systems (EdIS) Programme.Guidance and support on the use of AI and some of the emergent challenges is available to children, young people, their families and schools via the Safer Schools Northern Ireland app and the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland online resource hub.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department is creating guidance for schools, regarding the sharing of images of children and young people.
answer_text: I do not intend to issue separate guidance to schools on this matter as the existing statutory guidance ‘Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools’ already outlines the responsibilities of Boards of Governors and schools in safeguarding the welfare of their pupils. Schools should ensure that any sharing or publication of images of children and young people – including on school websites or social media – is carried out responsibly, with the appropriate permissions in place and in full compliance with their internal e-safety and data protection arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients removed from waiting lists due to criteria changes, in the last two years.
answer_text: Thank you for your previous correspondence and follow up question. There has been no change to the position previously set out in my recent response. The updated Effective Use of Resources (EUR) policy was published in 2023 alongside the rollout of the Encompass Programme, which is digitising patient records across all HSC Trusts.This has temporarily limited reporting on restricted procedures, including data on patients removed from waiting lists because they no longer meet EUR criteria. The most current data relates to 2023/24 and is referenced in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Performance of Restricted Procedures by Health Trusts (8 October 2025).The Department and HSC Trusts are working to embed future monitoring within the new digital infrastructure and strengthen governance of the EUR policy. Once this work is complete, the requested information will be more accessible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31763/22-27, to detail the rationale as to why this research, relevant to bullying, was removed and other reports into bullying, which predate it, have remained on his Department's website, including research from 2007 and 2011.
answer_text: A review of the department’s website was launched on 10th July 2025. As part of this review over 300 publications have been archived to date. This work is currently ongoing and Research Reports from 2007 and 2011 into the nature and extent of bullying in schools in Northern Ireland have been identified as part of this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the works undertaken in Dromara in relation to street lighting, since 2020; and (ii) the costs incurred by her Department for these works.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the provision and maintenance of lighting on adopted public roads and footways.Since 2020, all the streetlights in Dromara have been upgraded to LED lanterns under my Department’s LED transformation policy. In addition, the columns and associated cable network have been upgraded in Moybrick Road, Moybrick Grove, Hillsborough Road, Dromore Street, Rathfriland Road and Barclay Manor, Dromara. The estimated cost of these works is in the region of £200k-£250k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reason why the fully functional street lights on the Hillsborough Road and Rathfriland Road, Dromara, were removed.
answer_text: When a street lighting system in an area reaches the end of its serviceable life, a survey is carried out at the location and replacement lighting is installed only in those areas which meet my Department’s policy for the provision of road lighting, as outlined in Street lighting RSPPG E072 Following an assessment, the existing street lighting in Dromara Village, including Hillsborough Road and Rathfriland Road, was identified as requiring replacement. A new lighting installation was subsequently provided, with the extent of coverage reviewed in line with my Department’s policy.Lighting was provided to the boundaries of the 30mph speed limits on Hillsborough Road and Rathfriland Road. The areas beyond this do not meet my Department’s policy and the existing lights were therefore removed.This policy, which has been in place since 1993, helps to ensure that lighting is provided where it is most needed, while also balancing considerations such as maintenance costs, energy reduction and environmental impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action he is taking as a result of the final analysis of the School Uniform Consultation, which noted concerns around growing levels of misogyny in schools, and instances of upskirting, and sexual harassment towards schoolgirls.
answer_text: The consultation response you refer to also notes that school uniform policies will not address the root causes of the behaviours cited.  Bullying, misogyny and sexual harassment in our schools, for whatever reason and in whatever form, is always unacceptable.  I am - and my Department is - committed to tackling such issues in schools and supporting all our pupils.  This can only be progressed through a consistent approach across a range of education policy areas.  Should schools need to adjust their uniform policy to support pupils in feeling comfortable, there is no barrier in the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill to this. The curriculum places a strong emphasis on healthy relationships and schools can play a key role in addressing emerging issues such as violence against women and girls, misogyny, coercive control, and sexual harassment, including behaviours like upskirting.  The systems and protocols in place for educational settings impacted by reported instances involving sexual harassment or upskirting  - which are forms of problematic sexual behaviour - are outlined in DE Circular 2022/02 – Children Who Display Harmful Sexual Behaviour.   Recording requirements under the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 reflect relevant Section 75 groups as well as additional common motivating factors, such as misogyny. My Department issued circular 2024/14 due to the growing concerns about the impact of mobile phones on children and young people’s development and their unauthorised use in class or during the school day.  Implementing a robust and effective mobile phone policy in schools can significantly reduce the opportunities for inappropriate behaviour linked to personal mobile phones, including the misuse of smartphone apps and functions such as its camera during the school day.   More than 8,000 young people across Northern Ireland will be involved in the Department’s phone-free pilot scheme.My Department takes a range of actions and provides a range of guidance and advice to support Boards of Governors to discharge their statutory duties to safeguard and promote the welfare of their pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any businesses, skills groups, and stakeholders who have been consulted as part of the Consultation on the future of (i) CCEA (ii) GCSEs, (iii) AS levels; and (iiii) A levels.
answer_text: The consultation on the future of CCEA GCSEs, AS levels and A levels is an opportunity for stakeholders from all sectors to participate via the questionnaire, available online, until 13 November. I welcome the views of businesses, skills groups and any other stakeholder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of special schools that have had nursing provision withdrawn, in the last two years.
answer_text: All Special Education Needs (SEN) schools across Northern Ireland have had nursing provision available to manage the identified healthcare needs of the pupils attending them throughout the last two years.In 2023/24 the delivery model in Knockevin school changed to an in-reach model with Community Children’s Nurses (CCNs) providing assessment and care planning for those children with complex health care needs. The nurses provided knowledge and skills-based training to key staff within the school and were available for queries and guidance as necessary.After discussions with the South Eastern Trust, the presence of an onsite nurse in Knockevin was stepped up again in 2025 and currently a CCN is on site 2 mornings per week in addition to the presence of Allied Health Professional colleagues.Following the recent publication of the PHA review of the healthcare needs of children attending SEN schools, my Department is set to commence work alongside colleagues from Education and other key stakeholders, to ensure that all children in SEN schools have access to necessary healthcare.  This will seek to ensure the best outcomes for all these children, to enable them to access the curriculum in a school setting while receiving the right care at the right time by the right person and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the urgent actions her Department will take to improve road safety in the vicinity of Downshire Primary School, Hillsborough.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.I was sorry to hear of the recent collision involving a pupil from the school and wish them a speedy recovery. I have asked my officials to arrange a meeting with local elected representatives and Downshire Primary School to discuss their concerns regarding road safety.  This meeting is scheduled for 4th November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will restore nursing provision to all special schools.
answer_text: All special schools across Northern Ireland can access appropriate nursing provision in line with the identified needs of the pupils attending them.Any decisions regarding future levels of nursing provision in special educational schools, and the approach to its delivery, will be guided by the recommendations set out in the recently published Public Health Agency report on the healthcare needs of children attending SEN schools.My Department is now initiating engagement with key stakeholders to progress this important work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the data her Department captured, regarding the number of women starting businesses in Northern Ireland, over the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold official statistics on the number of women starting businesses in the North. However, we do monitor external research sources that provide valuable insights into female entrepreneurship. The Global Entrepreneurship Monitor for example, tracks total early-stage entrepreneurial activity, the percentage of the working-age population starting or running a new business.  In 2023, the rate of female total early-stage entrepreneurial activity was 8.2% in the North; this was an increase from the 7.3% recorded in 2022, and continued the upward trend seen in recent years. The Gender Index 2025 indicates that the North was the best performing region for new female led incorporations in 2024, with 21.8% of newly registered companies being female-led – this was higher than Britain’s average of 20.5%. The Gender Index also shows however, that we continue to have the lowest proportion of active companies led by women – this was 17.5% in 2024, compared to Britain’s average of 19.1%. While I am encouraged by the progress being made, I recognise that there is still more to do to support women in business. To that end, my Department, and its partner organisations, will continue to support female entrepreneurship through a range of initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding needed to ensure special educational needs and disabilities Local IMPACT Teams can support all statutory and non-statutory early years providers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:EA analysis suggests that an additional recurrent cost of approximately £6 million per annum is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 32023/22-27, to detail a breakdown of the data by (i) primary; (ii) post-primary; and (iii) special, schools.
answer_text: The data provided in AQW 32023/22-27 to the member has been broken down into school type by the Education Authority below and details assaults on staff and pupils within both Controlled and Catholic Maintained Schools.Assaults on Staff by School TypeSchool Type20202021202220232024Grand TotalNursery02013823Other2814442189Post Primary8579635Primary3935113119288594Special74112231444189532568559Grand Total79012731578208035799300Assaults on Pupils by School TypeSchool Type20202021202220232024Grand TotalNursery13815633Other077211449Post Primary9285133158230698Primary3750103178181549Special2564215147589202869Grand Total386566765113013514198

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an assessment of the implementation of the equality and good relations duties, as set out in Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, across public authorities.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) remains committed to the effective implementation of equality and good relations duties as set out in Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.  Each department is individually responsible for meeting these duties, with oversight provided by the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland (ECNI). TEO integrates equality considerations into decision making and policy development through screening exercises and, where necessary, Equality Impact Assessments. This work helps with identifying potential impacts and ensuring that opportunities to promote equality and good relations are actively pursued.TEO continues to work with other government departments and the Equality Commission to strengthen this work and help to deliver positive outcomes for people and communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 31072/22-27, to detail whether the Department of Education have policy responsibility for (i) the Employment of Children Regulations (Northern Ireland)1996 and the Employment of Children (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006; and (ii) the Children (Public Performances) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996.
answer_text: The Department of Health holds legislative responsibility for these Regulations. While the Education Authority administers the process for issuing the permits and licenses, the Regulations are primarily concerned with the safeguarding and welfare of children and young people in employment and performance contexts rather than in educational settings.  As previously indicated my officials will engage with both the Department of Health and Economy in respect of this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of how many times (i) the Education Authority has initiated legal action against parents, or carers, due to a child's school attendance; and (ii) these cases have related to children with special educational needs and disabilities, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The number of parents/guardians of children who have had court action initiated (Magistrates Court) as follows:Office2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Ballymena47141029Belfast53121Dundonald82100Omagh1045253723Armagh253061117TOTAL5287476070(ii) The Education Authority’s Education Welfare Service does not hold this data or breakdown cases based on SEN. The case is taken against the Parent/Carer based on the level of co-operation. Under Article 45 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, it is the duty of parents who have a child of compulsory school age to ensure their child receives efficient, full-time education suitable to their age, aptitude, ability and to any special educational needs they may have.Paragraph 3 (1) of Schedule 13 to the 1986 Order states that it is the duty of a parent of a registered pupil of a school to secure their regular attendance at school. This applies to all children of compulsory school age who are on the roll of a school. Parents are legally responsible for making this happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (I) whether a workforce strategy for the early years sector is forming part of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy; and (ii) how he will ensure that the workforce strategy won't simply become an action to be completed in the future.
answer_text: I will bring forward a draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.  This includes proposals to support the childcare workforce, training needs, recruitment and retention challenges. However, implementation of the Strategy will be subject to Executive approval and agreement to release the additional funding required.The following actions to support the early learning and childcare workforce have already been implemented from Executive funding provided in 2024-25 and 2025-26, including funding:-the Department of Communities to provide childminding academies through Labour Market Partnerships, resulting in over 150 newly trained childminders to date;the Department of Health for a pilot scheme to recruit Approved Home Childcare providers; andboth the Department for the Economy and the Department of Health to enhance training opportunities across the early learning and childcare sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail a timeline for the development of a proposal for a targeted business support service for childcare providers.
answer_text: The Childcare Providers Targeted Business Support Research Report was published on 24 September 2025 and can be accessed at Targeted Business Support for Childcare Providers Market Research.The Education Minister has agreed that my Department will take the operational lead on the design and delivery of Recommendation 1; namely, the creation of an on-going childcare business advisor role or service. Its longer-term strategic ambitions will be overseen by the Early Learning & Childcare Strategy Task and Finish Group on which my Department is represented. My officials are now working closely with Invest NI to develop a costed plan for the delivery of this service. I recognise the importance of ensuring that the service will be available to voluntary and community providers; childminders; and childcare businesses alike. It is equally important that the service operates on a continuous basis so that we can gain a better understanding of the financial needs of the sector and equip us to adapt our support to meet the evolving needs of childcare providers over time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether any additional funding will go to the Education Authority to ensure Local Impact Teams are properly staffed.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 31022/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 26 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy will be published.
answer_text: I will bring forward a draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for Executive consideration and public consultation in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans in place to prevent flooding on A26 underneath the railway bridge, near the Moira roundabout.
answer_text: Inspection and maintenance of gullies and drains are critical components of rural infrastructure management. I am advised that the road gullies at this location drain into open channels situated on adjacent land. My officials have made arrangements for both the gullies and open drains in the vicinity to be cleaned, and it is anticipated that this work will be completed by the end of October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 26332/22-27; and (ii) AQW 26329/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of requests received from schools for support for (i) cognition and learning; (ii) social, behavioural, emotional and well-being; (iii) speech, language and communication needs; (iv) sensory; and (v) physical needs, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided the following breakdown of requests received from schools: Educational Psychology Service(1 September 2024 - 31 August 2025)Local Impact Teams*(30 January 2025 - 6 October 2025)Cognition and Learning12431759Social, Behavioural, Emotional and Well-being20991142Speech, Language and Communication Needs1130428Sensory8810Physical Needs17421*All other data is held in Legacy areas and held by each individual service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any cost analysis carried out on moving the role of special educational needs coordinator to a dedicated non-teaching position in all schools.
answer_text: To date there has been no cost analysis completed on moving the role of special educational needs coordinator to a dedicated non-teaching position in all schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any contingency plans in place for schools and pre-schools in response to weather warnings issued by the Met Office; and (ii) whether the Education Authority will complete a review of their communication policy following Storm Amy.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) have a range of materials to support contingency planning within schools and pre-schools. These include contingency arrangements for schools to access Emergency Maintenance Support, Guidance on Managing a Critical Incident and a Critical Incident Helpline, Severe Weather guidance, and Emergency Planning guidance. This is further supported by adverse weather risk assessments. In addition to these resources the EA has a Severe Weather Protocol to support schools in periods of severe weather.The EA, as standard practice, will complete a full debrief and review following any emergency incidents responded to, and communications will be a part of this process. I have also commissioned an internal review following Storm Amy to consider the communication methods used by the EA and to assess the decision-making processes that were utilised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce a transitional bursary, or financial support package, for current PGCE students who are excluded from the 2026 Initial Teacher Education bursary scheme.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme, and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot, is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages.Extending the scheme to include current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the scheme. It would also incur significant additional costs, which cannot be met from within the Department’s existing 2025/26 budget. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when he plans to publish consultations relating to (i) Small Business Rate Relief; and (ii) Non-Domestic Vacant Rating.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of Rates for Small Business Rate Relief and Non-domestic Vacant Rating have now been completed. During the Review my officials carried out research, taxbase analysis, extensive engagement with key stakeholders and councils, along with a related business survey. I am currently reviewing the results of both Reviews after which I will provide an update to the Assembly, including on the timing of any consultation work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Department consulted with the Education Authority on what special educational needs and disabilities projects were included in the Public Sector Transformation bid.
answer_text: The projects within my department’s Public Sector Transformation bid were informed by the department and the Education Authority (EA) jointly led End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and drawn directly from the resulting SEN Reform Delivery Plan.  A wide range of stakeholders and delivery partners, including the EA, were involved in this process.  My department and the EA are working in partnership to implement the projects funded by the Public Sector Transformation Board (PSTB) and are fully committed to delivering on my wider SEN Reform agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he will ensure that future Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration pay recommendations are implemented in a timely way to ensure they do not face inflated tax liabilities in relation to pension contributions.
answer_text: I am committed towards implementing the pay awards in a timely manner. My aim has been, and will be in the years to come, to have the pay award in Northern Ireland paid to a similar timescale as in England. My officials are working with the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration to ensure recommendations are received as early as possible to allow maximum time to secure and implement the pay award. However, there is a need to recognise the challenge my Department faces in relation to our budget and the challenging financial position my Department finds itself in.Additionally, as with any pay award, there is a process that must be followed before it is paid to staff. My Department require approval from the Department of Finance prior to issuing a revised pay circular. From the date of issue of the circular, BSO require a period of 12 weeks to request, test and implement the required IT changes.I recognise the importance of avoiding delays in implementing pay awards and I remain committed to ensuring that, for now and in the future, our HSC workforce is fairly rewarded for their vital contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action her Department is taking to improve transport links to the Maze Long Kesh site, including the Eikon Exhibition Centre.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to improve transport linkage to the Maze Long Kesh Site. Should future development of the site be proposed, consideration will be given to any requirement for upgrading of the transport infrastructure.Whilst I acknowledge that the Balmoral Show, which attracts over 120,000 visitors per annum, creates significant traffic congestion in the local area over a 4 day period in May, other events during the year occur on a monthly basis with relatively low attendance. To date my officials have not been made aware of any particular traffic management issues during these lesser events.  Planning permission has been granted to a developer for the Knockmore Link Road and, if built, this developer led and privately funded project would provide links from the local road network to the Motorway at Sprucefield.  A planning application has recently been lodged for a significant mixed-use development on the lands between the existing M1 Junction 8 / A101 roundabout and Moira Road / Knockmore Road junction. The approved Knockmore Link Road is to be provided in support of this large-scale development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the number of patients that have been removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to a change of criteria in the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what active travel improvements will be introduced between Maghaberry and Moira Train Station, to improve foot and bicycle safety along the route.
answer_text: In relation to the planned active travel improvements between Moira and Moira train station, design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.  A typical scheme of this nature can take a number of years to design, and meaningful engagement with the public throughout the design process is crucial if we want to deliver high quality infrastructure that will be welcomed by users and the community. At this stage it is too early to provide an accurate timeline as to when public engagement will take place.Future investment in significant active travel infrastructure improvements will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. There are currently no plans to improve active travel infrastructure between Maghaberry and Moira Train Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30619/22-27 ,to detail (i) a timeline for the design stage; and (ii) when the public consultation is likely to take place.
answer_text: In relation to the planned active travel improvements between Moira and Moira train station, design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.  A typical scheme of this nature can take a number of years to design, and meaningful engagement with the public throughout the design process is crucial if we want to deliver high quality infrastructure that will be welcomed by users and the community. At this stage it is too early to provide an accurate timeline as to when public engagement will take place.Future investment in significant active travel infrastructure improvements will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. There are currently no plans to improve active travel infrastructure between Maghaberry and Moira Train Station.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30621/22-27, for a breakdown of additional funding provided to the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland, outside of salaries.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW 30621/22-27, the funding provided by my Department to the SBNI to support implementation of the Online Safety Strategy outside of salaries is as follows:2022/23:  £78,2082023/24:  £18,6102024/25:  £27,5972025/26:  £23,9352026/27 (forecast): £20,829

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 will be published for public consultation.
answer_text: The Eastern Transport Plan is the largest of the local transport plans and will integrate with the Local Development Plans of the Antrim and Newtownabbey; Ards and North Down; Belfast; Lisburn and Castlereagh; and Mid and East Antrim councils. Due to the five local council areas being at different stages in their Local Development Plans, the Eastern Transport Plan will therefore be published in iterations. As Belfast City Council is most advanced, the first iteration will cover the Belfast City Centre Transport Plan and Metropolitan Area Transport Plan. A draft of these elements of the ETP is being prepared with the intention that the draft will be ready for public consultation early in the New Year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of reported assaults against a) staff; and b) students, in schools in each of the last five years; and (ii) what specific measures his Department is taking to support victims within an education setting.
answer_text: Assaults on school staff and pupils are not reported to my Department but instead are reported to the Education Authority (EA).The EA has provided the following data, which details reported assaults against staff and students within both Controlled Schools and Catholic Maintained Schools in each of the past 5 years:Year 20202021202220232024TOTALNumber of reported assaults against staff79012731578208035799300Year 20202021202220232024TOTALNumber of reported assaults against students386566765113013514198The EA has developed a Violence at Work Policy along with associated guidance documents, designed to minimise risk from aggressive or violent behaviour and to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace. They also provide a framework for good practice including the steps to take and the support available to victims in an educational setting that have been subjected to an assault.   This Policy has been finalised, and discussions are currently underway with all education sectors and trade unions regarding its rollout and implementation.Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools have statutory duties under Articles 17 and 18 of the Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 2003 to promote and safeguard the welfare of all their pupils, including keeping them safe from harm when required. While the Department is not involved in individual school arrangements, we work with the Education Authority Child Protection Support Service (CPSS) who engage with schools where a concern has been raised in order to ensure guidance is being followed and to seek wider assurance. Any form of assault may constitute a criminal matter and need to be referred to the PSNI.All educational settings should put in place a Safety and Support Plan which considers the physical and emotional safety of any child who has allegedly been harmed.  The child, parents/carers, school staff, Social Services, the PSNI and external support agencies should be included in the development of the Safety and Support Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what actions within the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda - Delivery Plan 2025-2030, will be completed this year.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and its associated five-year Delivery Plan contain over 100 actions.  As outlined in the Delivery Plan, transformation will take time and full implementation will require significant investment. Since publication of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, substantial progress has been made on delivering the ‘enabling’ conditions to support reform, including the publication of an Outcomes Framework in June 2025 and the SEN Policy Statement in August 2025.  Governance structures and a delivery unit within the department have also been established this year.In addition and since publication in February 2025, there has been significant progress on completing and commencing a number of year one actions including:Publication of the Education Authority’s (EA) Operational Plan 2:  2024-26 (OP2) in January 2025;Publication of the EA’s Graduated Response Framework (GRF) in March 2025;Phased implementation of the EA’s Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) from September 2025; Completion in June 2025 of a public consultation on changing the ‘supernumerary’ status for children with a statement of SEN;EA’s Special Schools Plans of Action published September 2025;Pre-School Education Inclusion Fund (PSEIF) continued into 2025/26;Phase Two of the Education & Training Inspectorate’s (ETI) review of specialist provisions in mainstream schools is ongoing and expected to report by December 2025;Progression on the digitisation of the statutory assessment and review process; andDesign and development work on the 13 actions funded under the Public Sector Transformation Programme, the majority of which will commence during the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the annual cost of reintroducing the Happy Healthy Minds programme or a similar initiative enabling primary schools to have access to counselling services.
answer_text: It is important that any future provision is considered in conjunction with health colleagues and wider health services, as primary providers of mental health services and therapies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of the job roles within each of the Education Authority's local IMPACT teams.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 30988/22-27, published in the weekly answer booklet for week ending 26th September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what training is received by those employed by the Education Authority as escorts travelling with children with special educational needs and disabilities.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that escorts travelling with pupils on home to school transport complete a staff induction, mandatory EA staff training and they receive regular training related to their role. The training provided includes child protection and safeguarding, autism awareness and managing challenging behaviour. Any training specific to the individual Special Educational Needs (SEN) or medical needs of pupils is arranged as required. Additionally, escorts take part in the Passenger Assistant Training Scheme, which the EA informs me is currently being reviewed and further tailored to create a bespoke package specific to a SEN transport role. Manuals outlining the key procedures related to the EA transport escort role are also in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what surveys are being distributed to capture data for the Special Educational Needs Disability Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework 2025-2030; and (ii) when are they due to be sent out.
answer_text: My department is currently developing a range of surveys, supported by a NISRA statistician, to capture baseline data for the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda Outcomes Framework.  These surveys will gather a range of data which is not currently captured elsewhere from parents, schools, services and children and young people. Fieldwork is expected to begin during Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail research funded by his Department which has been removed from the Department's website, in the last six months.
answer_text: 231 publications have been archived from the Department’s website in the past six months. This included one piece of research, ‘Post-primary school experiences of 16-21 year old people who are Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual and/or Transgender’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) why research entitled Post-Primary School Experiences of 16-21 year old people who are Lesbian, Gay, Bisexual and or Transgender, was removed from the his Department's website; and (ii) who made the request for it to be removed.
answer_text: To ensure information published on the DE website remains clear, accurate and relevant, pages may be reviewed and updated or removed at any time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when RAISE projects can expect to receive relevant funding.
answer_text: The first call for Locality Led Projects launched on 5 September and will close on 30 September 2025. Each project proposal will be assessed, using the scoring guidance published on my department’s website, by a panel which will include officials from the Department of Education and at least one external member with experience of education or locality-based programmes.Successful organisations will be notified after the assessment process has completed, with Letters of Offer issuing thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how often the enhanced Access NI certificates for taxi drivers who deliver home to school transport services on behalf of the Education Authority are revalidated.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that Enhanced Access NI clearance is currently renewed every 14 months for taxi drivers who deliver home to school transport services on behalf of the Education Authority

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether there are taxi drivers who are currently delivering home to school transport services on behalf of the Education Authority, while still awaiting an enhanced Access NI certificate.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that all taxi drivers who deliver home to school transport must have Enhanced Access NI clearance in place prior to being deployed on a service and the EA will not award a contract to a private operator if this is not in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when statutory guidance will be introduced on children missing in education.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any engagement he has had with producers on the development of a Deposit Return Scheme.
answer_text: There has been extensive engagement with stakeholders throughout the implementation of the Deposit Return Scheme for Northern Ireland.  This engagement has included key producers, retailers, wholesalers, hospitality and material manufacturers.Following the appointment of the UKDMO in May 2025 to deliver the DRS scheme, my officials, alongside Defra and the Scottish Government, have continued engaging with representative bodies with further stakeholder events planned to take place in the coming weeks. UK DMO have also established both an Industry Advisory Group and Consumer Engagement Forum to engage with a broad spectrum of stakeholders providing an opportunity to discuss proposals and inform decision making on the implementation of DRS. Northern Ireland views are represented on both of these groups.My officials will continue to engage purposefully and collaboratively with stakeholders, colleagues across the three nations and UK DMO to meet our collective and individual ambitions in progressing the implementation of DRS ahead of launching the scheme in October 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients removed from waiting lists in the last 12 months due to changes in treatment criteria of his Department or the Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The criteria for restricted procedures are monitored via the implementation of the Effective Use of Resources (EUR) policy. Publication of the updated EUR policy in 2023 coincided with a major programme of digitisation of patient records across all HSC Trusts namely, the Encompass Programme. As a result, the rollout of Encompass has temporarily limited the development of detailed reporting capabilities for restricted procedures, including data on patients removed from waiting lists because they no longer meet the criteria set out in the EUR policy.My officials have confirmed that the most current data available relates to the 2023/24 period. This information has been noted in the recent report Performance of Restricted Procedures by Health Trusts published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO) on 8 October 2025. The Department of Health and five Health and Social Care Trusts acknowledge the recent NIAO report’s findings regarding gaps in monitoring, both within Trusts and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG). Efforts are currently focused on identifying effective ways to incorporate future monitoring into the new digital infrastructure.Over the next 12 months, the Department of Health and the five Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to strengthening the performance, governance, and oversight of the EUR policy and will take forward the necessary actions to support this.It is anticipated that, upon completion of this work, the information requested will be more readily accessible. In the meantime, the 2023/24 data remains the most up-to-date, as outlined in the NIAO report referenced above.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work they are doing to progress post-19 special educational needs provision.
answer_text: In June 2025, I outlined the findings of my Department’s review and set out my proposed programme of reform, subject to the necessary investment, to improve and expand the Department for the Economy funded pathways and support for learners with special educational needs.  It is a significant programme, involving many aspects of our provision and supports. Officials are currently scoping the timeline and resources required to bring forward our proposed reforms. Investment will be crucial to enable us to develop and deliver our reform programme. We have submitted a collaborative bid to the Transformation Fund, as well as preparing Departmental bids for the multi-year Spending Review. In June, I also stated my intention to write to Executive colleagues on a range of wider issues which the Economy review reported on, to ensure a cross government collective and coordinated approach to those issues. I have now progressed this, with an Executive paper discussed and my recommendations endorsed at an Executive meeting on Thursday 18th September. This collective approach is required across the Executive if we are to deliver on our shared commitment to ensure better support for young people with Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review of delegated nursing tasks in relation to direct payment carers will be published.
answer_text: My Department has engaged with stakeholders and families through the establishment of working groups and workshops, seeking to understand current practices and identify viable pathways to enable the safe and effective delegation of healthcare.  This approach seeks to ensure the provision of the right care, in the right place, at the right time, by the right person, enabling people to live as independently as possible, in line with governing guidelines and legislation.It is anticipated that the review and recommendations on the way forward will be presented to me by Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to update legislation requiring all children that are home educated to be registered with (i) the Education Authority; and (ii) his Department.
answer_text: The legislative position in Northern Ireland is that parents are responsible for ensuring their child receives an education, at school or otherwise. Parents may therefore choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school and they may choose to deregister their child from school with the intention of educating them at home. Whilst the Education Authority (EA) is aware of children who have attended and subsequently been deregistered from a school, there is no duty on parents who have never registered their child at a school to inform the Department of Education (DE) or the EA if they have chosen to educate their children at home. Thus, there is no complete record of children being electively home educated in Northern Ireland; this is also the case in other jurisdictions. Work is underway to examine processes and policies in other jurisdictions and to monitor any changes to legislation regarding the registration of home-educated children.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any work ongoing to understand what costs families are being asked to pay by schools for laptops, tablets and other technology.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), through C2K and the Education Information Solutions Programme (i.e. EdIS), provides and manages the critical technology infrastructure and core provision of IT equipment, including digital devices, across the school’s estate. The EA does not ask parents for any financial contribution for laptops, tablets or any other technology used in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how actions within the Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland are being tracked and reported on.
answer_text: Delivery of the actions within the Online Safety Strategy are monitored through a Cross-Departmental Implementation Group (CDIG). Each Department is responsible for reporting progress on their assigned actions to the CDIG. These updates are compiled into a comprehensive report, which is submitted to the Children and Young People’s Strategy Child Protection Group. This group is responsible for monitoring the overall progress and effectiveness of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he is taking to ensure that no pupils with special educational needs and disabilities are travelling in Education Authority provided transport with an escort whose Access NI clearance is (i) pending, (ii) incomplete, or (iii) rejected.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised me that all drivers and escorts who work on EA bus transport must have Enhanced Access NI clearance in place prior to taking up the role and this forms part of the pre-employment checks carried out during the recruitment of staff.This is also the case for drivers and escorts working on private hire taxi and bus services delivering school transport and the EA will not award a contract to a private operator if Enhanced Access NI clearance is not in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail why criteria for the assessment and treatment for vascular varicose veins does not include chronic pain.
answer_text: I am currently unable to provide the number of patients removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to changes to the criteria for assessment and treatment made in 2023.  This information is held at Trust level and has been requested from the services.In 2022, the Department of Health updated the Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy, following the review of the current evidence-based commissioning position across a broad range of procedures and treatments. The updated policy also takes account of emerging clinical evidence from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence and similar organisations. Subsequently, certain procedures, such as some vascular surgery, are only commissioned in Northern Ireland for patients who meet the criteria set out in the Effective Use of Resources Policy. This policy is designed to ensure that services provide the greatest clinical benefit and health gain for patients, while also maximising the effectiveness of public resources, including clinical staff and health service infrastructure.Further to the direction outlined in 2022, due to the current pressures on the Vascular Service across Northern Ireland, a decision was taken in 2023 that varicose vein assessment and treatment could only be provided to patients with severe varicose veins.  Patients would now be assessed and reviewed under these criteria.  New referrals, which do not meet these criteria, will not be offered an outpatient appointment.Patients already on the waiting list for a new outpatient appointment were revalidated, and if they did not meet the criteria were removed from the outpatient waiting list.  The patient was subsequently informed, with guidance on best medical management provided to all patients with varicose veins.  Patients can be re-referred to the service if the clinical condition has changed.The Northern Ireland Audit Office are currently undertaking a review of Performance of Restricted Procedures by Trusts, which my officials are engaged with. I recognise the disappointment for those patients who were removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to a change of criteria, after having being on the waiting list for years.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for vascular surgery are lengthy, and growing, for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need, and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the patients who were removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to a change of criteria, after having being on the waiting list for years.
answer_text: I am currently unable to provide the number of patients removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to changes to the criteria for assessment and treatment made in 2023.  This information is held at Trust level and has been requested from the services.In 2022, the Department of Health updated the Effective Use of Resources (EUR) Policy, following the review of the current evidence-based commissioning position across a broad range of procedures and treatments. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of a reasonable length of time for a bus journey time for home to school transport.
answer_text: The Department of Education’s Home to School Transport Policy does not set out a maximum journey time, rather it outlines factors the EA should take into account when determining if a transport service is suitable. These include duration of the journey, age of the pupil, early start or late drop off, distance from pupils’ home and legal vehicle capacity. The policy also states that in determining the most suitable methods of assisting pupils, the EA should have regard to the interests of efficiency and economy as set out in Article 44 of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. The EA is responsible for the delivery of school transport and it informs me that it plans routes and services to minimise journey times as much as possible. Some journeys can take longer than may be expected for reasons such as distance from school, the need for door-to-door assistance for some pupils who require additional support and the time required for pupils to board and disembark the vehicle safely. Transport assistance is provided for over 90k pupils each day and the EA must balance journey times for individual pupils with making best use of the available capacity on school transport services to ensure that those pupils who need transport to school are able to avail of it. Where it is unable to provide suitable transport which meets these aims, the EA may offer parents an allowance in lieu of transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to changes to the criteria for assessment and treatment made in 2023.
answer_text: I am currently unable to provide the number of patients removed from the vascular varicose vein outpatient waiting list due to changes to the criteria for assessment and treatment made in 2023.
