question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has discussed data sharing agreements for safeguarding with the Minister of Health, including access to the child health system to have an accurate number of children.
answer_text: While I have not had discussions with the Minister of Health directly about this, my officials have been engaging with colleagues within the Department of Health in respect of the ability to track across various data sets from the child health system to inform early planning in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to modernise and update the Employment of Children Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996.
answer_text: The Department of Health has not brought any proposed amendments of the Employment of Children Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996, to my attention for consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 30279/22-27, to outline the reasons why no actions have been taken, given the acknowledgment within the Attendance Guidance & Absence Recording By Schools – Circular 2024-13 that The Department of Education and the Education Authority are aware that schools may not always be completing these forms, despite it being a legal requirement to do so.
answer_text: It is the role of the Board of Governors is to ensure that the Principal keeps a General Register of the pupils at the school. Part of this role will be the completion and submission of completed SA1 forms when it is appropriate to remove a pupil from the school register.Current guidance for Boards of Governors in respect of the maintenance of a school register is available at the link below:Guidance on pupil registration and attendanceA pupil’s name may be removed from a school’s register only in the following circumstances:Reason for RemovalDate of RemovalConfirmation that pupil is registered at another school in Northern IrelandDay of last attendanceDeparture from Northern IrelandDay of last attendanceExpulsionDay of expulsionDeathDay of deathLeaves school after having completed his/her compulsory educationDay of last attendanceParent/carer advises that child is being removed under Regulation 6(2) of The Registration and Attendance of Pupils Regulations (NI) 1974 [No. 78], for example elective home education.Day of last attendance

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the reasons why the appointment of independent members to the Industrial Tribunals Panel has been significantly delayed.
answer_text: There was a delay in the recruitment process due to the limited availability of suitable interview panel members; however, all interviews have now been completed, and I expect to be in a position to announce appointments in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail when she will announce the appointment of independent members to the Industrial Tribunals Panel.
answer_text: I expect to be in a position to announce the appointment of new non-legal panel members to the Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Department of Education’s Parental School Admission Survey 2025 on attitudes to Integrated Education was sent to (i) the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education; or (ii) any other sector body representing integrated education.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what his Department is doing to address the impacts of the price increases of GLP-1 medication.
answer_text: Following a request from Eli Lilly reflecting changes in the commercial environment and to ensure continuity of supply for a product that has been subject to systemic long term supply constraints, a UK list price increase for tirzepatide (also known as Mounjaro®) has recently been approved by the Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC). DHSC has discretion to approve increased UK list prices where there is otherwise a risk of discontinuation, withdrawal or non-supply with negative patient impact and in response to global supply constraints.While pricing for patients accessing tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) privately is a matter for Eli Lilly and for private providers of weight loss services, I do acknowledge that the recent increase in the UK list price will be of concern to individuals in Northern Ireland currently accessing weight management services from private healthcare providers. Anyone receiving tirzepatide (Mounjaro®) privately should seek advice about their current treatment, and available options, from their private provider.I do recognise that innovative medicines for weight loss can be an important tool to help people with obesity to lose weight and reduce their risk of severe health complications, when used alongside support for changes to diet and physical activity, and the phased introduction of the Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS) from 2026 onwards will begin to provide for eligible HSC patients to access lifestyle support and obesity medication, if clinically appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether street lighting can be installed on Station Road, between Moira and Moira Train Station, to improve safety for those choosing to use the train and active travel.
answer_text: My department is considering potential future improvements to pedestrian and cycling infrastructure between Moira and the train station. While there are no plans to change the rural street lighting policy at this time, I am conscious that street lighting can contribute significantly to feelings of personal security and safety, particularly for women travelling at night. This is an important consideration when officials are developing proposals to improve pedestrian and cycling facilities to key destinations with the aim of encouraging modal shift. However, the desire for lighting must be balanced against environmental considerations, our desire to reduce the carbon footprint and electricity costs associated with our infrastructure, and limited budgets. Design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of pupils with special educational needs and disabilities who are currently experiencing contingency arrangements due to not having a full-time school place; (ii) a list of what those contingency arrangements are; and (iii) a breakdown of the number of pupils experiencing each contingency arrangement.
answer_text: As unavoidable construction works continue, 80 children across 17 schools require interim arrangements, with 62 children across 14 mainstream schools and 18 children across three special schools supported through bespoke contingency arrangements including alternative classroom provision, structured home visits, and dual day classes to minimise disruption to learning whilst vital accommodation enhancements  are completed.Whilst the Education Authority (EA) support schools in the identification of contingency based on their individual circumstances, school leaders are responsible for the delivery of each contingency in line with the annual phased induction and school calendar within their school communities. Therefore, EA would not retain a breakdown of the number of pupils experiencing each contingency arrangement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQO 2179/22-27; and (ii) AQW 26113/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to improve (i) cycle routes; (ii) greenways; and (ii) the active travel infrastructure in Lagan Valley.
answer_text: Future active travel routes within our towns and cities will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Officials are currently reviewing responses to the public consultation, which closed in February 2025, and I hope to publish the final plan later this year. When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for prioritising delivery of improved active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond. Delivery of greenways is generally council led with my Department funding up to 50% of the capital cost of delivering approved projects.  My officials engage regularly with councils on their greenway priorities and any other active travel related initiatives. My Department is also considering potential future improvements to pedestrian and cycling infrastructure between Moira and the train station. Design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any provision being made within the cervical screening programme for women who are unable to avail of regular screening.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency has advised where an individual has particular needs relating to cervical screening that cannot be met within the GP practice, the GP has the ability to refer that patient to a Trust gynaecology service where additional support and alternative options may be considered. This is managed at an individual patient level, by the practice nurse or GP, and would apply to all intimate examinations, not just screening.All women are offered cervical screening and colposcopy only after full informed consent has been obtained, and these women are treated with the utmost respect and sensitivity.I would encourage all eligible women to accept the invitation to screening, when invited and for women who require extra support, to contact their GP practice or clinic before making an appointment to discuss their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of incidents of schools not providing a correct code on attendance records, over the last five years.
answer_text: Neither the Department of Education, or the Education Authority holds a record of the number of incidents where a school is incorrectly utilising attendance or absence codes incorrectly.It is the requirement of each school to accurately code attendance in line with the current guidance, issued at the start of each academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what active travel improvements will be introduced between Moira and Moira Train Station, to improve foot and bicycle safety along the route.
answer_text: My department is currently considering potential improvements to pedestrian and cycling infrastructure between Moira and the train station. Design development is at an early stage and engagement with stakeholders and the public will commence once plans are suitably developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount (i) spent on actions within the Online Safety Strategy in each year since it commenced; and (ii) budgeted for the Strategy and Action Plan in the next two years.
answer_text: The Department of Health has sponsorship of the Executive’s ‘Keeping children and young people safe: an Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland.’ Although this is an Executive Strategy, no funding from the Executive Office was ever secured. My department has provided direct funding to the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) to support implementation of the Strategy. The funding breakdown is as follows:2022/23: £130,1872023/24: £83,494 (plus £16,680 additional spend by SBNI)2024/25: £101,200 (plus £21,000 additional spend by SBNI)2025/26: £101,200 (plus £20,304 additional spend by SBNI)2026/27 (forecast): £101,200The additional spend by SBNI is covered from within its existing budget allocation. Other government departments are responsible for funding their own actions under the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 29570/22-27, (i) for a further annual breakdown of the number of children reported missing in excess of 24 hours; and (ii) the longest period a child was missing.
answer_text: Missing > 24 hours20/21Missing > 24 hours21/22Missing > 24 hours22/23Missing > 24 hours23/24Missing > 24 hours24/2528 323237 25Pursuant to AQW 29570/22-27, the further annual breakdown of the total number of looked after children who have been reported missing in excess of 24 hours is detailed above. The table shows the total number of Looked After Children across the 5 HSCT’s who have been notified as being absent or missing from care settings in excess of 24 hours broken down over each of the last five years as requested in the previous AQW 29570/22-27.My Department receive untoward events notifications from the individual HSCT’s when a child goes missing from care in excess of 24 hours. The Trusts have an obligation to report these young people missing given their vulnerability and assessed risk. The data collated in respect of missing children displays a degree of regional variation across the HSC Trusts. However due to small numbers of individual children who have been reported missing in excess of 24 hours in some HSC Trust areas and taking into consideration that the release of these individual HSC Trust figures could potentially identify one or more children within this vulnerable group, my Department have provided an overall total of individual missing children for the five HSC Trusts.My Department in collaboration with the PSNI and HSCTs have developed new Regional Guidance ‘The Interface Protocol between HSC Trusts and PSNI’ (October 2023). This Guidance sets out a broad operational overview of interface arrangements between PSNI and the Residential Children’s Home sector including arrangements for reporting when a child or young person goes missing. The aim of this guidance is to promote an effective joint agency response to missing children.My Department do not hold data on how long a child is missing. This data would be held by the respective HSC Trust as the HSC Trust’s would hold statutory responsibility for each looked- after child who is reported missing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the Health and Social Care Trusts which offer psychological support pathways for bereaved parents following (a) miscarriage; (b) stillbirth; and (c) neonatal death, as part of perinatal community mental health services; and (ii) whether he will commit to introducing specialist care pathways for bereaved parents in every Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  (i) Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS UK, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.You may also be aware that the Department of Finance is introducing ‘The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill’ in the Assembly which includes provision for the introduction of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland.  This initiative, currently under public consultation, would enable a pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, to be recognised.  Departmental officials will continue to work closely with the Department of Finance, around the consultation process and development of the scheme. The Department of Health Northern Ireland and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. (ii) SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience will be integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.My

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions that he is taking in line with the recommendation within the Independent Review of Education to 'right size' the network of schools.
answer_text: There are several references in the Independent Review of Education (IRE) report to ‘right-sizing’ that are linked to the Panel’s recommendation to reconfigure the network of schools (recommendation 20).A network of viable and sustainable schools, of the right type, the right size, located in the right place, at the right time and with a focus on raising standards is critical for the future of our education system.The Independent Panel determined that Northern Ireland currently has too many non-viable small schools and too many small sixth forms.  To tackle this problem – which has both educational and financial dimensions – the Panel stated that we need a new approach to Area Planning.  This will require managing authorities and sectoral bodies to work together to create thriving, sustainable schools that support higher standards of education and promote inclusion for all children and young people.Change of the scale proposed by the Independent Panel will take time and require significant financial investment.  The Panel estimated capital costs at £1bn over a 10-year period.  That level of funding is not available at present, but I will continue to make the case to the Executive for additional funding to deliver on my priorities for Education.However, actions are and will continue to be taken to reconfigure the network of schools, in line with my Department’s priorities, through the Area Planning process.  These changes are detailed in the Strategic Area Plan (2022-27: Planning for Sustainable Provision) and the Education Authority’s published Operational Plans and are brought forward through the statutory Development Proposal process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how many incidents have been recorded of schools failing to correctly complete a S.A.1 form to de-register a pupil, in the last five years; and (ii) any communication sent to schools on this matter.
answer_text: Neither the Department for Education (DE) nor the Education Authority (EA) have records of schools failing to complete SA1 notifications. All schools receive notification from the Department on an annual basis of the requirement to complete SA1 forms when removing a pupil from the school register.The most recent guidance issued in August 2025 is available from the DE website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education why the Education Authority or his Department were not involved in the Interface Protocol for children reported missing, as developed between Health and Social Care Northern Ireland and the PSNI.
answer_text: The Protocol is set out in two main sections and is not inclusive of Children Missing in Education. The protocol focuses on Missing Children; and General Policing Response to Incidents involving Children in Residential Children’s Homes. The Protocol also complements existing guidance in relation to child protection, Protocol for Joint Investigation (2014), Planning for Looked After Children and Sexual Exploitation of Children and Young People.This protocol covers all children who go missing, and who give rise for concern to the PSNI and / or to Health & Social Care Trusts. This includes children in the community with family, children in need known to Trusts, and children in care.The Education Authority was consulted on the document as a member of the Safeguarding Board NI (SBNI) The ‘protocol’ makes a valuable contribution to Education based ‘Children Missing in Education’ policy development which will focus on early identification of children missing or educated in other provisions in advance of Trust or PSNI involvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action he is taking to rebuild relationships with the schools that do not believe they will receive the required special educational needs support from the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:SEN CapacityThe Education Authority (EA) recognises the concerns raised by some schools regarding the adequacy and consistency of special educational needs (SEN) support provided by the EA. In seeking to rebuild trust and strengthen collaborative relationships with schools across Northern Ireland the EA will continue to liaise directly with school Principals both individually and through established forums.A key focus is on improving transparency, communication, and partnership working between the EA and schools specifically with the aim of establishing Specialist provision classes. The EA has developed clear guidance and support mechanisms to help schools understand the process for establishing specialist provisions within mainstream settings. This includes detailed information on funding, accommodation and the types of professional support available, as outlined on the SEND Plan website Establishing Specialist Provisions in Mainstream Schools | SEND Plan.All schools establishing Specialist Provision classes have access to a wide ranging package of support including;Financial support whereby, in addition to the ongoing additional funding schools receive through the Common Funding Formula, the Education Authority funds all costs associated with staff working in the Specialist Provision classes and provides other financial benefits to schools including, initial furniture and equipment funding and initial resourcing funding.Accommodation improvements whereby the Education Authority develops a capital project to ensure the classrooms used for hosting Specialist Provision class are refurbished and enhanced to appropriate levels to allow the school to meet the needs of pupils placed in the Specialist Provision classes.Enhanced training and development programmes whereby the Education Authority provide integrated support for schools with Specialist provision classes to ensure that staff working in the classes are effectively supported in meeting the challenge of delivering high quality provision and educational experiences for our children and young people.SEND Statutory Services:Every school will have both a named Education Psychologist (EP) and SEN Link Officer.  The EP will meet with the SENCo alongside the Local IMPACT team link in the first term to ensure collaborative working and that the school is best supported to meet children’s needs.  In addition, the EA will continue in the next school year, to deliver training to all schools on the Graduated Response Framework for SEN.  As part of the EDiS programme, SEN Statutory Services are developing a digital case management system to ensure there is better communication with parents and with schools, and this is scheduled to be live from January 2026.  SEND TransformationIn relation to children and young people who are at Stages 1 and 2 of the SEND Code of Practice, the EA is also in the process of reforming its relevant support services and how they can

The report is available here. My officials will continue to monitor the EA’s implementation of the actions set out in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a regional policy and procedure for the repatriation of patients to another Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The HSC Protocol for the repatriation of patients between Trusts was updated in August 2024 to support the safe and timely repatriation of patients to their Trust of Residence following an inpatient episode of care in another Trust.  This includes hospital to hospital repatriations and hospital to community repatriations following emergency or elective care admissions.  It does not replace existing repatriation arrangements for pathways for patients receiving treatment in critical care, children’s services or mental health settings, or for patients requiring repatriation from outside of Northern Ireland.The protocol provides a clear standardised process that enables the coordination of patient flow between Trusts, with the aim for repatriation to be completed within 12 hours of medical acceptance of the receiving hospital.  This target is for conditions where there is no recognised standard for specialist repatriation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what involvement parents or carers have within proposed repatriation of sick babies and children.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department regularly engages with colleagues in other jurisdictions in respect of missing children; and (ii) what official arrangements are in place for this engagement to take place.
answer_text: (i) The Department of Health recognises the importance of effective cooperation and information sharing in cases involving missing children, particularly where there are concerns relating to safeguarding, health or cross-jurisdictional movement.My Department regularly engages with relevant counterparts in other jurisdictions, including other UK nations and the Republic of Ireland, through established safeguarding and child protection frameworks. These arrangements are guided by both statutory responsibilities and inter-agency protocols to ensure that the welfare and safety of children remains paramount. In situations where a child goes missing and there are implications for Health or Social Care, the Department works closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), Health and Social Care Trusts, and, when appropriate, international and national agencies such as Interpol, the UK National Crime Agency (NCA) and Tusla – the Child and Family Agency in the Republic of Ireland.Additionally, Northern Ireland is an active participant in cross-border and UK wide forums focused on child protection and safeguarding, which facilitate the sharing of best practices and the development of coordinated responses in complex cases.My Department remains committed to working collaboratively with all relevant agencies to protect children and ensure robust systems are in place for timely and effective responses when a child goes missing.(ii) A number of formal and operational arrangements are in place to facilitate engagement between the Department of Health in Northern Ireland and colleagues in other jurisdictions in respect of missing children. These include;Protocols and Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs):Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts operate under joint agency agreements with statutory partners including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and have established relationships with counterparts in the Republic of Ireland through cross-border safeguarding protocols.2.	North-South Child Protection Work:There is regular liaison with the Child and Family Agency (Tusla) in the Republic of Ireland, particularly in cases with cross-border elements. This includes cooperation through the North-South Child Protection Sub-Group, which supports joint working arrangements between both jurisdictions.3.	National Referral Mechanism (NRM) and UK-Wide Safeguarding Frameworks:Engagement occurs through participation in UK-wide safeguarding and child protection structures, such as the UK National Safeguarding Steering Group. These forums ensure consistent approaches to managing high-risk cases, including those involving missing or trafficked children.4.	All-Island Child Protection Initiatives:Northern Ireland is part of All-Island initiatives supported by bodies such as SPPG and the NSPCC, which promote shared learning and aligned protocols for dealing with vulnerable children, especially in the context of cross-border mobility.5.	Information Sharing Protocols:He

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has a repatriation policy for the proposed move of patients to other Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) whether the policy includes a clear process for the proposed move of sick babies and children.
answer_text: (i) While the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust does not explicitly have a publicly available "repatriation policy" document, a HSC wide protocol for the movement of patients to other Health and Social Care Trusts is in place.  Transfers are typically handled based on the individual needs of the patient and the capacity and services available in the receiving trust.(ii) There is a distinct section within the protocol for the repatriation of paediatric transfers.  This includes hospital to hospital repatriation as well as hospital to community repatriation.  Clinicians and service managers in the various Trusts work closely together to ensure that children and babies get the right care in the right place in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any contingency plans which are in place to ensure that children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs will still receive the education they have a right to, if they do not have a school place by September 2025.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:It is the EA aim to have all children who have a statement of SEN allocated a placement for September 2025.  Over the last five to ten working days the residual pressure for placements has reduced to around 50 children, and with continued work over the summer, it may be possible to reduce this to zero by the start of the next school year.  Finding the final places will be particularly challenging.Contingency arrangements are based on the needs of the pupils and EA will work closely with the impacted schools and families to implement contingencies to make sure there is no or minimal impact for our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the equality of provision of speech and language therapy input for children with special educational needs in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary, broken down by (a) mainstream schools; (b) specialist provision in mainstream schools; and (c) special schools.
answer_text: Children with SEN at primary and post-primary level can access Speech and Language Therapy (SLT) support across the education sectors as follows;Primary Mainstream – RISE NI Children in mainstream primary schools across Northern Ireland (NI) can access SLT support as part of the Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) teams. These teams deliver a trans-disciplinary, early intervention service that works directly within the school environment for children from preschool to the end of Key Stage 1 where there are two or more areas of need.  The service delivers input at a Universal, Targeted and Specialist level and is provided by a range of health professionals that includes Speech and Language Therapists (SLT/s), Occupational Therapists, Physiotherapists, Behaviour Therapists, Clinical Psychologists and Therapy Assistants. The RISE teams were established in 2007 as part of the Children and Young People’s Package to address barriers to learning.Mainstream - Core SLT Services – Children in mainstream primary schools who have one area of need or are in Key stage 2 (i.e. Primary 5-7) who cannot access the trans-disciplinary model of support offered by RISE NI can access input as deemed appropriate from Trust SLT services. The input provided and model of support is dependent on the CYP’s needs and how such needs impact on their speech, language and communication development including how the SLT needs affect their learning. These services tend to be provided in community clinics, the child’s home and/or other suitable community environment dependent on Trust’s arrangements. CYP with fluency difficulties who attend mainstream primary schools also access core Trust SLT services due to the research evidence that indicates the need to work closely with parents/main carers.Specialist Provisions in Mainstream - The SLT provision for CYP availing of Specialist Provision in Mainstream tends to be delivered by Trust core SLT services similar to the model offered for CYP with one area of need and CYP enrolled in Key Stage 2. Children referred to core SLT Services are triaged and may require a detailed assessment of their communication skills followed by appropriate intervention, if required dependent on their need. Intervention may take the form of blocks of individual or group therapy, treatment updates, home and school programmes and advice, support and training for those involved in the child’s care.  Onward referral, liaison with other professionals and report writing may also be required.   Special Schools – SLT services for CYP in Special schools may be delivered within the school environment, the CYP’s home and/or the clinic setting, depending on assessed needs, service models and demands on SLT services. The historic model of specific therapists being aligned to and embedded within specific special school has had to change due to the increasing numbers of CYP with Special Education Needs and the geographical spread of their educational placement

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many looked-after children have been reported missing in the last five years.
answer_text: The number of children who have been reported missing from Care 1st April 2020 - March 31st 2025.TrustMissing 20/21Missing 21/22Missing 22/23Missing 23/24Missing 24/25BHSCT164143215128198NHSCT725243012SEHSCT1913252210SHSCT111721185WHSCT58152571340Total259350342211265The above table shows the total number Looked After Children who have been notified as being absent or missing from care settings broken down, by Trust over each of the years specified in the.My Department receives untoward events notifications from the individual HSCTs when a child goes missing from care. The particular vulnerabilities of children who are reported as missing to potential Child Sexual Exploitation have been successively identified in the Marshall Inquiry 2014 followed by the Pinkerton Review 2015 and the Leonard Consultancy Audit 2020.The above table can be further broken down by the number of children reported missing in excess of 24 hours and the number of Looked After Children who have been notified as being absent or missing from care settings who were identified as being at risk of Child Sexual Exploitation. The Trusts have an obligation to report these young people missing given their vulnerability and assessed risk.In collaboration with the PSNI and Trusts, my Department has developed new Regional Guidance ‘The Interface Protocol between HSC Trusts and PSNI’ (October 2023). This Guidance sets out a broad operational overview of interface arrangements between PSNI and the Residential Children’s Home sector including arrangements for reporting when a child or young person goes missing. The aim of this guidance is to promote an effective joint agency response to missing children.The data collated in respect of missing young people displays a degree of Regional variation with BHSCT reporting significantly higher numbers of children missing including children at risk of CSE on a consistent basis over time when compared with other Trusts. My Department is working in collaboration with the Trusts to further interrogate this pattern and to promote greater consistency across the Region. This variation may in part be associated with operational cultures within the HSCTs, however distinctions between urban and rural contexts may be influencing this metric. These patterns may also be impacted by annual trends associated with significant public holidays such as 12th July and with periods of civil unrest.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of whether there are any legislative gaps with regards to children missing in education.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing potential legislative gaps and working to develop comprehensive departmental guidance on the issue of Children Missing in Education (CME). Addressing CME effectively requires a coordinated, multi-agency approach. To that end, my officials continue to engage closely with colleagues in the Department of Health and the Education Authority (EA) to explore opportunities for improved information sharing and collaborative action in this policy area.Work is underway to examine the processes and policies in place across other jurisdictions, with a view to identifying best practice and informing local approaches. In parallel, consideration is being given to the range of data sources that may be required to support a more effective response to CME. The cross-departmental working group leading this work is scheduled to meet again ahead of the commencement of the 2025/2026 academic year.Actions taken by the Education Welfare Service (EWS), which has operational responsibility for CME in Northern Ireland, are outlined below. This includes protocols where a case involving a child missing in education requires engagement with colleagues in other jurisdictions. CME ProtocolsThe EA’s EWS will conduct an internal check of the CAPITA database, which hosts the School Information Management System, to see if there any records of the child.If there is a recent school record, the school will be contacted via telephone to confirm that the child is attending the educational setting and their start date.If there is no current record and if contact numbers or email addresses have been given for the parents, contact will be made to find out the current situation regarding a school placement.If only an address has been given, local schools will be contacted to see if an application has been made to send the child there.If there is no record, a tracer will be sent out to all Northern Ireland schools to ascertain school registration.The EWS also maintains a working relationship with C2k, which has access to attendance data across all schools. Through this system, C2k can verify whether a child is registered and attending any school. This functionality is particularly valuable during the summer holiday period, when direct school contact may not be possible.If there is no positive response but an address has been given, this will be passed onto EWS Management, and a local officer will conduct a home visit. If the EWS has been informed that a child is in a particular area of Northern Ireland and they cannot be located, the EWS will contact the local Gateway Team in that area to confirm if the child is registered with a local doctor’s surgery.Following completion of these steps, the EA will notify the ‘referrer’ of the outcome. The EA maintains an internal database of all CME requests.The Department of Education does not hold information in relation to the number of children currently missing in education in Northern Ireland. However, my Departm

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the working group which was set up in November 2024 to scope out the requirements needed to address children missing in education.
answer_text: Work has commenced on looking at the processes and policies in place in other jurisdictions as well as considering the range of data sources that may be required.  The working group is scheduled to have its next meeting in advance of the commencement of the 2025/2026 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how many children are currently not attending school and have not been de-registered from education, therefore are children missing in education.
answer_text: The department receives monthly management information from the Education Authority on attendance.  This information is cumulative, it counts information throughout the year and therefore cannot identify pupils who are currently absent from school. In addition, there are many reasons for a pupil to be absent from school and the pupil level information received does not provide the reasons for absence, only counts of present and absence half day sessions for each pupil.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to confirm that if a child has never attended school, there is no legal requirement on parents to make any contact with the (i) Education Authority, or (ii) Department of Education.
answer_text: There is no duty on parents to register a child educated outside of the formal school system with the Education Authority or my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown the number of children that have been de-registered from education for the purposes of (i) Elective Home Education; and (ii) other reasons, over the last five years.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing potential legislative gaps and working to develop comprehensive departmental guidance on the issue of Children Missing in Education (CME). Addressing CME effectively requires a coordinated, multi-agency approach. To that end, my officials continue to engage closely with colleagues in the Department of Health and the Education Authority (EA) to explore opportunities for improved information sharing and collaborative action in this policy area.Work is underway to examine the processes and policies in place across other jurisdictions, with a view to identifying best practice and informing local approaches. In parallel, consideration is being given to the range of data sources that may be required to support a more effective response to CME. The cross-departmental working group leading this work is scheduled to meet again ahead of the commencement of the 2025/2026 academic year.Actions taken by the Education Welfare Service (EWS), which has operational responsibility for CME in Northern Ireland, are outlined below. This includes protocols where a case involving a child missing in education requires engagement with colleagues in other jurisdictions. CME ProtocolsThe EA’s EWS will conduct an internal check of the CAPITA database, which hosts the School Information Management System, to see if there any records of the child.If there is a recent school record, the school will be contacted via telephone to confirm that the child is attending the educational setting and their start date.If there is no current record and if contact numbers or email addresses have been given for the parents, contact will be made to find out the current situation regarding a school placement.If only an address has been given, local schools will be contacted to see if an application has been made to send the child there.If there is no record, a tracer will be sent out to all Northern Ireland schools to ascertain school registration.The EWS also maintains a working relationship with C2k, which has access to attendance data across all schools. Through this system, C2k can verify whether a child is registered and attending any school. This functionality is particularly valuable during the summer holiday period, when direct school contact may not be possible.If there is no positive response but an address has been given, this will be passed onto EWS Management, and a local officer will conduct a home visit. If the EWS has been informed that a child is in a particular area of Northern Ireland and they cannot be located, the EWS will contact the local Gateway Team in that area to confirm if the child is registered with a local doctor’s surgery.Following completion of these steps, the EA will notify the ‘referrer’ of the outcome. The EA maintains an internal database of all CME requests.The Department of Education does not hold information in relation to the number of children currently missing in education in Northern Ireland. However, my Departm

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the current situation for school places for children with special educational needs ahead of the next academic year, given that over 160 children are currently without a place.
answer_text: The current situation for school places for children with special educational needs remains a top priority for my department and the EA and we will continue to work collaboratively over the summer to ensure that every child has a school place for September. It is critical that the annual cycle of emergency planning comes to an end and that a more sustainable approach is taken forward over future years.The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Since September 2024, EA has engaged extensively with schools not yet offering Specialist Provision and remain committed to working in partnership with all schools to find sustainable solutions.  To address the pressures, and working collaboratively with a number of schools, in preparation for September 2025, EA has already created more than 150 additional classes, for approximately 1,350 pupils, across 123 schools.  In early June the Minister wrote directly to 59 schools, encouraging them to engage with EA on this matter.  EA followed this up throughout June and recently wrote to a number of schools in particular areas of pressure.  Unfortunately, many of these schools have still not engaged with EA in the way that we would want.  Despite that, upon hearing of the difficulty of the position, a number of those schools that have previously worked with us to create Specialist Provision have, again, stepped forward to help.The consequence of this is that the residual pressure for placements has reduced to around 50 children, and we feel that with continued work over the summer, it may be possible to reduce this to zero by the start of the next school year.  However, finding the final places will be particularly challenging, and consequently some parents may not receive notification of their child's school placement until late August.  We recognise that this delay may lead to understandable concern.  Furthermore, accommodating the remaining pupils could require some children to travel greater distances than would be the case if a local solution was available.  EA remains committed to minimising these impacts wherever possible.Despite this reduction in the number of children remaining unplaced, we recognise that, for those children still without a place, it remains a frustrating and worrying time.  Ensuring every child has appropriate educational support remains EA’s highest priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether proposals for the draft Climate Action Plan 2023-2027 are sufficient for reducing emissions in line with the first carbon budget.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has to increase the funding available to (i) the Intercultural Education Service; and (ii) the Addressing Bullying In Schools team. following the recent racially-motivated disorder.
answer_text: The Intercultural Education Service (IES) is a mainstreamed service, the cost of which is met from the Education Authority’s (EA) annual block grant.  The allocation and management of the funding from the annual block allocation made available for the operation of IES is therefore a matter for the EA.  As the Department is facing a significant budgetary overcommitment in the current financial year, there is no scope for increasing the earmarked budget for ABSIT at this time.  The level of Departmental funding for ABSIT in future financial years will be considered within the context of the Department’s overall budgetary settlement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline whether his Department holds any data on the number of (i) sectarian; and (ii) racially motivated, bullying incidents in schools.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act) requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation and their outcome, however schools are not required to share this information with the Department. The Act assists schools in identifying the motivations for bullying type behaviour and putting in place suitable interventions to address this behaviour. Capturing the motivation for bullying type behaviour will help a school identify any specific areas of concern, such as sectarian or racially motived bullying type behaviour. This will enable the school to identify when they may benefit from specialist expertise and advice.While the Department does not hold this level of information, the Education Authority’s (EA) Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) holds records of the cases referred to them.In 2024/25, ABSIT dealt with 327 cases of bullying type behaviour. In 41% of cases, the referrer identified that they did not know the motivation. When the referral was able to identify a specific potential motivation, 2 cases (0.6%) were identified as being motivated by religion/sectarianism, and 23 (7%) were identified as being motivated by race.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he is considering to (i) understand the prevalence of bullying in our schools; and (ii) reduce the number and impact of bullying incidents.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation, methods used and their outcome. Officials are progressing work on a number of fronts with a view to ensuring the Department has access to comprehensive data to better understand the frequency, motivations and methods of bullying type behaviour.The Department has included a range of questions in relation to bullying type behaviour for the first time in the 2024 School Omnibus Survey by including questions in relation to bullying type behaviour motivations.  The results provide some insight into the prevalence, motivations and methods of bullying type behaviour in our schools.  Officials have also engaged with the ETI to ensure the inclusion of questions as part of a wider ETI pilot to help determine how schools are fulfilling their statutory obligations in relation to implementing the Act.Schools are presently required to complete a Bullying Concern Assessment Form (BCAF) for each incident of bullying type behaviour.  The BCAF records statutorily required data, however presently this data cannot be collated or anonymised.  The BCAF is being digitised as part of the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) digital change programme.  The Department is exploring with the EA the potential for accessing anonymised regional data resulting from the digitisation of the BCAF.The Department has worked closely with the EA’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) who provides the Department with termly reports in relation to the referrals they deal with, the training they undertake and the associated outcomes for both. ABSIT provides advice and support to schools on concerns of bullying type behaviour and, following a schools collaboration programme, has co-developed three tiers of updated training and resources aimed at effectively preventing and responding to reported concerns in line with legislation and guidance.Tier 1 Training: Online session for all staff working in schools.Tier 2: Leadership training and support for school-identified staff.Tier 3: School based training-Targeted advice for key staff working with complex cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions (i) the Education Authority; and (ii) his Department are taking following the recent racially-motivated violent disorder.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) activated its Major Emergency Plan arrangements in response to the impact of Civil Disorder on education settings and youth provision. This has provided a coordinated response across all required directorates.Throughout this period of unrest, the EA has engaged with the schools in the Ballymena area and wider school community. The EA has fully participated in the multi-agency coordination structures led by the PSNI to represent schools and youth provision.The EA has supported schools through direct engagement, holding joint Principal engagement meetings and the ongoing provision of information and guidance. This process has been led through the Intercultural Education Service (IES) and Education Welfare Service providing support for Newcomer families to access support and supporting schools with safeguarding and welfare concerns.The EA Youth Services have been embedded within local communities to provide a place of shelter and youth diversionary activities.The EA Pupil Emotional Health and Wellbeing Services provided schools with wellbeing resources and information to support children and young people impacted by these events. They also contacted school directly as required to provide advice and support. The IES is providing advice, guidance and support to families and schools around effective responses to disorder and disruption including assisting families with referrals to family support, free interpreting and relocation to other schools, if requested.In conjunction with the IES, my officials continue to liaise with schools in the local area to offer support to those impacted by the unrest due to Roma children and other Newcomer children being either absent from school or worried about their safety in attending school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline when she will present her paper on a legal right for young people with special educational needs to an appropriate pathway, to the Executive.
answer_text: I recently wrote to my Executive Colleagues on a range of issues which were raised during our review of post-school education, skills and training provision, and legislative protections, for young people with special educational needs but are beyond my Departmental remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a list of the (i) buildings; and (ii) land owned, by the Education Authority which have been deemed as surplus, for this year and next year.
answer_text: The buildings deemed surplus and for disposal by the Education Authority (EA) are:Former Drumsallen PS – For disposal 25/26Former Dunmullen PS – Planning to revert 25/26Former Lisfearty PS – For disposal 25/26Former Strabane GS – For disposal 25/26Former Glencairn YC site – For disposal 26/27Former Ravarnet PS – For disposal 26/27The Lands deemed surplus and for disposal by the EA are:Former Ballykeel Playing Fields – For disposal 25/26Former Castle Gardens PS – For disposal 25/26Former Parkhall Integrated College site – Disposal paused to consider SEN provisionFormer Forthriver PS – For disposal 26/27

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider making chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy available through the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to get reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  The Scheme, launched 2 June 2025, applies the following strict eligibility criteria:•	Patients who are ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland; And •	Patients who are on a treatment or surgical waiting list within HSC services in Northern Ireland for 2 years or more.Unfortunately, Car-T is a very specialist service not available in NI currently and therefore unavailable through the waiting list scheme. That said, NI patients do have access to CAR-T treatment, on an equitable basis to patients in GB, through extra contractual referral arrangements with NHS providers. This is managed through SPPG Hospital Care Directorate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the research into the business support needs of the early years and childcare sector will be published.
answer_text: The cross-departmental Steering Group for the research project met on 10th June to discuss the draft final report. Departmental officials are in the process of briefing me on the outcome of that meeting. I will shortly be writing to the Minister of Education to apprise him of the research findings and to provide advice regarding implementation of the recommendations. The report will be published on the departmental website once these steps have been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what she is doing to ensure students at higher and further education colleges have access to affordable childcare.
answer_text: I recognise the rising cost of childcare and the impact this has on parents and families who are already struggling with the cost of living. My Department provides a means-tested childcare grant to help towards the costs of childcare for eligible full-time undergraduate students. My Department is also committed to supporting Further Education (FE) students access affordable childcare by providing a range of student support packages which assist FE students with the cost of childcare.  FE students can claim for assistance with childcare costs through the FE Grant, Hardship Fund and Care to Learn Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the recent research paper by Stranmillis University College and Anti-Bullying Alliance 'All Together? The Nature and Extent of Sectarian Bullying in Northern Ireland'.
answer_text: Bullying type behaviour in our schools, for whatever reason and in whatever form, is unacceptable. My Department is committed to tackling bullying in schools and supporting all of our pupils.Capturing the potential motivation for reported bullying type behaviour is important to enable identification of school specific issues, patterns and trends, such as sectarian bullying type behaviour. In this data informed context, members of staff are better placed to address need appropriately with tailored pastoral, safeguarding and curricular interventions.With that in mind, I welcome the 'All Together? The Nature and Extent of Sectarian Bullying in Northern Ireland' research paper. My Department will consider fully the findings and recommendations of the paper, however, it should be noted that a number of measures are already in place to address some of the recommendations.The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), requires schools to record incidents of bullying type behaviour, their motivation, methods used and their outcome. The legislation provides guidance on exemplar motivations to inform the school assessment and recording process. This list reflects Section 75 groups including those who may be targeted due to their religious belief and political opinion. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority was established in September 2022 and provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT has a focus on preventative education to embed cultures that eradicate bullying. The team also provides children, young people and parents/carers, with support to deal with specific incidents of bullying. Furthermore, ABSIT has delivered professional learning to student teachers in Stranmillis University College.Established procedures are currently in place within schools to address incidents of bullying. Although there is no dedicated process specifically for managing instances of sectarian bullying type behaviour, such incidents are encompassed within the scope of the existing procedures and would be addressed accordingly.
