The data presented reflects the number of students enrolled in ITE programmes and may differ from the number of places originally allocated due to factors such as applicant demand, institutional capacity and recruitment outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how his Department determines the number of places allocated to Initial Teacher Education providers each year.
answer_text: The Department of Education determines the number of places allocated to Initial Teacher Education (ITE) annually through a process that combines statistical modelling with strategic workforce planning. Central to this is the Teacher Demand Model (TDM) which projects future teacher vacancies in grant-aided primary and post-primary schools and estimates the required ITE intake to meet this projected demand.While the TDM provides a  key evidence base, the final intake figures approved by the Department reflect a range of considerations, including school recruitment challenges, the Department’s statutory duty to support Irish-medium Education, migration trends (both inward and outward), the need to maintain the viability of ITE courses and the proportion of ITE graduates who do not enter the teaching profession. This approach ensures that the system remains responsive and adaptable to both current and anticipated needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the work to increase the provision of children's residential respite care.

To ask the Minister of Education, further to the comment he made on BBC Radio Ulster on May 27th in the context of upskirting, to detail: (i) when the smartphone ban in schools will come into place; and (ii) if there will be consultation with schools on how it will be implemented.
answer_text: Current guidance for schools on the use of personal mobile phones is contained in Circular 2024/14.Many primary schools have taken steps to ban the use of mobile phones in schools and a significant number of post-primary schools have also significantly restricted their use during the school day. Circular 2024/14 endorses this approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) what information is sought from; and (ii) what questions are asked of, medical practitioners by the Education Authority in order to determine the transport needs for children with a statement of special educational needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that when carrying out a statutory assessment of educational needs, under The Education (Special Educational Needs) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 (regulations 6 and 8), the EA seeks advice from the health and social services authority. This advice contains educational, medical, psychological or other features of the nature of the child or young person’s needs (in relation to the advice sought) which appear relevant to their educational needs. In providing medical advice, recommendations about transport are included only where this is evident that transport will be required i.e. that there is a clear medical need that the child will require assistance with transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to confirm if the Education Authority are responsible for transporting meals to pre-schools that do not have the facilities to supply meals on-site, or if external contractors are used.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that it is unable to provide a breakdown of transport costs for school meals to pre-schools as it is unable to disaggregate costs for pre-schools when vehicles are transporting meals to other school settings as well as to pre-schools. The EA is responsible for the provision of school meals for pupils in funded pre-school education provision. Where meals are not produced on-site, local arrangements will be implemented based on the circumstances of each individual setting. This may involve transportation of meals by the EA or an external provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount spent on transporting school meals to pre-schools that do not have the facilities to supply meals on-site, over the last 3 years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that it is unable to provide a breakdown of transport costs for school meals to pre-schools as it is unable to disaggregate costs for pre-schools when vehicles are transporting meals to other school settings as well as to pre-schools. The EA is responsible for the provision of school meals for pupils in funded pre-school education provision. Where meals are not produced on-site, local arrangements will be implemented based on the circumstances of each individual setting. This may involve transportation of meals by the EA or an external provider.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the sites identified for a potential park and ride facility in Dromore.
answer_text: The Department considered a number of sites around Dromore, and in conjunction with Translink we have identified a preferred Park and Ride site off the Banbridge Road at Quillyburn Businss Park. This would subject to land being made available by the Education Authority as part of their plans for a proposed new school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the development of the Dromore Central Primary School site.
answer_text: The former site of Dromore Central Primary School continues to be under consideration for special educational need (SEN) provision. As detailed in my response to AQW 21600/22-27, initial technical assessments have been carried out and draft plans developed for extensive refurbishment works to the building. The former Dromore Central Primary School site is one of a number of options being explored as locations for a potential new school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the number of requests which have come through the Education Authority's Special Educational Needs Digital Request for Involvement Portal, since it opened.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Over 3,100 requests for EA involvement have been received through the digital Request for Involvement (RfI) form since the portal opened on 30 January 2025. This includes requests for involvement from existing Special Educational Needs and Disability Pupil Support Services and the Educational Psychology Service. It should be noted that previous referral routes remained open in parallel to the digital RfI form across February, March and April 2025 and these constitute additional referrals on top of the 3,100.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the Education Authority's Special Educational Needs Digital Request for Involvement Portal is currently closed to new referrals.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that: The Request for Involvement Portal closed for new requests at 4pm on 16 May 2025 and will re-open in Term 1/September 2025. The portal was closed to allow support packages for referred children and young people to be completed by the end of June 2025. This will also facilitate transitioning the system architecture from the current Special Educational Needs and Disability service destinations to the 28 new Local IMPACT Team destinations for September 2025. Those requests where support has been recommended but could not commence before the end of June will be supported in the next academic year.In the case of critical situations, Special Educational Needs Coordinators and schools have been provided with an alternative contact method to access support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail an update on the building of new special schools.
answer_text: Departmental officials have been working alongside Education Authority Area Planning, Children and Young People's Services and Capital colleagues on the plans for new build special schools.  I hope to be in a position to make an announcement early in the 2025/26 academic year on progress, to coincide with the publication of the Plans of Action for all special schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Special Educational Needs Capital Investment Programme.
answer_text: Almost £49m was invested in my SEN capital programme in 2024/25 and I have allocated a further £70m in 2025/26.  Annual Maintenance & Equipment ProgrammeCirca £3m in furniture and equipment grants was provided to special schools and mainstream schools with new specialist provision classes.   A programme of planned and continuous maintenance was also delivered across the special school estate, including an ambitious £2m programme of works to facilitate and improve access to the outdoors including landscaping and new pathways, play and sensory areas.Specialist ProvisionOver 1,450 new places in both special and mainstream schools were created by the Education Authority for September 2024, with building works delivered to establish 114 new specialist provision in mainstream classes in 110 primary and post-primary schools and an additional 67 classrooms created in special schools across Northern Ireland.  My officials continue to work at pace with Education Authority colleagues on delivering capital solutions to enable more new specialist provisions to open in September 2025. School Enhancement Programme The business cases were approved for the school enhancement projects (SEPs) at Thornfield House, Clifton School, Hill Croft School and Riverside School and in May 2025 construction commenced on the extension to Kilronan School.I set aside the funding thresholds for SEPs in special schools and as a result, seven of the projects have now been expanded in scope.  New Special School Provision The business case for the first of potentially four new special schools in Belfast is well advanced and it is currently anticipated that it will be submitted to my Department for approval in July 2025. The long-term capital strategy for the special school estate is also well advanced, this includes individual Plans of Action for each special school and proposals for large scale extensions, refurbishments and new school builds.  I hope to be in a position to provide a more full update on this in September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 26329/22-27; and (ii) AQW 26332/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the BMC Public Health research report entitled, Sport is not a luxury: the perceived impact of the cost-of-living crisis on sport and physical activity opportunities for children and adolescents in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail his assessment of the equality of provision of speech and language therapy input for children with special educational needs in (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary, in (a) Mainstream Schools; (b) Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools; and (c) Special Schools.
answer_text: The provision of speech and language therapy is a matter for the Minister of Health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 24057/22-27, when the needs assessment to scope the healthcare needs of children attending mainstream and special education schools will be (i) completed; and (ii) published.
answer_text: I anticipate that the Public Health Agency will complete and submit its comprehensive needs assessment to scope the healthcare needs of children attending special education schools to me by the end of June 2025. The need to collate and analyse significant additional data informing this work has led to a short delay to the planned completion of this report.  On receipt of the completed report, my officials will work to identify solutions to address the identified specific health challenges faced by this population, evaluate the complexity of their needs, and determine the support systems necessary to ensure timely, child-specific care within the school environment.The report is expected to be largely of a clinical nature for consideration and analysis by health and education. It may also contain sensitive information. Given this, once I receive the report I will consider what would be appropriate to publish in terms of the report or associated recommendations as to the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline: (i) the details of the nine projects within the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda being funded from the Public Service Transformation Fund; and (ii) the progress of each.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 24357/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for the week ending 21 March 2025.All nine projects are at design stage, working in collaboration with delivery partners.  Projects will commence in 2025/26 in line with the bid made to the Public Sector Transformation Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education pursuant to AQW 25380/22-27, for an update on the number of appropriate school places that need to be found for children with a statement of Special Educational Needs for September 2025.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The pressure to secure placements for children and young people with statements of Special Educational Needs (SEN) remains challenging. Despite significant efforts to increase capacity across the school estate in advance of the new academic year, it is currently estimated that there is a critical capacity requirement for circa 330 children with SEN across all transition years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to list the actions which have been taken by the task and finish group set up to address the recommendations from the Education and Training Inspectorate's thematic evaluation of physical education provision in primary schools.
answer_text: The Strategic Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum is nearing completion, with the report expected to be published in the coming days.  The Review’s report will set out the core principles for curriculum re-design as well as detail any subject or phase specific issues that need to be addressed.  I have already signalled my intention to establish a Curriculum Taskforce to lead on this work.  Further detail on the Taskforce and programme of reform will be provided following consideration of the Review’s report.  The Education and Training Inspectorate's (ETI) thematic evaluation will inform this work.In the meantime, as a result of a Task and Finish Group, three primary schools have been identified as examples of effective Physical Education (PE) practice: St John the Baptist, Pond Park and Fourtowns.  My Department, in collaboration with the Education Authority (EA), has produced video interview clips of the PE co-ordinators from these schools that will soon be published on the EA’s Supporting Learning website.  These interviews provide guidance and examples of effective practice for schools and teachers seeking to enhance their delivery of PE, covering a number of the areas of development highlighted in the ETI report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has formally proposed actions to be included in the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy, to support those in training or further or higher education, with childcare costs.
answer_text: I am fully committed to working with the Minister for Education and his officials as they progress the development of an Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, including representation on a range of cross-departmental groups.		My officials are currently working with the Department for Education to develop and agree options for inclusion in the Strategy, with a focus on skills and qualifications for the workforce, as well as the need for support for the sector.		Regarding support for those in training or further or higher education. My department already provides; the FE Childcare Grant for FE students aged 20 and over who are in receipt of an FE grant; the Hardship Childcare Grant for eligible FE students aged 18 and over; the Care to Learn Scheme for eligible FE students aged 16-19; and the Childcare Grant for eligible full-time undergraduate higher education students. Support is provided towards the costs of registered or approved childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will publish data on the costs of formal childcare gathered through the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The administrative data for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme was published on 28 May 2025. This data covers the period from 1 September 2024 to 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has put forward formal proposals for what should be included in the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I am fully committed to working with the Minister for Education and his officials as they progress the development of an Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, including representation on a range of cross-departmental groups.		My officials are currently working with the Department for Education to develop and agree options for inclusion in the Strategy, with a focus on skills and qualifications for the workforce, as well as the need for support for the sector.		Regarding support for those in training or further or higher education. My department already provides; the FE Childcare Grant for FE students aged 20 and over who are in receipt of an FE grant; the Hardship Childcare Grant for eligible FE students aged 18 and over; the Care to Learn Scheme for eligible FE students aged 16-19; and the Childcare Grant for eligible full-time undergraduate higher education students. Support is provided towards the costs of registered or approved childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail: (i) when he last raised the need to increase tax-free childcare support with the UK Government; and (ii) the response received.
answer_text: I raised the need for increased support through the Tax-free Childcare Scheme with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury (CST) most recently in April along with other Executive specific issues to be considered as part of the second phase of the Spending Review.I am meeting the CST on 26 June 2025 at the next Finance: Interministerial Standing Committee which will provide an opportunity to raise this matter again if not addressed within the Spending Review announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the work being undertaken to improve water quality in the Lough Neagh catchment area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why informal childcare has not been separated from paid formal childcare in NISRA childcare survey tables, for example in the  'Average monthly payment for childcare by hours used' table, which were produced in conjunction with his Department.
answer_text: The NISRA childcare survey was designed to provide a representative overview of childcare usage and costs as experienced by households in Northern Ireland.  The focus was on capturing the overall financial impact on families, rather than isolating costs by provider type.  For this reason, some of the survey tables produced independently by NISRA as official statistics, such as ‘Average monthly payment for childcare by hours used', combine both formal and informal childcare to reflect the full range of arrangements being used by families. In relation to the request for a median daily rate, supplementary data has been published by NISRA on the Department of Education website: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-childcare-survey-results. This includes the median cost paid by parents for formal childcare, broken down by provider type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education if the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency will publish the median daily rate paid to formal childcare providers before subsidies.
answer_text: The NISRA childcare survey was designed to provide a representative overview of childcare usage and costs as experienced by households in Northern Ireland.  The focus was on capturing the overall financial impact on families, rather than isolating costs by provider type.  For this reason, some of the survey tables produced independently by NISRA as official statistics, such as ‘Average monthly payment for childcare by hours used', combine both formal and informal childcare to reflect the full range of arrangements being used by families. In relation to the request for a median daily rate, supplementary data has been published by NISRA on the Department of Education website: https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-childcare-survey-results. This includes the median cost paid by parents for formal childcare, broken down by provider type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the timetable for the establishment of a permanent Public Service Transformation Board.
answer_text: I expect to agree the terms of reference for the Public Sector Transformation Board with the Secretary of State in the coming weeks. I also expect that these will largely reflect those previously agreed for the interim Board.I also intend on supplementing the skills of the Public Sector Transformation Board through the inclusion of additional experts in the areas of ‘value for money’ and ‘data and science’. I will be engaging with Executive colleagues on these issues, prior to formally agreeing the terms of reference with the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the phased review of the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008.
answer_text: As you are aware I have recently asked officials to undertake a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of the Class C taxi licensing system. To help understand the views of the taxi industry, in relation to the Class C taxi licensing system, I have agreed to meet with a number of key stakeholders over the coming weeks. My Officials are also planning on gathering information from across all four taxi classes via survey sampling, to help inform policy development for this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will ensure that drainage systems and road surfaces will be upgraded and improved when public realm schemes are taking place in (i) Hillsborough; and (ii) Moira.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working in partnership with local councils and other stakeholders to support the delivery of high-quality public realm schemes including those in Royal Hillsborough and Moira.Where schemes impact on adopted roads, my officials engage closely during the planning and design stages to assess the condition of the carriageway and associated infrastructure, including drainage systems. Whilst efforts are made to align these improvement works, it is not always possible, particularly where funding constraints exist or where the road network does not meet the necessary condition thresholds. My Department continues to operate a rolling programme of maintenance works and seeks to coordinate wherever practical to support town centre regeneration and ensure long-term benefits for road users and pedestrians.My officials will continue to engage with their local government counterparts to identify opportunities for joint delivery and ensure that statutory responsibilities for the public road are upheld throughout the development and implementation of public realm schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any structures that will be in place to support collaboration and sharing of information between the Sensory Support Service and the new Local Impact Teams.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The EA’s Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation Team are nearing finalisation of the SEND Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) operating model ahead of its roll out in September 2025.  The EA’s Sensory Service will continue to be delivered in its current form (i.e. regionally) but will sit within the Regional Management Unit which will oversee the Local IMPACT Teams. As such collaboration and sharing of information will be delivered through a single management structure for both services. This will include the Sensory Service collaborating directly with the relevant Local IMPACT Team to co-ordinate blended support for a sensory-impaired child or young person who also has wider special educational needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress to create statutory guidance in relation to children missing education.
answer_text: 8. AQO 1966/22-27 			Question:To ask the Minister of Education for an update on progress to create statutory guidance in relation to children missing in education.(Michelle Guy, MLA – Lagan Valley)Answer: A DE working group was set up in November 2024 to scope out the requirements needed to address Children Missing Education in Northern Ireland. The objectives of the Working Group are:To analyse the existing legislation, current duties, obligations, and potential gaps. To produce a process map of the various scenarios.To establish the current and potential future operational support available.To outline what happens in other jurisdictions including how we link with them.To analyse the available data. (What do we know? What is missing?).To link with other Departments (e.g. DOH / TEO).To produce a report that sets out the current landscape and options on next steps.This work is progressing however, no decision has been taken to create statutory guidance in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the current Sensory Support Service will be reviewed.
answer_text: As per the answer provided to AQW 26516 the Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation Team is nearing finalisation of the SEND Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) operating model ahead of its roll out in September 2025.The EA’s Sensory Service will continue to be delivered in its current form (i.e. regionally) but will sit within the Regional Management Unit (RMU) which will oversee the Local IMPACT Teams. As such collaboration and sharing of information will be delivered through a single management structure for both services. This will include the Sensory Service collaborating directly with the relevant Local IMPACT Team to co-ordinate blended support for a sensory-impaired child or young person who also has wider special educational needs.The structure and function of the Local IMPACT Team network and RMU will be under continual review across its first phase of operation and this will include the positioning of the remaining regionalised services, such as the Sensory Service.It should also be noted that the EA’s Sensory Service underwent an internal audit in financial year 2024/2025 and the Opinion Rating was green.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Sensory Support Service has a published delivery and action plan.
answer_text: In accordance with Education Authority procedures, the Sensory Service maintains a Business Plan which is reviewed on a quarterly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when public realm schemes will be taken forward in (i) Hillsborough; and (ii) Moira.
answer_text: My Department works collaboratively with Councils to deliver Urban Regeneration projects, such as Public Realm schemes, in urban towns and city centres with a policy focus on urban settlements with a population of 5,000 or more. Hillsborough and Moira, each have populations of less than 5,000 residents.  Therefore, Public Realm schemes for those settlements would not fall under the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council to coordinate appropriate works in relation to drainage systems and road surfaces when public realm schemes are being delivered in (i) Hillsborough; and (ii) Moira.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working in partnership with local councils and other stakeholders to support the delivery of high-quality public realm schemes including those in Royal Hillsborough and Moira.Where schemes impact on adopted roads, my officials engage closely during the planning and design stages to assess the condition of the carriageway and associated infrastructure, including drainage systems. Whilst efforts are made to align these improvement works, it is not always possible, particularly where funding constraints exist or where the road network does not meet the necessary condition thresholds. My Department continues to operate a rolling programme of maintenance works and seeks to coordinate wherever practical to support town centre regeneration and ensure long-term benefits for road users and pedestrians.My officials will continue to engage with their local government counterparts to identify opportunities for joint delivery and ensure that statutory responsibilities for the public road are upheld throughout the development and implementation of public realm schemes.  My Department is also represented at the Destination Royal Hillsborough Programme Board.  The Public Realm scheme in Royal Hillsborough is one of the projects included in this programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of how appropriate the practice of seeking evidence from community paediatricians, rather than General Practitioners, in order to determine the transport needs for children with a statement of special educational need.
answer_text: The Education Authority seeks Medical Advice for the purposes of a statutory assessment of a child's special educational needs, as specified in The Education (SEN) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005 (regulations 6 and 8), which state that advice should include features which appear to be relevant to the child's educational needs and should be sought from the health and social services authority, who shall obtain the advice from a registered medical practitioner.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current arrangements and criteria for home to school transport for children with a Statement of Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: Where the Education Authority (EA) maintains a statement of Special Educational Needs under Article 16 of the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1996, the decisions about that pupil's placement and their entitlement to transport assistance are made by the EA. The EA Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS) assesses if a pupil with a statement of SEN has additional transport requirements and, if so, a referral is made to the EA transport service who work to put those arrangements in place. Where SARS has identified that a pupil has additional transport needs, the general criteria of the Department for Education (DE) Home to School Transport Policy may be set aside, however the EA must ensure that the placement and transport arrangements are compatible with the efficient use of resources. Transport assistance may not be available if parents choose to bypass the nearest suitable school identified by EA SARS.Where a pupil with a statement of SEN has no additional transport needs, an application for transport assistance must be made to the EA Transport Service in the regular manner and pupils are assessed against the criteria in DE's transport policy. Pupils may be eligible for transport assistance under the DE policy where there is no suitable school within statutory walking distance of their home, which is 2 miles in the case of a primary school pupil and 3 miles for post-primary pupils. A suitable school for a pupil with a statement of SEN is determined during the statutory assessment process and if parents choose to bypass the nearest suitable school identified by EA SARS, transport assistance may not be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will request that Health and Social Care Trusts develop a joint-regional plan for respite and residential care services.
answer_text: My Department’s strategic response to the increasing level of Children with Disability need across Northern Ireland has been set out through a regional Framework for Children with Disability. This has been partly progressed through the Regional Children’s Services Strategic Reform Programme, which my Department is leading on, and which was established in response to Professor Ray Jones’ Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services. The regional needs of Children with Disability are being considered across specific workstreams focusing on Residential Care (Workstream 3), Fostering (Workstream 4), and Children with Disability (Workstream 5). Focused Work in respect of the projected residential care need for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts, in addition to work regarding enhancing the Short Break provision (both residential & fostering) for Children with Disability across the five HSC Trusts is being completed through the Regional Social Care Reform Workstreams. This work remains ongoing, and it will form the basis for future commissioning of services and investment plans in the coming years. Within the Reform Programme, we are looking carefully at the models of residential care, short breaks and fostering for children which are working well, to see how they could be replicated and expanded across Northern Ireland. The Strategic Reform Programme aims to ensure that we have the right services to meet the holistic needs of young people (including those with disability), now and in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any changes that have been made to the pre-school Published Admission Number allocation criteria and process for both statutory and non-statutory providers in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The EA has advised that no changes have been made to the criteria and processes for allocating PANs to non-statutory providers over the past 5 years. Statutory pre-school settings are not allocated a PAN, but rather they are set an admissions and enrolment number via a legislative process as set out in the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail all buildings that have been fully assessed for their potential use for respite or residential care in the last year, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting time for children's autism assessments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As part of the Encompass roll-out, the South Eastern, Belfast and Northern Trusts have now gone live with a new electronic patient record system and their data is undergoing extensive validation. There is no validated information available from these Trusts at this time.SHSCT at 31/12/2024 there were 1,598 children awaiting an ASD assessment and WHSCT at 31/12/2024 there were 2,369 children awaiting an ASD assessmentWaiting lists for autism assessment remain a challenge. The Department acknowledges that this is not acceptable and can have a profound impact on mental health.Department officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency and Child Health to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway for children and young people that complies with NICE guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on actions to address blue-green algae at Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I am pleased to report that implementation of the actions in the Lough Neagh Action Plan is progressing well. Of the 37 actions contained within the Action Plan, 10 of these have been delivered and I am committed to driving forward the implementation of the remaining actions.This work includes the progress on the Sustainable use of  Livestock Slurry and Lough Neagh Blue Green Algae Small Business Research Initiatives, enhanced responses to reports of water pollution through an increase in Water Quality Inspectors and an increased focus on areas of highest risk, with DAERA officials undertaking increased waterway inspections within several Lough Neagh catchments of concern.I would stress that there are no quick fixes to the issues in Lough Neagh and it will take many years, if not decades, to see substantial recovery, such is the depth and complexity of the problem.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a review of the Online Safety Strategy 2020-2025 will take place.
answer_text: The Online Safety Strategy and an initial 3-year action plan were published in February 2021 however funding for implementation only commenced in 2022. Year 3 of the Strategy has therefore just ended, and we are in the process of drafting an updated action plan to cover the remainder of the period. As we approach the end of the 5-year Strategy, the Department will carry out a review to assess overall success and inform future strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of times the Safer Schools NI app has been downloaded; and (ii) the number of monthly users the app has had in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The department is advised that 922 schools have created a schools account on The Safer Schools Northern Ireland app with a total of 55,000 downloads (data provided at 8 April 2025).The app does not currently collect monthly active user data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department is using the Keeping Safe relationships and sexuality education resources to support the ongoing delivery of the Personal Development and Mutual Understanding curriculum.
answer_text: The delivery of the ‘Keeping Safe’ programme ceased from May 2023 and these resources are no longer available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of actions within the Online Safety Strategy 2020-2025 that have been achieved.
answer_text: The initial 3-year action plan of the Online Safety Strategy contained 21 actions. Of those 21 actions, 17 have been achieved. Actions 5, 15 and 16 are progressing but with a slight delay. They are reliant on the online safety training needs analysis report which is due in June 2025.Action 18 has not yet been achieved. Although all further educational colleges have online safety policies in place, there is not yet a sector-wide pilot programme developed. This action will be rolled over into the updated action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children that were not successful in getting an offer of a funded pre-school place for September 2025 in Admissions Stage 1.
answer_text: The EA has advised that at this stage in the pre-school admissions process, 97% target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and stage two remains ongoing at this time.  The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places available to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.I would encourage parents to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences as they have been invited to do in order to maximise the changes of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how teachers can access the Keeping Safe relationships and sexuality education resources.
answer_text: The delivery of the ‘Keeping Safe’ programme ceased from May 2023 and these resources are no longer available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any measures she will take to ensure that public realm schemes undertaken in Hillsborough and Moira will resurface roads and improve drainage at the same time.
answer_text: My Department is committed to working in partnership with local councils and other stakeholders to support the delivery of high-quality public realm schemes across the North.Where schemes impact on adopted roads, my officials engage closely during the planning and design stages to assess the condition of the carriageway and associated infrastructure, including drainage systems. Whilst efforts are made to align these improvement works, it is not always possible, particularly where funding constraints exist or where the road network does not meet the necessary condition thresholds. My Department continues to operate a rolling programme of maintenance works and seeks to coordinate wherever practical to support town centre regeneration and ensure long-term benefits for road users and pedestrians.My officials will continue to engage with their local government counterparts to identify opportunities for joint delivery and ensure that statutory responsibilities for the public road are upheld throughout the development and implementation of public realm schemes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the role that social media platforms can play in tackling hate and ending violence against women and girls.

To ask the Minister of Education whether he will ensure the Education Authority carries out a full review into why Primary 1 and pre-school admission notifications were sent to parents early in error.
answer_text: On 3 April 2025, pre-school and primary school admission notifications were released six days ahead of schedule due to a technical error during testing by service provider Version 1. The EA Connect team discovered the error shortly before 7am and immediately escalated it. An EA Incident Response Team was quickly established and a number of key actions were taken which included: Contacting all pre-schools and primary schools to notify them of early release of admission notifications and providing contact details for further support. Issuing a statement to key media outlets and across EA social media channels providing assurance to parents that the outcome of applications in any emails parent/carers received was correct; advising that parents/carers will be able to log on to EA Connect and accept/reject their child’s place, or list other preferences, from 12 noon on Monday 7 April; and providing contact details for further support. EA immediately set up a Helpdesk and additional resources were added to throughout the morning and Helpdesk hours were extended until 6 pm Ensuring all parents and carers received an email or were contacted in relation to the admissions outcome. Responding to a significant volume of calls and emails. In a statement Version 1 apologised for the error: “We would like to apologise to our valued customer, the Northern Ireland Education Authority, and parents and guardians of children applying for P1 or pre-school places for the 2025-26 academic year. Due to human error during testing of the EA Connect portal, early notifications were sent out today. We are actively investigating to understand how it happened. We recognise the additional pressure early notification has placed on school principals and staff, and we are committed to providing the necessary support to manage the situation.”  The EA also added its own apology to that already offered to parents by Version 1. Work is underway to determine how this happened and to ensure it cannot happen again.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the definition each Health and Social Care Trust has for a short break in terms of respite care.
answer_text: There are agreed short break definitions in terms of respite care within Children’s and Adult Services, which are utilised by all Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) across Northern Ireland. Adult Services: Short break care is defined as the provision of regular, occasional or emergency but time limited breaks to those who are caring for others. It is any activity or service of limited duration designed to provide a break for the persons cared for and their carer from the usual commitment. Such a break can be provided on a regular basis over a long term.Children’s Services: Short breaks aim to allow a child with a disability to have enjoyable planned experiences away from their primary carers and to create some personal space for carers. The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 [the Children Order] provides that children accommodated for a period of 24 hours or more outside their family home are deemed to be Children Looked After.  HSCTs utilise both statutory and independent sector providers to provide short breaks. Short break services can also be provided through domiciliary care support within people’s own homes. Direct Payments can also facilitate short breaks for individuals and their carers. Shared Lives is also an option for older people and for adults with a learning disability.  HSCTs also deliver short breaks (a series of short term-term placements with the same Foster Carer or in the same place) through Trust provision or in partnership with the community and voluntary sector.Provision of short break services is aligned to assessed needs and within the financial resources available to each Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a review is taking place of management culture and communication within Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to families seeking children's respite services.
answer_text: There is currently no regional review of management culture and communication within Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to families seeking children’s respite services. However, a Collective Leadership Approach would be the cultural approach adopted across the HSC Trusts.The Belfast HSC Trust has informed the Department that they are not aware of any review of management culture and communication within Health and Social Care Trusts, in relation to families seeking children's respite services.The Northern HSC Trust has stated that whilst there is currently no formal review in place, Children with Disability management are continuously trying to improve communications including:Consideration of effective engagement methods with parents/carers, and families who can advise on how to improve communications which will shape and inform an appropriate communication strategy. The South Eastern HSC Trust has advised that they have established a range of communication forums with those families seeking Short Breaks. The South Eastern HSC Trust is currently planning to review the management culture and communication across the breath of children’s services to inform a practice of continuous improvement.  The Southern HSC Trust has informed my Department that if a child has been assessed as requiring short break (respite) services - the Southern HSC Trust will seek to ensure that there is timely and transparent communication with the family in relation to the type of support proposed and when this will be available. Where there is a waiting list in operation - Residential and Fostering waiting lists are regularly reviewed at the overnight forum and the social worker will provide an update to families. In addition, the Southern HSC Trust stated that where there are issues/concerns identified regarding the Trust’s communication with families regarding short breaks via complaints formally or informally, management will be advised of same and will seek to clarify the current position for families and identify any learning which will improve communication processes between the Trust and families.   The Western Trust have not undertaken a formal review of management culture and communications with families seeking respite services. However, the Western Trust has stated that they are continuously trying to improve communication with families and hold regular planned forums with parents and carers to provide updates regarding the Trust’s position on residential Short Breaks.  The Western HSC Trust advise that parents were recently involved in the co-production of service developments in relation to the additional funding allocated.  The Service are also in the process of setting up an Involvement Working Group to include some parents who can advise on how to improve communications within the Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how each Health and Social Care Trust measures assessed need for children and families seeking children's respite services.
answer_text: It is important that HSC Trusts identify and assess the needs of vulnerable children to ensure that needs are appropriately met at an early stage. Across all HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland – the Understanding The Needs of Children In Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) assessment framework is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to measure assessed need for children and families – this includes assessed need in terms of short break / respite support. The UNOCINI is an inter-agency assessment model that was developed in Northern Ireland to:improve the quality of assessment within stakeholder agencies.assist in communicating the needs of children across agencies.avoid the escalation of children’s needs through early identification of need and effective intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the definition each Health and Social Care Trust has for children deemed on the edge of care.
answer_text: In June 2022 my Department and the Department of Education updated ‘A Life Deserved: ‘Caring for Children & Young People in Northern Ireland’. This strategy for Looked After Children outlines that by ‘edge of care’ we mean one of a number of things:Living in Families with intense needs and requiring intensive supports [on the edge of coming into care];Returning home from a period of care [on the edge of a [short] period in care];Leaving care to make the journey into adult life [on the edge of ageing out of care].It also applies to children and young people who have left care and are still in need of some support; including those who have been adopted; those who are living in a family under a Private Law Order; and those who are supported in independent living in early adulthood. Each Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they utilise this definition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children with disabilities who have Direct Payments listed on their assessed needs.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust has 319 children with disabilities availing of Direct Payments as an outcome of their assessed need. The Northern HSC Trust has 225 Direct Payments for children who are receiving services from the Children with Disability teams. There are 24 Direct Payments for children who are receiving services from the Family Support and Intervention teams.The South Eastern HSC Trust currently provides 254 families with Direct Payments to deliver a variety of supports to families with children with severe learning disabilities and or complex medical needs.The Southern HSC Trust Children with Disabilities Service currently has 439 children listed as receiving or commencing Direct Payments based on their assessed needs.The Western HSC Trust has 293 children with disabilities who have Direct Payments listed on their assessed needs, and are currently in receipt of same.  The Western HSC Trust advise that there may be additional children who are also assessed as needing Direct Payments, but who are currently moving through the administration process. Currently, the Western HSC Trust are unable to quantify this number without a manual trawl of cases, which is not possible within the timescales for response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of teachers that have taken leave due to stress-related illnesses; and (ii) the total number of sick days recorded due to stress-related illnesses, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Department’s Teachers’ Pay Team has provided the following figures;There have been 2,169 teachers who have been absent due to stress-related illness in the last 5 years.A total of 120,308 days absence due to stress-related illness in the last 5 years has been recorded.Please note;Figures do not include teachers from voluntary grammar schools.Figures are taken for the previous 5 financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils (i) suspended; and (ii) permanently excluded from (a) primary schools; and (b) post-primary schools, in the last five years.
answer_text: The number of pupils (i) suspended; and (ii) permanently excluded from (a) primary school; and (b) post-primary school the last five years for which data are available.Academic yearPrimary schoolsPost-primary schoolsNumber of pupils suspendedNumber of pupils expelledNumber of pupils suspendedNumber of pupils expelled2022/2320604904232021/2216603530202020/2111803371252019/2019703088112018/193170417030Source: Pupil suspensions and expulsions | Department of EducationNotes: Figures relate to pupils of statutory school age.Due to school closures related to Covid-19 in 2019/20 and 202/21 comparisons to other years should be treated with caution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether recommendations from the Education Training Inspectorate's 2012 - 2013 report entitled An evaluation of the provision to meet the needs of pupils with persistent and challenging behaviour in special schools, were implemented.
answer_text: This report and its recommendations were considered within the End-to-End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) which identified the need for investment in and building of a skilled and confident workforce with access to SEN focused resources and support to meet children’s needs.My SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, under the Right People pillar, includes a number of actions, and demonstrates my commitment to investing in the educational workforce ensuring they are supported, valued and empowered to build supportive learning environments for pupils with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of nursery schools that are currently in deficit are in receipt of both principal release funding and small school support.
answer_text: There are 93 statutory nursery schools.  38 reported a deficit as at 31 March 2024.Under the Common Funding Scheme, nursery schools do not attract Primary Principals’ Release Time factor or the Small Schools Support factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of nursery schools currently in deficit.
answer_text: There are 93 statutory nursery schools.  38 reported a deficit as at 31 March 2024.Under the Common Funding Scheme, nursery schools do not attract Primary Principals’ Release Time factor or the Small Schools Support factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of appropriate school places that need to be found for children with a statement of special educational need for September 2025.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Early planning assumptions for September 2025 showed that there was a need for circa 1300 places. While the EA has worked to increase the majority of this capacity across the school estate, there is an assumed pressure of circa 400 places remaining in advance of the new academic year. The EA has now commenced a more intensive round of engagements, prior to Easter, with those schools in areas with the greatest demand for capacity to secure the remaining placements in advance of the new academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what the remit of the Special Educational Needs Champion will be.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to complete a phased, costed operational plan which will sit behind the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan, with immediate priority on the nine projects funded from the Public Service Transformation Fund.  The remit of the SEN Champion and how that person(s) will be appointed will be defined in due course in collaboration with key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the process to appoint a Special Educational Needs Champion.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to complete a phased, costed operational plan which will sit behind the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan, with immediate priority on the nine projects funded from the Public Service Transformation Fund.  The remit of the SEN Champion and how that person(s) will be appointed will be defined in due course in collaboration with key stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will ensure that the number of pupils on a reduced timetable are tracked.
answer_text: Officials are working closely with EA colleagues to ensure that it will be possible to record absences at a more detailed level than at present through the use of a designated absence code from the commencement of the 2025/26 academic year, subject to the implementation of Edis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are developing an updated Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health has sponsorship of the Executive’s ‘Keeping children and young people safe: an Online Safety Strategy for Northern Ireland’ and accompanying three-year Action Plan, which were published in February 2021. As the online safety regulator, Ofcom has responsibilities for addressing harms to women and girls online. In February, Ofcom launched a consultation on Guidance for A Safer Life Online for Women and Girls as part of the requirements of the Online Safety Act 2023. Junior Ministers met with Ofcom earlier this year to discuss a range of issues including the consultation on Ofcom’s draft guidance on VAWG, as well as their Media Literacy Strategy. Our officials continue to liaise with Ofcom officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact the funding cuts affecting Foras na Gaeilge have had on the development of Scoil Spreagtha as a scheme and the strategic growth of the Irish language as a result.
answer_text: My department provided around £3.6m in funding to Foras na Gaeilge in 2024 to allow them to meet their statutory responsibilities. I have no plans to reduce this funding in 2025. Decisions around funding priorities, including the level of funding provided for the Scoil Spreagtha scheme, is an operational matter for Foras na Gaeilge, and their Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the learnings that can be considered from the Scoil Spreagtha scheme in bridging the gap that exists in terms of the absence of second-language learning at primary level.
answer_text: My Department has not been involved in the Scoil Spreagtha project nor has it provided any funding.  Therefore, no such assessment has been carried out.The teaching of a second language is encouraged at both Foundation and Key Stages 1 and 2.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there has been a policy change to the effect that special schools are now the only location where children and young people with Severe Learning Difficulties are placed.
answer_text: The statutory duty for the placement of children rests with the Education Authority (EA) under Article 6 of the 1986 Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order. Children with special educational needs (SEN) are educated in a range of education settings dependent on their individual needs and parental preference.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of special schools that have been re-categorised from the category of schools for Moderate Learning Difficulties to that of schools for Severe Learning Difficulties.
answer_text: My department has not received any development proposals to change the designation of a Special School.The Education Authority has confirmed that since the approval of the Special Schools Area Planning Framework in October 2021, no Special School has been re-designated from Moderate Learning Difficulties to Severe Learning Difficulties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children and young people with Severe Learning Difficulties in special schools in the last five years.
answer_text: School Census information below shows the number of pupils enrolled in special schools who were recorded as having Severe Learning Difficulties 2019/20 to 2024/25. This information relates to census day which is the Friday of the first full week in October.  Academic YearNo. of pupils2019/203,1372020/213,3412021/223,6052022/233,9252023/24No data available2024/254,648Source: Northern Ireland School CensusNotesThe 2023/24 School Census data collection was impacted by industrial action, due to Action Short of Strike.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the introduction of legislation for miscarriage leave and pay.
answer_text: The addition of the entitlement for parental bereavement leave and pay in cases of miscarriage is to be provided for by April 2026, in line with the requirements of the Parental Bereavement (Leave and Pay) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the outcome of the consultation from 2022 on Miscarriage Leave and Pay.
answer_text: The consultation analysis is at an advanced stage and the intention is to publish the Departmental response paper before the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans in place to ensure that bereaved parents are engaged and consulted with in relation to forthcoming legislation on the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised. The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will provide a power to make regulations to form a scheme, completed its First Stage in the Assembly on 25 March 2025.It is my intention that this scheme will be preceded by a public consultation in order to take the views of families who have been impacted Baby Loss. It is important to me that the final scheme is shaped by the findings of that consultation. My Department will continue to work with the Department of Health to ensure that the legislation we bring forward to enact the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme reflects the needs of people here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23560/22-27, for a breakdown of those employed directly by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is the employing authority for all support staff employed in controlled and maintained schools. The table below provides a breakdown of the categories of staff by whether the role of employer is carried out by the Board of Governors, on behalf of the EA, or by the EA itself.Board of GovernorsEAClassroom AssistantSupervisory AssistantBuilding SupervisorCleaning Staff*Extended & After Schools StaffOther including invigilators, language assistants, sports coaches and techniciansTransport StaffCatering StaffYouth Workers Cleaning Staff (EA Cleaning Service)* Schools that do not avail of EA Cleaning Service employ cleaners directly

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of public liability claims against her Department due to injuries suffered as a result of the pavement conditions in Moira in the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain its claim records by town, but by DfI Roads Section Office which broadly mirror council areas.  Moira is therefore covered by the Lisburn and Castlereagh Office.  Table 1 below details the number of personal injury public liability claims in each of the last full financial years, where the accident has been specifically recorded on my Department’s Claims Management System as having occurred on a footway in the Lisburn and Castlereagh office area.Table 1Financial yearNumber of claims received2019/2020102020/2021152021/202292022/2023192023/202420

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are incidents when road defects reported through the NI Direct reporting tool are deleted without action being taken.
answer_text: My department receives a high volume of surface defect enquiries, such as potholes, and regrettably it is not possible to investigate all of these, and therefore many are dealt with during the next planned routine inspection of the area.  During an ad-hoc investigation of a reported defect, repair work is also only carried out subject to a defect meeting our current intervention levels set out in the ‘Limited Service’ policy, (see Director of Engineering memorandum ‘Road maintenance standards for safety – limited service': https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/network-maintenance).  Defects that meet the intervention level will be updated with a repair and added to a contractor works programme with other work.  Should a reported defect not meet the intervention level, is a duplicate, or is already repaired following a recent safety inspection of the area, then this is updated with a no further action (NFA) description.  Customers can obtain an update on an individual reported defect by entering the enquiry reference number into the Track-a-fault facility on the NI Direct website. https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/track-progress-reported-road-fault-or-issue.      You can upload pothole enquiries on the following Open Data NI website link: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes reported through the NI Direct reporting tool where (i) action has been taken; (ii) action is awaiting; and (iii) no action was taken and the report deleted, in the last two years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance.  Customers can report road and lighting defects directly on the NI Direct website portal, or can phone 0300 200 7899 (24 hours a day), and a member of the call centre staff will submit this on the portal on a customer’s behalf. The table below shows the total number of pothole enquiries received between 1st March 2024 to 11th March 2025 broken down by Council area.  The information also shows the number investigated by my officials, detailing where action or no action has been taken as a result.Action taken with regard to defects can involve the addition of the repair to a works programme or notification to a third party for them to take action.  Those marked as no action refer to defects which do not meet the intervention levels of my department’s current policy, are duplications in the system, or have already been repaired as part of follow up action from a routine safety inspection of the area.Period1-Mar-23 to 1-Mar-241-Mar-24 to 1-Mar-25TotalAction taken (Investigated)14,220  8,715    22,935 No Action (Investigated)7,616 4,959    12,575 Details on pothole enquires can be found by following the Open Data NI website link: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries. You might also be interested in the road surface defects data published on: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects, mainly recorded during routine safety inspections as well as ad-hoc site investigations.  This includes the surface defect category descriptions, e.g. pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc, and also shows the status and completion date of any repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following his request that the Education Authority (EA) urgently reviews the Access NI process, whether the EA will publish any changes it is making to the process.
answer_text: On 4 February 2025, the Education Authority (EA) notified all schools that they had changed the Access NI process to require new employees to provide all the documentation necessary for checks before they receive their first payment. This should prevent any future build-up of outstanding vetting checks associated with temporary engagement.The Department of Education (DE) is also carrying out an urgent review of DE Circular 2024/10 Pre-Employment / Access NI Check Requirements and Safer Recruitment Practices for Staff and Volunteers Working in or Providing a Service for Schools/Education Settings. The proposed changes include the introduction of a requirement for Access NI documentation to be submitted to the EA on day one of employment where an individual is appointed via the emergency recruitment process.  In addition, it is proposed that the school leader will be required to seek a reference from a previous employer, which focuses on safeguarding and child protection.  The revised Circular will be published on the DE website as soon as the consultation process concludes, and all schools will be notified accordingly. Any additional changes to the Access NI process will be communicated to schools and service users in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will publish the business case for Strule Shared Education Campus.
answer_text: The Department does not routinely publish business cases. The Full Business Case (FBC) for Strule and all accompanying Departmental advice are commercially sensitive documentation and there are no plans to publish the FBC at this time.The FBC was shared with the Executive who following review agreed that the Strule Shared Education Campus should proceed immediately to contract award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether nursing provision will immediately be restored to all special schools who have had it removed.
answer_text: My priority remains to ensure that all children have safe access to education, supported by the necessary clinical support. To help ensure this, my Department’s Chief Nursing Officer has commissioned the Public Health Agency to work in partnership with the Education Authority and other key stakeholders to undertake a comprehensive needs assessment to scope the healthcare needs of children attending mainstream and special education schools. I anticipate that a final report will be completed for consideration by the end of May 2025.  It will allow us to identify the specific health challenges faced by this population, evaluate the complexity of their needs, and determine the support systems necessary to ensure timely, child-specific care within the school environment.There are no plans for further changes in the current provision of on-site nursing support in Special Education Needs schools until that review is completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current number of children awaiting paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: As of 14th November 2025, 7 children are waiting for paediatric Percutaneous Endoscopic Gastrostomy Surgery.  Of these 7 patients waiting:2 patients have dates booked for surgery before the end of November;3 patients are booked for surgery in December;BHSCT have advised that the they are aiming to book the 2 further children for surgery in January.A further 10 children are currently medically or socially suspended on the waiting list.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans in place to clear the waiting list of children awaiting paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to send patients to Evelina Children’s Hospital for paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether her Department monitors the level of heavy good vehicles passing through (a) Moira; and (b) Hillsborough; and (ii) how this impacts the requirement for road repairs.
answer_text: My Department does not specifically monitor the level of heavy goods vehicles passing through Moira Village. Officials have commissioned an assessment of the suitability of alternative routes within Royal Hillsborough following a request for a weight limit restriction to be introduced in the whole village, including Carnreagh. This work is nearing completion and, as part of the assessment, surveys of heavy goods vehicle movements have been undertaken. A final report on the findings will be available in the coming months.The footways and carriageways in Royal Hillsborough and Moira are inspected on a routine cyclical basis and any defects identified are noted and passed to the contractor for repair in accordance with my Department’s current maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline next steps for the development of the Maze Long Kesh site.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the pavement resurfacing work that has been completed in Hillsborough in the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. There are many roads that would undoubtedly benefit from investment, including those in Hillsborough, however, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of resurfacing work carried out in Hillsborough is provided in the Department’s Annual Reports to Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council. These reports can be viewed on the Department’s website www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to reduce levels of loneliness in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Northern Ireland does not currently have a specific strategy to address loneliness nor one organisation identified to lead on this work. However, it is widely acknowledged that it is an important cross cutting issue that no one organization can tackle independently. It requires a collective and collaborative approach across sectors including, central government, statutory, voluntary and community sectors. Recognising that as a society, the issue of loneliness needs to be given greater prominence, my department carried out a scoping study in 2020 to identify existing and emerging policies, strategies and initiatives that are in place across the HSC that make a positive contribution to tackling loneliness.  Subsequently a Loneliness Forum was established that  includes HSC Trusts, the Public Health Agency, the Northern Ireland Social Care Council and DoH staff. DAERA also participate in the forum.  The DOH/HSC forum focuses on promoting and sharing best practice.  My Department is committed to continuing to lead the DOH/HSC Forum and we will liaise with all stakeholders, including other Departments, to further develop plans to tackle loneliness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will install a filter light for cars turning from Main Street, Moira, onto Meeting Street.
answer_text: When considering traffic signalling arrangements, there needs to be a balanced approach to both the differing traffic movements at any given junction and also the competing pedestrian demands whilst minimising congestion and maintaining road safety. Any change to a signalling needs to consider the impact on this balance. Officials within my Department have reviewed the current traffic signalling arrangements at Main Street, Moira and consider that the current volume of right turning traffic is adequately managed within the present traffic signalling arrangement.  The extra time required for the additional stages needed to facilitate a right turn filter lane would create unnecessary delays to this junction, causing increased congestion in Moira Village.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she is considering increasing the Education Maintenance Allowance and linking it to inflation.
answer_text: The Education Maintenance Allowance scheme is a joint policy for which responsibility is shared between my Department and the Department of Education (DE) and any plans to increase the Education Maintenance Allowance would be agreed between both Departments. There are currently no plans in place to increase the Education Maintenance Allowance and link it with inflation. I do, however, recognise the valuable support that the Education Maintenance Allowance provides to enable young people to remain in education or training and my officials will continue to work with DE officials on these cross-cutting issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of his decision to make it compulsory for children to remain in education or training until they are 18 years old, to detail any work he has undertaken with the Minister for the Economy to review the Education Maintenance Allowance.
answer_text: The Minister for the Economy and I have not had any discussions on uplifting the Education Maintenance Allowance payment for young people in Northern Ireland. I recognise that the Education Maintenance Allowance provides valuable support to enable young people to remain in education or training and my officials will continue to discuss support for these young people with DfE officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the pavement resurfacing work that has been completed in Moira in the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. There are many roads that would undoubtedly benefit from investment, including those in Moira Village, however, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Details of the resurfacing work carried out in Moira is provided in the Department’s Annual Reports to Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council. These reports can be viewed on the Department’s website www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 23262/22-27, why Access NI checks have not been completed for statutory youth workers.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the youth workers in question were engaged through the temporary engagement process to ensure that safe staffing levels were maintained in youth centres. In these circumstances it was not possible to complete the Access NI check prior to engagement.
