However, the EA has confirmed that these individuals have been working in a supervised capacity only, while their checks are in progress.I have asked the EA Chief Executive to urgently review the Access NI process. As a result, I have been assured that the EA is taking robust action to ensure that the issue of outstanding checks is addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the last inspection of footways in Moira town centre was carried out.
answer_text: My officials carry out inspections of the footways in Moira town centre on a four-week cyclical basis in line with my Department’s maintenance policy.The most recent inspection was completed on 5 February 2025. Two defects were identified during this inspection, and these have been passed to our contractor for repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21924/22-27, for a breakdown of the category numbers by who has been working without a check for (i) more than one month; (ii) more than six months; and (iii) more than 12 months, including the number which are employed by the Education Authority in each category.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the number of outstanding Access NI checks related to staff engaged in schools on a temporary basis has reduced from 1,534, as of 5 February 2025, to 828, as of 28 February 2025.The breakdown of the outstanding checks at 28 February 2025 is set out in the table below. Category >1 month>6 months>12 monthsTotalClassroom Assistant28793117497Cleaning & Building Supervisor723838148Transport174122Catering325845Supervisory Assistant2011839Extended & After Schools1881137Other including invigilators, language assistants, sports coaches, technicians and youth workers17101340Total463169196828When awaiting the outcome of an Access NI check, the school principal is required to complete a risk assessment, ensure the staff member is supervised at all times by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity and follow-up to confirm that the Access NI check has subsequently been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given current financial constraints, to detail how her Department prioritises pavement resurfacing.
answer_text: Under Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993, my Department has a statutory duty to maintain all public roads in a safe and serviceable condition and to seek to fulfil this duty, an annual programme of resurfacing and reconstruction works is undertaken, the extent of which is dictated by the availability of funding.On trunk roads, information is routinely gathered and analysed from a variety of specialist machine surveys and visual inspections and this information is used in order to help inform decisions regarding our annual maintenance programmes. On other roads, divisional programmes are prepared on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage and general surface condition surveys as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims. Programmes are subject to continual review and take into account a wide range of factors such as worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, contractor capacity etc. In the absence of a fully funded allocation for the structural maintenance programme, my Department aims to prioritise decisions and interventions on the basis of the indicators outlined above as well as on the professional engineering judgements of local engineers. Those schemes not included in a particular year’s programme as result of funding constraints will routinely be considered in future years’ programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether funded nursery principal release time is part of the ongoing workload discussions and their recommendations.
answer_text: Nursery principal release time was considered under the Review of Workload Impact on School Leaders.  This was one of nine reviews committed to within the Teachers’ Pay and Workload Agreement.  Eight reviews, including this one, have been completed by joint working groups comprised of representatives from teacher unions, DE, EA, CCMS, NICIE, CnaG and GBA and supported by a dedicated joint project team. Following careful consideration of the issue of funded nursery principal release time it was agreed that this issue was not a terms and conditions issue therefore it was agreed that DE representatives would engage with National Association of Head Teachers (NAHT) outside of the remit of the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) but will keep Management Side colleagues appraised of developments. The five main teachers’ unions, which make up Northern Ireland Teachers’ Council (NITC) including NAHT were informed of this.Funding for nursery principal release time does not form part of the current pay and workload discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the gender breakdown of principals in (i) primary schools; and (ii) nursery schools.
answer_text: The table below gives the number of male and female principals in (i) primary schools and (ii) nursery schools in 2023/24.MaleFemaleNumberPercentage (%)NumberPercentage (%)Nursery0094100Primary31640.845959.2Source: Teacher workforce statistics in grant-aided schools in Northern Ireland, 2023/24. Notes.Primary includes preparatory departments of grammar schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the level of funding received received by (i) a primary school of 52 pupils; and (ii) a nursery school of equivalent size.
answer_text: All grant aided schools are funded under the Common Funding Scheme.  The Common Funding Formula (CFF) is the Department’s mechanism for allocating budgets to schools funded through Local Management of Schools arrangements. A school’s CFF allocation is determined by both the quantum of funding available to the Aggregated Schools Budget and a combination of the school’s overall enrolment, the differentiated needs of its pupils and the school’s characteristics.  (i) In 2024-25 the funding allocated to the four primary schools with 52 full time equivalent (FTE) pupils ranges from £262,902 to £272,833.  The average funding is £266,183.(ii) In 2024-25 the funding allocated to the 38 nursery schools with 52 FTE pupils ranges from £231,535 to £288,669.  The average funding is £255,021.The Education Authority, as funding authority for grant aided schools, publishes an analysis of resource allocations and expenditure for individual schools on its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when he will outline his plans following the consultation on a Regional Obesity Management Service; and (ii) why there is a delay in action following the consultation closing.
answer_text: Last year my Department completed a public consultation on the proposed introduction of a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS), which would provide a means for people living with obesity in NI to access treatment and specialist support which is not currently available to them. I am currently considering the analysis of the responses received and potential next steps in the context of the difficult financial position my Department must operate within.  Decisions regarding future development of this service will be based on consideration of the evidence presented, availability of resources, and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the agreed measures his Department has in place to track the number of children better prepared to start school, as referenced in the Fair Start Report.
answer_text: The 3+ Review introduced as part of the Early Intervention Transformation Programme is a Health-led initiative which provides a developmental assessment of pre-school children carried out by the Health Visitor in the pre-school setting.  It is intended to use this source to estimate the percentage of children in their immediate pre-school year at their appropriate stage of development.  The data is still under development and work is ongoing between officials in my department, the Public Health Agency and the Department of Health to assess its reliability and ensure it is robust.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing legislation on the operation of private car parking businesses.
answer_text: Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement across the north, it has no responsibility for the operation and management of private car parks nor the enforcement companies that operate within them.There is no legislation which specifically applies to privately owned car parks and my Department currently has no plans to introduce legislation in this area. However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association (BPA). Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he has taken following the Northern Ireland Audit Report into Managing the Schools' Estate, November 2024.
answer_text: I welcome the report by the Northern Ireland Audit Office on my Department’s approach to Managing the School’s Estate. While officials are working on how best to implement the various recommendations, this report leaves no doubt that the schools’ estate is in need of significantly increased and sustained funding to address the many years of underinvestment. We have an estate of over 1,100 schools, many of which are in extremely poor condition.The report recognises that the Department’s current approach to estate management is largely driven by the level of funding available within a difficult overall financial position.  It also recognises that I, as Minister, have agreed a clear vision for the schools’ estate, which is aligned to the Department’s goals. More work is necessary to build on this and develop an Estate Strategy to ensure more effective planning and delivery.However, the bottom line is my Department can only do so much with the budget made available to it, which means that difficult decisions have been, and will continue to be, necessary and in some cases some things will simply not get done unless the level of funding is significantly increased.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans being considered to improve transport links to the Maze Long Kesh site, including to the Eikon Exhibition Centre.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:The 325G Lisburn City service passes the Maze Long Kesh site. This service runs hourly between circa 8am to 8pm at present. Translink has no plans to increase the service at this time and any increase in service would require additional funding.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22958/22-27, to provide a breakdown of the seven youth workers who have been working without an Access NI check in place for more than six months, by whether the employer is the voluntary sector or statutory sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority has confirmed that the seven youth workers without an Access NI check for more than six months are all employed within the statutory sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the budget that has been allocated to further education colleges for post-19 special educational needs provision in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has provided a ring-fenced budget of £4.5 million, per annum, for each of the last 5 years, through the Additional Support Fund (ASF) to Further Education (FE) colleges towards the necessary additional technical and/or personal support required to allow students with learning difficulties or disabilities to participate in mainstream FE courses, or in discrete programmes. ASF is available to all students who enrol in FE colleges, irrespective of age, the department does not hold information in relation to the age profile of students who avail of this support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils that left special schools at the age of 19 in each of the three years.
answer_text: School Census information below shows the number of pupils enrolled in special schools who were recorded as being 18 in each of the last three years. Age is recorded at 1 July, so the pupils below will turn 19 before the end of the academic year.Census YearNumber of pupils aged 182024/252502023/242842022/23236

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the roads in Moira village that are scheduled for resurfacing in 2025.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. There are many roads that would undoubtedly benefit from investment, including those in Moira Village, however, due to a limited budget, resurfacing work must be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention.Officials are currently developing the 2025/26 resurfacing programme, and this will be provided in the Department’s Annual Report to Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council.In the interim, my officials will continue to undertake regular inspections in Moira and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s current maintenance policy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have considered including the development of a link road between the M1 and the Maze Long Kesh site in the final Programme for Government 2024-2027 and the Investment Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: We recognise the immense economic, historical and reconciliation potential of the site and are committed to working with the Board to maximise that potential for the benefit of all. We recognise the benefit an improved road connection between the MLK site and the M1 motorway would bring, not least for the annual Balmoral Show.  Other events at the Eikon Centre and the wider site would also benefit, and it would potentially improve access across the surrounding area.  Of course, an improved road connection is also of critical importance to any future development that may take place on the site. The Investment Strategy is currently being finalised along with the accompanying Medium Term Financial Plan (MTFP), which sets out priorities for infrastructure investment over the next 10 years.  The potential for a link road will be considered as part of discussions on these issues.Any proposals for a road connection will be subject to the normal planning and internal governance approvals and the allocation of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of speech and language therapists needed to deliver the commitment to the development of a Speech, Language and Communications Intervention Programme which will target 10,000 children over the next three academic years.
answer_text: Work is ongoing between my officials and Health colleagues to develop the operational detail of joint Health/Education initiatives within the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any further action that can be taken to improve the traffic flow into Moira from the M1 in order to prevent tailbacks onto the Moira Roundabout and the M1.
answer_text: My Department has previously introduced measures to improve traffic flow in Moira including the signalisation of the Main Street/Meeting Street junction, and a road marking scheme to provide right turn pockets between Backwood Road and the Old Kilmore Road junction.		The village’s future transport needs, being situated within the Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council area, will be considered as part of the new Eastern Transport Plan being developed by my Department.  The plan will take account of the growth ambitions of Lisburn and Castlereagh city and other councils in the plan catchment area and will consider potential transport interventions for Moira.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on a feasibility study into the reopening of the Knockmore railway line.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount spent by her Department in 2024-25 on measures to deliver affordable, quality childcare.
answer_text: While childcare policy is primarily the responsibility of the Department of Education, delivering ‘more affordable, accessible, high-quality Early Learning and Childcare’ is an Executive priority in the Programme for Government.  In July 2024, £740k of the Skills Fund was ringfenced to support pathways into the childcare sector and increase the supply of suitably qualified workers. In addition, in August 2024, a business case was approved for £172k for a pilot collaborative Assured Skills Academy in Early Learning and Childcare delivered through Belfast Met. The aim of the academy was to equip 20 participants with the necessary skills and experience to commence roles in the childcare sector. The department also provides a suite of pathways for childcare training and qualifications through Further and Higher Education, and through vocational qualifications like apprenticeships and traineeships, which will qualify individuals to work across a range of childcare settings.A further £153k has been committed to delivering market research on a targeted business support scheme for childcare providers. This will report in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update of where the £25 million Early Learning and Childcare Executive funding has been (i) spent; and (ii) earmarked for.
answer_text: My written statement to the Assembly in May 2024 set out the earmarked allocations from the £25 Early Learning and Childcare package:Written Ministerial Statement - Early Learning and Childcare Measures 2024/25 | Department of Education.The final spend position is not yet available.  However, monitoring to date indicates that the profile is broadly in line with the earmarked allocations with the exception of £1.8m intended for a targeted childcare business support fund, which was returned to the Department of Finance as part of January monitoring.  I will provide the final expenditure position at the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21924/22-27, for a further breakdown of the number of sports coaches and youth workers currently working in schools without an Access NI check, broken down by those who have been working without a check for (i) more than one month; (ii) more than six months; and (iii) more than twelve months.
answer_text: When a school engages a temporary staff member, they are required to follow the guidance set out in DE Circular 2024/10, related to pre-employment and Access NI requirements and safer resourcing practices. This includes ensuring that non-vetted staff are supervised by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity. Where a staff member has not submitted the paperwork needed by Access NI, the Education Authority (EA) engages with the school Principal to remind them of the requirement to have this completed. Despite protracted communication between the EA and the employee/school, the documentation required to undertake the check can remain outstanding for a prolonged period.  The table below details the number of sports coaches and youth workers without an Access NI check, broken down by the length of time the check has been outstanding.Time PeriodNo. Sports Coaches1No. Youth Workers14 weeks to 6 months256 months to 1 year01Greater than 1 year06Total2121 Figures as at 20 February 2025I have asked the EA Chief Executive to urgently review the Access NI process within schools and the EA. As a result, I have been assured that the EA is taking robust action to ensure that the issue of outstanding checks is addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21687/22-27, to clarify that the Southern Health and Social Care Trust provides no other support apart from a leaflet and details for community organisations.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support available to homeowners living in houses where the water infrastructure has not yet been adopted by NI Water and the developer has closed the relevant development company.
answer_text: At present, there is no support available to homeowners living in houses where the wastewater infrastructure has not yet been adopted by NI Water and the developer has closed the relevant development company.Unadopted developments, remain private developments and neither the Department nor NI Water has any legal remit to carry out works in private developments, with the responsibility for the development resting with the developer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the bids made for the Draft Budget 2025-26 which relate to (i) supporting childcare businesses; and (ii) delivering quality, affordable childcare for families.
answer_text: The draft Executive Budget 2025-26 contains an allocation of £50m for proposals brought forward by the Early Years and Childcare Task and Finish Group.  Any future bids to provide business support to childcare providers will be informed by the market research currently being undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail why the £1.8 million returned to the Department of Finance in relation to earmarked Early Learning and Childcare funding was not used for other childcare support interventions, such as the Childcare Subsidy Scheme.
answer_text: The c£1.8m initially earmarked for childcare business support was returned to the Department of Finance when it was confirmed in November 2024 that the timeframe for the Department for the Economy completing its sector scoping study had been revised to March 2025.  Re-allocating the funding at that point to other early learning and childcare measures was considered but deemed not feasible within the timeframe. In line with financial budgeting processes, the c£1.8m underspend within the Early Learning and Childcare 2024/25 budget was returned to the Department of Finance as part of January monitoring and it became available to meet other Executive priorities in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the £1.8 million returned to the Department of Finance in respect of earmarked Early Learning and Childcare funding will be retained for Early Learning and Childcare measures.
answer_text: The c£1.8m initially earmarked for childcare business support was returned to the Department of Finance when it was confirmed in November 2024 that the timeframe for the Department for the Economy completing its sector scoping study had been revised to March 2025.  Re-allocating the funding at that point to other early learning and childcare measures was considered but deemed not feasible within the timeframe. In line with financial budgeting processes, the c£1.8m underspend within the Early Learning and Childcare 2024/25 budget was returned to the Department of Finance as part of January monitoring and it became available to meet other Executive priorities in this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 20966/22-27, whether the £1.8 million returned to the Department of Finance which had been ring-fenced for Early Learning and Childcare Funding will still be available to support childcare measures.
answer_text: As required for underspends in earmarked funding, the Department of Education returned the £1.8 million, and this formed part of the funding that was made available in the January monitoring round for allocation across departments.The Executive’s Draft Budget 2025-26 sets aside £50 million for early learning and childcare support measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20966/22-27, to detail (i) how much of the £2 million, earmarked from the £25 million Early Learning and Childcare Funding, has been used in respect of scoping and developing a Targeted Business Support Scheme; and (ii) why the full £2 million was not spent.
answer_text: On 23 May 2024, I received Executive approval for a package of early learning and childcare measures to be implemented in the 2024/25 financial year.  Within this, £2m was earmarked for a targeted business support scheme for childcare providers.  Following engagement between officials through the Task and Finish Group, I wrote to the Economy Minister in July 2024 to formally request that his Department, in conjunction with Invest Northern Ireland, lead on the development and implementation of this measure given their expertise in designing and delivering business support instruments and the need to ensued alignment with existing economic development interventions.  The Economy Minister advised that his department needed greater clarity around the scheme objectives and offered to lead a scoping exercise to better understand the support needs of the childcare sector and identify what an appropriate government response should be.  Just over £150,000 of the £2m available was requested and transferred to DfE in the September monitoring round to carry out this scoping work which was initially expected to complete by the end of December 2024.  The timeframe for completion of the DfE-led exercise was subsequently extended to end March 2025 in order to allow for a more comprehensive assessment.  The balance was returned to the Department of Finance as part of the January monitoring exercise, given that this funding is ringfenced by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 16788/22-27.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 16960/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 16960/22-27 was answered on 8 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 20843/22-27, whether they will publish the minutes of the latest meeting with the Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation.
answer_text: Meetings within the Department and its Arm’s Length Bodies are considered internal meetings and TEO does not publish minutes of internal meetings as a matter of course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 18861/22-27, to provide a date when they will appoint a new Chair and board members to the Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation Board.
answer_text: We have asked officials for further advice on the options for filling the vacancies on the MLKDC Board.  This will inform decisions on the way forward and the timeframe for appointments to be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact following Samaritans NI not receiving funding from the Core Grant Funding Scheme 2025-26.

To ask the Minister of Education in relation to the action to develop, where appropriate, bespoke services to support children in Irish-medium education, outlined in the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda - Delivery Plan 2025-2030, to detail how his Department will determine what bespoke services are appropriate.
answer_text: A working group has been established, which includes representation from my department, the Education Authority (EA), Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnAG) and Altram, to examine the gaps in current services to support children with special educational needs (SEN) in Irish-medium education (IME) settings and develop a range of costed, evidence-based and co-designed solutions to address any such gaps. This work will inform decisions on the delivery of bespoke services, subject to budget considerations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide a breakdown of estimated costs for each action in the Special Education Needs Reform Agenda five-year Delivery Plan which amount to the estimated £570 million total.
answer_text: As set out in the SEN Reform Agenda costings will be finessed in detailed implementation plans and in collaboration with key delivery partners.An analysis of the current SEN financial model has identified opportunities to restructure existing resource towards more effective interventions and support, creating return on investment as well as opportunities for better outcomes for children and young people with SEN. There are also opportunities to draw in additional investment. A bid has been made to the Interim NICS Transformation Board which if successful will enable us to commence the phased implementation of this long-term inclusive SEN Reform Agenda. We will also seek opportunities within education and with other identified partners in Health and Economy, the voluntary and community and private sectors to jointly fund actions facilitating not only value for money, but a collaborative and child-centred approach to investment

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of recent increases in National Insurance contributions and the Minimum Wage, to detail any support that can be provided to childcare providers.
answer_text: As the Department of Education has policy responsibility for childcare, it will be for the Education Minister to determine if any additional support can be provided to childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 22298/22-27, whether the Education Authority's in-house team is now replacing the role Fujitsu were originally contracted to fulfil.
answer_text: I can confirm that there are additional Education Authority (EA) responsibilities as a consequence of the decision not to continue with the SPSMS Contract. These responsibilities are being delivered under the management of the EA’s Education Information Solutions Team.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether his Department is tracking information on (i) the pricing of all childcare providers registered on the NI Childcare Subsidy Scheme; and (ii) staff salaries, to understand the level of support required.
answer_text: Childcare providers recorded details of their fees upon registering for the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS).  As the Scheme is continuing into 2025-26 financial year, providers will be required to update this information. As the NICSS is designed to reduce the cost of childcare for eligible working parents, it does not collect information on staff salaries.  A scoping study led by the Department for the Economy has been set up to assess the business support needs of childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps her Department is taking to support childcare providers and ensure families have access to quality, affordable childcare.
answer_text: High quality, affordable childcare is a priority for the Executive. My department is working closely with the Department of Education on the development of their Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy, including representation on the ELC Task and Finish Group where workforce is a specific focus. Supporting skills and education pathways for those working in the sector is a key focus for my department. My department currently provides a suite of pathways for childcare training and qualifications through Further and Higher Education, and through vocational qualifications like apprenticeships and traineeships, which will qualify individuals to work across a range of childcare settings. In July 2024, my predecessor ringfenced £740k of the Skills Fund to support pathways into the childcare sector, with the aim of increasing the supply of suitably qualified workers to help maintain and grow the sector across the North. In addition, we funded 20 places on the Assured Skills Academy in Early Learning and Childcare at Belfast Met. We will continue to monitor the need for additional or alternative skills provision to support the childcare sector. In addition, my officials are assisting the Department of Education with market research on the childcare sector. This research will determine the key issues impacting the financial sustainability of providers within the childcare sector and will inform whether specific sectoral support is required and, if so, what form this should take.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to (i) increase the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme cap of £167 per month; (ii) increase the subsidy percentage from 15 per cent; and (iii) extend the subsidy scheme to school aged children.
answer_text: I have secured £50m for Early Learning and Childcare provision in the draft 2025-26 Executive Budget, currently out for consultation.  I am considering options for its allocation, based on what is affordable.  When the budget is agreed, I will bring forward proposals to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will provide a breakdown of how the £50 million allocated for childcare measures in the Draft Budget 2025-26 will be spent by his Department.
answer_text: I am considering options for the allocation of the £50m, based on what is affordable.  When the budget is agreed, I will bring forward proposals to the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the Education Authority will appoint a new contractor to deliver the EdIS Strategic Partner and School Management System contract.
answer_text: The Education Authority are currently planning for a new procurement process to appoint a new contractor to deliver Strategic Partner services to schools. The aim is to complete the procurement process and award of contract by March 2027. Continuity of services to schools will be provided by the incumbent managed services provider, Capita, under its existing contract i.e. Education Network Northern Ireland, which will be extended until 31 March 2027.  The Schools Management System (SMS) element of the SPSMS, provided by a sub-contractor (Bromcom) to Fujitsu, was novated from Fujitsu to the EA. Therefore there is no requirement to procure this element of the contract.  The development of the Bromcom SMS is ongoing and it is anticipated the replacement SMS will begin full implementation in schools in May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education Authority are obligated to pay any money to Fujitsu following the cancellation of the contract for the EdIS Strategic Partner and School Management System.
answer_text: The Education Authority are not obligated to pay any money to Fujitsu following the mutual termination of the Strategic Partner and School Management System contract.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether it is protocol for someone living in Northern Ireland, who has been initially treated in the Republic of Ireland but needs further, non-urgent, outpatient care in Northern Ireland, to be sent to an Emergency Department.
answer_text: There is no formal protocol about referring patients to Emergency Departments for follow up treatment after initial treatment abroad.  GPs will use their clinical judgement when referring patients to secondary care.  This could be to out-patient care for non-urgent follow-up, or to an Urgent Care Centre or Emergency Department for care that is time critical such as fracture management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the protocol for interaction between the Public Health Agency and the Health and Social Care Trusts when an issue arises from screening.
To ask the Minister of Education whether there is a Department-approved criteria defining (i) what a sensory room is; and (ii) what a sensory room contains.
answer_text: The term “sensory room” is not formally defined by my Department.  A sensory room is a withdrawal space used for children and young people with sensory processing disorders, or cognitive conditions who may become dysregulated.  The room utilises a range of audio, physical and visual sensory stimuli.Equipment for a sensory room is normally requested to meet the specific needs of the child/children that will use the space by health professionals, or special educational needs staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (i) that have been set up to date; and (ii) that are projected for the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: There are 593 operational specialist provision classes in mainstream schools;The Education Authority’s (EA) current projections indicate a need for between 50 and 70 new specialist provision classes for the 2025/26 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether occupational therapists are required to (i) be consulted on; and (ii) sign-off equipment purchased for a Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools' classroom.
answer_text: General classroom equipment does not require approval from an Occupational Therapist (OT) and is procured by individual schools.The Education Authority (EA) may purchase child-specific equipment for specialist provision classrooms on the recommendation of an OT.  It is a matter for Health to have the appropriate governance and sign-off in place regarding OTs’ recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are auditing procedures to ensure the effectiveness of money spent on Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools.
answer_text: Vacant or derelict sites are not encompassed within either the domestic or non-domestic rating administered by my Department. A policy to place a levy on vacant sites could not rest within the Department of Finance as a sole entity. Rather it is cross-cutting and spans a number of ministerial portfolios including Communities, Economy and Agriculture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of people currently working in schools without an Access NI Check in roles that have contact with children and young people.
answer_text: All education workers employed in a statutory education setting in a regulated activity post are required to complete an Access NI check. These checks are carried out by the relevant employing authority.Both the Education Authority (EA) and Council for Catholic Maintained schools (CCMS) have confirmed that there are no teaching staff working in schools with an outstanding Access NI check. Support staff are employed by the EA, and they have advised that in the case of a short-term temporary hire or emergency cover appointment, it is not always possible to finalise the Access NI check prior to engagement. When awaiting the outcome of an Access NI check, the school principal is required to complete a risk assessment, ensure the staff member is supervised at all times by someone who is vetted to work in regulated activity and follow-up to confirm that the Access NI check has subsequently been completed.As of 5 February 2025, there were 1,534 Access NI checks in progress for support staff engaged in this manner. These can be broken down as follows: CategoryNumber*Classroom Assistant862Cleaning & Building Supervisor240Transport42Catering100Supervisory Assistant107Extended & After Schools71Other including invigilators, language assistants, sports coaches, technicians and youth workers112Total1,534*The above figures include support staff employed in both Controlled and Maintained schools. I have written to the EA Chief Executive asking him to review the process for support staff and take action to ensure that the number of outstanding checks is kept to a minimum

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the International Ministerial Advisory Panel will be providing expertise on pre-vocational and technical pathways, as advised in the Independent Review of Education.
answer_text: The International Ministerial Advisory Panel is primarily focused on curriculum and assessment matters, and this may include consideration of pre-vocational curricular pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when the School Building Handbooks for nursery, primary and post-primary schools were (i) last reviewed; and (ii) last updated.
answer_text: The School Building Handbooks for nursery, primary and post-primary schools are live documents and are reviewed on a continual basis. The latest updates to the handbooks were published on 27 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he is taking with the Minister for the Economy to improve effective collaboration between schools and further education colleges.
answer_text: The 14-19 Framework “Developing a More Strategic Approach to 14-19 Education and Training – A Framework to Transform 14-19 Education and Training Provision” was developed jointly by my Department and the Department for the Economy (DfE) as a basis on which to drive forward a more strategic and collaborative approach to 14-19 education and training.  There has been significant engagement with stakeholders right across the 14-19 landscape, including schools and the further education colleges, both in the development of the Framework and in taking forward the actions contained within it.  Together with representatives from schools and the education sector, Mr Leo Murphy, Principal of North-West Regional College, sits on the 14-19 Framework Programme Board.  The Programme Board was established to oversee the implementation of the actions contained within the Framework.  Following the Gateway review in early 2024, officials in DE and DfE worked closely with stakeholders to devise an improved delivery model.  This included hosting a workshop for departmental staff and for the wider 14-19 stakeholder group in the Belfast Metropolitan College to devise new governance arrangements to support delivery of work.  Representatives from the education and further education sectors attended that workshop.  Through the Entitlement Framework, schools and FE Colleges work collaboratively to meet the requirement to offer students access to a minimum of 21 courses.Representatives of schools and FE colleges also have the opportunity to engage on a regular basis at meetings of their local Area Learning Community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there are minimum specifications for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools, including (i) specifications for the room or building; (ii) equipment available; and (iii) teacher and staff training.
answer_text: Officials in my Department have prepared a Handbook for specialist provision accommodation which is currently going through the review and approval process.  This Handbook will provide guidance for new school building projects.  Whilst accommodation works for specialist provision classes currently being delivered in existing schools are based on this draft Handbook, you will appreciate that due to various restrictions including the age of the building, floor plans and site size, it is not always possible to deliver in line with all the Handbook’s recommended requirements.   Equipment for specialist provision classes is purchased and obtained at the discretion of the school and the SEN coordinator or specialist provision teacher.  Newly established specialist provision is allocated a budget of £20,000 to purchase furniture and equipment to meet the needs of its pupils.  During the statutory assessment or review process, an Occupational Therapist may recommend specific equipment for an individual pupil to support their access to the curriculum, in these circumstances the Education Authority’s (EA) Statutory Assessment and Review Service is responsible for ordering and funding the item(s). The EA’s Specialist Setting Support Team (SSST), is a multi-service team, providing integrated support for specialist provision classes. The SSST team provides support at pre and post set up level for new settings. A comprehensive professional learning and development programme is available, delivered both online and in person, for senior leadership, teachers, classroom assistants and parents.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any capital funding programmes currently open to schools to apply for funding.
answer_text: There are not currently any capital funding programmes open to schools to apply for funding. However, if a school identifies an issue which  meets the current Ministerial Priority 1 criteria, i.e. minor works that meet inescapable statutory requirements such as Health and Safety, fire protection or statutory obligations under the Disability Discrimination Act, they can submit an ‘Unavoidable Minor Works Application' to either the Department of Education, if it is a maintained, voluntary grammar, grant-maintained integrated or Irish-medium school, or the Education Authority, if it is a controlled school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the threshold cost of capital works to qualify as Minor Works has been changed.
answer_text: In October 2017 the Department increased the Education Authority’s (EA) capital delegation from £500k to £1m. This effectively meant that from that date, all EA capital projects below that threshold were carried out by the EA without reference to the Department. The EA is responsible for determining its own minor works priorities and for planning, managing and carrying out these works. Projects above £1m can still be undertaken but require approval by the Department’s Finance Director.Similarly, all capital projects above £1m in the non-controlled sector, which are managed by the Department’s Investment and Infrastructure Directorate, are subject to approval by the Department’s Finance Director. All capital projects are subject to a value for money business case and relevant statutory approvals. Costs associated with the schemes are reviewed and assessed by Quantity Surveyors to ensure value for money.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail all projects that have received funding under his Department's capital funding allocation in the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate.
answer_text: Details of capital expenditure on projects from 1 April 2022 to 31 January 2025 across capital programmes, broken down by school or programme, is set out in tables which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the capital projects that have received funding where an application for funding was not submitted, since the beginning of the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: The information that you have requested relating to the number of capital projects that have received funding where an application for funding was not submitted, since the beginning of the 2022-2027 mandate will take some time to collate.I will arrange to have the information forwarded to you as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21055/22-27, whether information on the reason for leaving the Education Authority is recorded.
answer_text: The reasons for Educational Psychologists leaving the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) is recorded by the EA via exit interviews.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the unavoidable Minor Works applications submitted to his Department since the beginning of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate, including (i) name of school; (ii) date of submission; and (iii) whether the application was approved.
answer_text: The information that you have requested relating to the number of unavoidable Minor Works applications submitted to the department since the beginning of the 2022-2027 Assembly mandate, will take some time to collate. I will arrange to have the information forwarded to you as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 21056/22-27, for his assessment of an increase in the bursary amount.
answer_text: A range of bursary options are being assessed as part the Doctorate in Educational, Child and Adolescent Psychology (DECAP) 2025/26 – 2027/28 business case process.  As the business case is currently in draft form it would not be appropriate to comment further at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether Minor Work applications for schools are still limited to inescapable statutory requirements.
answer_text: Projects progressing under the Minor Works programme are currently prioritised on the basis of health and safety, statutory compliance, the need to address urgent and emergency repairs and implementation of approved Development Proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the role of Deputy Secretary for Resources, Governance and Early Years being vacant in his Department is delaying any work on the Childcare and Early Learning Strategy.
answer_text: The Directorate responsible for developing the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy has been temporarily re-assigned to the Deputy Secretary for Education Policy and Children’s Services.  There has been no delay in progressing this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the review of the Further Education delivery model.
answer_text: Question 12 – AQO 1521/22-27I am very supportive of the critical role played by Further Education Colleges in delivering the skills which the economy needs by providing learners wit high quality vocational and technical education.  With courses available from access level to post graduate level, colleges have courses to suit learners of all ages. Additionally, our colleges provide support to many businesses to help them to innovate and grow.  This locally based provision is critical to ensuring all areas are able to benefit from economic growth.My Department will be taking forward a co-designed programme of work in conjunction with Further Education Colleges over the next 24 months to review the Further Education delivery model. This work aims to promote greater consistency in the provision of technical and vocational educational pathways as well as innovation support for employers in pursuit of my Economic Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support that is provided to mothers and families who have experienced a miscarriage, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast HSCT TrustWomen are cared for within different departments depending on their gestation and their clinical condition on presenting at the hospital.  These include the:Maternity Assessment Unit (MAU)Early Pregnancy Unit (EPU)Delivery suiteSome women will be seen in the MAU, which provides care to women who require assessment and or additional care at various gestations as appropriate.  Some women may need to remain in the MAU until their physical condition is stabilised prior to transfer to theatre and or onto a different department.  Women are also supported through miscarriage in the Early Pregnancy Unit.  Specially trained midwives who offer transvaginal ultrasound and bereavement support are available in the unit Monday – Friday 08:00-16:00.  In addition to this, the service has two Consultant Obstetricians and a Speciality Doctor who provide cover three mornings weekly. The midwives provide counselling in relation to the options for the management of miscarriage and provide care for medical management both as an outpatient or an inpatient option.  Surgical management of miscarriage is also offered.  Women who chose this option will be cared for in the ward setting of EPU where they are transferred to theatre for the procedure and returned to the ward to receive post-operative care from the midwives within EPU.   For women who chose medical management of miscarriage they will remain under the care of EPU for a number of weeks until the miscarriage is fully resolved.  This can involve additional visits to the unit or virtual appointments over the phone.All midwives, doctors and maternity support workers provide bereavement care.  They are mindful of the need to provide sensitive and compassionate care, which meets the needs of individual families.  Some women will need to return to the EPU for ongoing care and they are cared for in a small private area with private toilet facilities.  The EPU has been recently refurbished while working in partnership with the Snowdrop staff. Women are provided with supporting documentation from the BHSCT / Miscarriage Association / Cradle and also informed of the bereavement support available from the childbirth and loss midwives.  Women experiencing recurrent pregnancy loss are also referred to the recurrent pregnancy loss clinic.It is recognised that families who experience miscarriage are vulnerable to mental health difficulties and trauma symptoms alongside their grief.  If required those families can be referred to the Maternity Psychological Service, embedded within the Royal Maternity Jubilee Hospital.  Referrals for women, and birth partners/fathers are accepted and they are offered individual or couples-based sessions, depending on the clinical need.  There is a longstanding high demand for psychological input into Maternity services and the Service is actively exploring opportunities to improve access for psychological interventions and reduce waiting times for bereaved families.  South Easter

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether different Health and Social Care Trusts have different protocols in response to women who experience bleeding while in their first trimester of pregnancy.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts offer early pregnancy services for women who experience bleeding following a positive pregnancy test.  Please see below the Trusts protocols in response to women who experience bleeding while in their first trimester of pregnancy: Belfast HSCT TrustThe Belfast Trust have a policy on the Management of Bleeding in Pregnancy in place.  The Trust also follows National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance NG 126 on Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management NG126.Referrals to the Early Pregnancy Unit (EPU) are accepted from Emergency Department/ GP/ Maternity Assessment Unit / Antenatal Clinic / Self-referral from women.  Access to EPU is available within a few days of referral but is also available on a same day basis consistent with clinical need.  Specific arrangements are in place to support women with a history of ectopic or a scan confirmed ectopic pregnancy.  EPU services also operate ongoing review every two to three weeks for women with ongoing bleeding in early pregnancy.Women in the very early stages of pregnancy e.g. 4/5 weeks who report bleeding may be asked to repeat a pregnancy test in one week.  Should the test remain positive and there remain concerns re bleeding they will then be offered review in EPU. South Eastern HSC TrustSouth Eastern Trust provide early pregnancy services for women who experience bleeding following a positive pregnancy test. Women can self-refer or be referred by their general practitioner.Northern HSC TrustThe Northern Trust operates early pregnancy clinics (EPC) on both Antrim and Causeway sites seven days a week.  The EPC is a nurse-led service offering ultrasound assessment and blood testing.  The EPC accepts self-referrals, referrals from gynaecology, midwifery care, GP’s and ED.  Initial assessment is primarily via telephone triageAntrim EPC is available 9am – 5pm Monday to Friday and 9am to 3pm on Saturday and Sunday.  Causeway EPC runs 9am – 1pm Monday to Sunday. Out of hours or if women’s history indicates they may become unstable, such as heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, dizziness or vasovagal episode, they should attend the closest emergency department (ED) for assessment. Depending on presentation of symptoms and quantification of bleeding different pathways may be followed;Scant – light bleeding: women are advised to observe with worsening symptoms advice given. A log of the call will ensure woman is allocated appointment if bleeding persists or worsens.Light – moderate bleeding: woman is allocated an appointment in EPC – if they have not had a scan in this pregnancy already, it is ideal that the woman is seen within 24 hours Heavy bleeding: same day assessment is required. If clinical triage indicates, women are diverted to ED and then assessed by Gynae / EPC team at a later point in the assessment when stabilised.The above information is in relation to PV bleeding in the first trimester with no associated symptoms such as abdominal

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support that is provided to mothers and families who have experienced a stillbirth, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Belfast HSC TrustWithin Belfast Trust, all midwives, doctors, neonatal nurses as well as clinical support staff provide bereavement care. The service employs two full-time specialist Childbirth and Loss midwives who will meet with bereaved women and their partners upon request to provide help and support in the initial bereavement period and following discharge home. In Royal Jubilee Maternity Hospital the Trust endeavour to care for families who have experienced pregnancy loss from 12 weeks gestation in the Snowdrop Room.  This is a dedicated bereavement room on a separate corridor within delivery suite with an ensuite bathroom, tea and coffee making facilities and has also been co-designed with members of the Snowdrop Group.  The Snowdrop Group works in partnership with the bereaved parents to enhance and improve bereavement care.Families are supported to spend time with their babies, make memories and share this precious time with extended family members including siblings as appropriate. They are guided in making decisions in relation to further investigations including hospital post-mortem examination.  Staff discuss and assist families in making funeral arrangements for their baby.  In addition staff recognise the impact for families experiencing stillbirth where English is not their first language.  They engage with the appropriate interpreting services and are respectful of the cultural needs of individual families. Families can also avail of the chaplaincy service. Bereaved families receive one to one care from a dedicated midwife within delivery suite, supported by the Childbirth and Loss Midwives.  Families are supported to spend time with their babies, creating memories and introducing their baby to siblings and or extended familiesOn discharge to the community, GP services provide ongoing support. Bereaved families are also given information about a range of charities including Sands, Child Bereavement UK, Anam Cara, Tiny Life, the Twins Trust, Cradle and Aching Arms. It is recognised that families who experience a stillbirth are vulnerable to mental health difficulties and trauma symptoms alongside their grief. If required those families can be referred to the Maternity Psychological Service, embedded within the Royal Maternity Jubilee Hospital. Referrals for women, and birth partners/fathers are accepted and they are offered individual or couples-based sessions, depending on the clinical need. There is a longstanding high demand for psychological input into Maternity services, coupled with limited resources within the Maternity Psychological Service, and the service is actively exploring opportunities to improve access for psychological interventions and reduce waiting times for bereaved families.  South Eastern HSC TrustSouth Eastern Trust provide a bereavement suite for women who sadly experience a stillbirth. The maternity service has two bereavement midwives who provide listening support with guidance to families who have exper

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the role of Deputy Secretary for Resources, Governance and Early Years will be filled.
answer_text: The role of Deputy Secretary for Resources, Governance and Early Years is currently being covered jointly by the Acting Permanent Secretary, Deputy Secretary of Sustainability and Infrastructure and Deputy Secretary of Education Policy and Children’s Services. This is an interim measure that will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on (i) plans to redevelop the former Dromore Central Primary School site; and (ii) when he will know if the site will be used for special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority is holding the former Dromore Primary School site from disposal as it is currently under consideration for SEN provision.Initial technical assessments have been carried out and draft plans developed for extensive refurbishment works to the building. Unfortunately, timeframes cannot be provided at this stage as other options are also under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement he has had with the Utility Regulator in relation to the development of a NIE Networks compensation scheme for those impacted by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Officials from my Department are engaging with senior representatives from the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to determine what options are available to provide payments to electricity customers across the North who were most impacted by a loss of power due to Storm Éowyn. My officials will continue to work with the Utility Regulator and NIE Networks to identify the most appropriate option to provide payments to eligible electricity customers impacted by Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is considering any actions to enable the Department to better track the (i) level of bullying incidents in schools; and (ii) trends of bullying incidents to support schools with interventions.
answer_text: Officials are progressing work on a number of fronts with a view to ensuring the Department has access to comprehensive data to better understand the frequency, motivations and methods of bullying type behaviour (BTB).The Department has included a range of questions in relation to bullying type behaviour or the first time in the 2024 School Omnibus Survey including questions in relation to BTB methods and motivations.  Officials have also engaged with the ETI to ensure the inclusion of questions as part of a wider ETI pilot from September – December 2024, to help determine how schools are fulfilling their statutory obligations in relation to implementing the Act, in terms of recording of all incidents of bullying, development, implementation, monitoring and review of Addressing-Bullying policies and procedures.Schools are presently required to complete a Bullying Concern Assessment Form (BCAF) on the School Information Management System (SIMS) for each incident of BTB.  The BCAF records statutorily required data, however presently this data cannot be collated or anonymised.  The BCAF is being digitised as part of the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) digital change programme.  The Department is starting to explore with the EA whether there is potential for accessing anonymised regional data resulting from the digitisation of the BCAF.The Department has worked closely with the EA’s Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) in order to recently introduce an outcome based reporting approach which will see ABSIT providing the Department with termly reports in relation to the referrals they deal, the training they undertake and the associated outcomes for both.I also fully recognise the benefit of completing a new externally commission research study in order to gather valuable, up to date data on the nature and prevalence of bullying type behaviour.  I will consider the scope to commission this research within the context of the Department’s overall final budget allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he is taking to create a training and career pathway for classroom assistants.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) employs the majority of classroom assistants working in schools across Northern Ireland. The EA has commenced a strategic review of the classroom assistant employment model which includes a specific workstream on professional standards, career pathways and professional development. This work is at an early stage and is linked to the wider Special Educational Needs reform programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of schools using temporary engagement forms to recruit classroom assistants.
answer_text: Temporary engagement forms are used by schools to engage staff for emergency cover and can be used for all categories of staff, including classroom assistants. The temporary engagement process is used to provide cover for sickness absence, or where schools are unable to fill a vacancy via the formal recruitment process. While, in most cases, temporary engagements should not exceed six months, they are occasionally used for longer periods as a result of recruitment difficulties.The Education Authority has made a bid to the Executive Transformation Fund to establish an emergency register for support staff across education, similar to the supply teacher register, which, along with service reform, should mean that schools only need to use temporary engagement forms in exceptional circumstances.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of classroom assistants currently working that have not completed an Access NI check.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 21519/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for the week ending 7 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will commission research to establish the current prevalence of bullying in schools to provide a baseline to measure interventions.
answer_text: I fully recognise that it would be beneficial to complete a new externally commission research study in order to gather valuable, up to date data on the nature and prevalence of bullying type behaviour.  Such a study would help support the Department’s understanding of the profile of pupils involved in Bullying Type Behaviour (BTB), to quantify the impact, identify challenges and emerging issues in relation to bullying type behaviour, and to highlight best practice in addressing BTB in schools.  I will consider the scope to commission this research within the context of the Department’s overall final budget allocation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any request they have made of NIE Networks to provide a compensation scheme for those impacted by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: We are acutely aware of the significant impact on the thousands of people who were without power as a result of Storm Éowyn.  We met with the Managing Director from Northern Ireland Electricity Networks (NIE) on Saturday 25 January and again at the Executive meeting on Monday (27 January) and have urged him to consider how his organisation could provide a good will payment to support families and businesses impacted by the effects of Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education and Training Inspectorate is required to inspect the work of the Education Authority Sensory Service.
answer_text: The Education and Training Inspectorate exercises its functions under Article 102 of The Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986. Inspectors have a duty to: promote the highest standards of education and of professional practice among teachers and staff in schools and settings; advise the Department on any aspect of the curriculum which the Department may refer to them or on which they think advice is appropriate; and monitor, inspect and report on the nature, scope and effect of advisory and support services provided by the Education Authority in relation to the curricula and staff of grant-aided schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Education and Training Inspectorate inspections will assess how the Curriculum Framework for Children and Young People with a Visual Impairment is embedded in schools which support a pupil or pupils with a visual impairment.
answer_text: As part of the inspection process, the Education and Training Inspectorate monitors, inspects and reports on the quality of education provision for all learners within a school or organisation, including those with a visual impairment. Inspectors also work with schools and other statutory and non-statutory services to identify and share effective practice from which others may learn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the Education Authority Sensory Service supports schools with visually impaired learners to implement and embed the Curriculum Framework for Children and Young People with a Visual Impairment.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Sensory Service Qualified Teachers for Vision Impairment (QTVIs) and Qualified Habilitation Specialists (QHSs) (commissioned from Guide Dogs NI) work collaboratively to provide the additional support outlined in the Curriculum Framework for Children and Young People with a Visual Impairment (CFVI) to enable children and young people to gain the additional skills they need to access the curriculum with increasing independence.  The CFVI is deemed an effective tool in assisting schools to support pupils with vision impairment.  The EA’s Sensory Service is represented on the four nations steering group for the CFVI and the Service’s QTVIs contribute resources to the central resource bank linked to the CFVI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support available to schools that have incurred damage as a result of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) staff and contractors worked extremely hard to protect the schools’ estate and ensure those schools which incurred damage could reopen safely and as quickly as possible. Remedial works to repair damaged buildings will be ongoing in some schools for several weeks.The EA and the Department will continue to liaise with schools to provide support and ensure that any outstanding repairs or replacements are progressed as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a school's Board of Governors is required to keep a record of official complaints made by parents, students and staff.
answer_text: It is the policy of the Department of Education that each school Board of Governors should establish formal written procedures for dealing with complaints; a school’s complaints procedure should be made available to parents, and the wider school community on request.Schools are required to record and handle complaints in line with its complaints procedure.  Records of school complaints are retained and disposed of by schools in line with their retention and disposal schedule.There are also specific requirements for schools to maintain a record of complaints associated with safeguarding concerns.  Under the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act, all schools are required to keep a record of all reported instances of bullying type behaviour, the steps taken to deal with these and the outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether training in supporting visually impaired learners for those classroom assistants supporting a child with a visual impairment is mandatory.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not hold data regarding the number of Classroom Assistants (CAs) assigned to a child with a visual impairment (VI). The EA’s Sensory Service requires that all teachers and classroom assistants new to supporting a pupil with VI receive induction training delivered by Qualified Teachers of Vision Impairment (QTVIs), designed to the individual needs of the pupil.  Refresher training is provided if appropriate.Induction training includes: educational implications of VI; learning strategies; visual acuities and VI conditions (individualised); interpretation of diagnosis and medical information; assistive technology (as appropriate); font size; accessible learning resources; social and emotional needs and transition training.The EA also offers training and webinars for teachers and assistants regarding understanding the role of the QTVI, clinicians, the eye and specific eye conditions; VI and its impact; Cerebral Vision Impairment; simulation; accessible teaching and learning; creating resources and assistive technology. All special schools receive whole school training for VI.  In addition, the EA requires that CAs working with blind students complete RNIB Certified Braille, UK Association for Accessible Formats (UKAFF) Guidance on braille production and how to produce tactile diagrams.The EA also commissions Guide Dogs Northern Ireland to deliver habilitation training to teachers and CAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to evidence provided at the Committee for Education meeting on 22 January, for his assessment of the reason why his Department is unsure of how many schools have an anti-bullying policy in place, as required by the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Northern Ireland Act (NI) 2016 (the Act), which came into effect in 2021, requires schools to record incidents or alleged incidents of bullying type behaviour (BTB), their motivation and their outcome.  Each school’s Board of Governors (BoG) has responsibility to oversee the implementation of the Act in their school.   The Act places a statutory duty on a BoG collectively to take responsibility for the development, implementation, monitoring and periodic review of the school’s Addressing-Bullying policies and procedures.  The legislation does not include the requirement for schools to share their policies with the Department.Officials are progressing work on a number of fronts with a view to ensuring the Department has access to comprehensive data in order to better understand implementation of, and compliance with, the Act.The Department has included a range of questions in relation to bullying type behaviour for the first time in the 2024 School Omnibus Survey.  These questions covered the awareness of the Act and its statutory duties, effectiveness of implementation, and BTB methods and motivations.  This information will allow the Department to understand more about the impact of the Act and how best to support schools to effectively implement the Act.ETI inspectors, in all inspections, evaluate how well schools meet the needs of their pupils through the lens of ‘Health, wellbeing and keeping safe’.  As part of inspection, inspectors therefore review all pastoral policies and guidance within a school, as set out in the Department guidance, Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools.  Officials have also engaged with the ETI to ensure the inclusion of questions as part of a wider ETI pilot from September – December 2024, to help determine how schools are fulfilling their statutory obligations in relation to implementing the Act, in terms of recording of all incidents of bullying, development, implementation, monitoring and review of Addressing-Bullying policies and procedures.Schools are presently required to complete a Bullying Concern Assessment Form (BCAF) on the School Information Management System for each incident of Bullying Type Behaviour.  The BCAF records statutorily required data however, presently this data cannot be collated or anonymised.  The BCAF is being digitised as part of the Education Information Solutions (EdIS) digital change programme.  The Department is starting to explore the potential opportunities in relation to data presented by the digitisation of the BCAF, which include the potential to better measure how schools are meeting the requirements of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of classroom assistants currently assigned to a child with a visual impairment; and (ii) how many of these have accessed formal training in supporting visually impaired learners.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not hold data regarding the number of Classroom Assistants (CAs) assigned to a child with a visual impairment (VI). The EA’s Sensory Service requires that all teachers and classroom assistants new to supporting a pupil with VI receive induction training delivered by Qualified Teachers of Vision Impairment (QTVIs), designed to the individual needs of the pupil.  Refresher training is provided if appropriate.Induction training includes: educational implications of VI; learning strategies; visual acuities and VI conditions (individualised); interpretation of diagnosis and medical information; assistive technology (as appropriate); font size; accessible learning resources; social and emotional needs and transition training.The EA also offers training and webinars for teachers and assistants regarding understanding the role of the QTVI, clinicians, the eye and specific eye conditions; VI and its impact; Cerebral Vision Impairment; simulation; accessible teaching and learning; creating resources and assistive technology. All special schools receive whole school training for VI.  In addition, the EA requires that CAs working with blind students complete RNIB Certified Braille, UK Association for Accessible Formats (UKAFF) Guidance on braille production and how to produce tactile diagrams.The EA also commissions Guide Dogs Northern Ireland to deliver habilitation training to teachers and CAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the training provided to teachers that have a pupil with a visual impairment in their class; and (ii) whether this training is mandatory.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not hold data regarding the number of Classroom Assistants (CAs) assigned to a child with a visual impairment (VI). The EA’s Sensory Service requires that all teachers and classroom assistants new to supporting a pupil with VI receive induction training delivered by Qualified Teachers of Vision Impairment (QTVIs), designed to the individual needs of the pupil.  Refresher training is provided if appropriate.Induction training includes: educational implications of VI; learning strategies; visual acuities and VI conditions (individualised); interpretation of diagnosis and medical information; assistive technology (as appropriate); font size; accessible learning resources; social and emotional needs and transition training.The EA also offers training and webinars for teachers and assistants regarding understanding the role of the QTVI, clinicians, the eye and specific eye conditions; VI and its impact; Cerebral Vision Impairment; simulation; accessible teaching and learning; creating resources and assistive technology. All special schools receive whole school training for VI.
