In addition, the EA requires that CAs working with blind students complete RNIB Certified Braille, UK Association for Accessible Formats (UKAFF) Guidance on braille production and how to produce tactile diagrams.The EA also commissions Guide Dogs Northern Ireland to deliver habilitation training to teachers and CAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consider funding the development and implementation of a Northern Ireland-wide anti-bullying programme, similar to the FUSE programme in Ireland.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in September 2021. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority (EA) was established in September 2022 and provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT has a focus on preventative education to embed cultures that eradicate bullying. The team also provides children, young people and parents/carers, with support to deal with specific incidents of bullying. ABSIT’s Whole School Leadership Training offer is a comprehensive professional development programme for Safeguarding, Social, Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing (SBEW) pastoral leads and SENCOs/Learning Support Coordinators in schools. This is delivered using a blended, modular approach that has been co-developed with 140 schools and delivered to over 250 schools in the region who have participated to date.The purpose of the leadership training is to build the confidence and ability of school leaders to align and implement an evidence-based, whole school approach to support children and young people, to address statutory requirements and embed best practice. This training includes modules on legislation, research, policy, systems/processes, assessment, preventative approaches, effective responses, stakeholder engagement and communication. The Department and ABSIT are open to exploring best practice in other jurisdictions, including FUSE, which could complement the comprehensive, schoolwide approach ABSIT has developed through its whole school leadership training and other existing relevant provision such as the Safer Schools NI App.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of Educational Psychologists that have left the Education Authority in the last five years.
answer_text: The table below details the number of Educational Psychologists (EPs) that have left the Education Authority’s (EA) Educational Psychology Service (EPS) in each of the last five financial years (April–March).YearNo of EPs who have left EA2020-2192021-2262022-23112023-2492024-2515

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is considering increasing the bursary available to those undertaking the Doctorate in Educational, Child and Adolescent Psychology.
answer_text: Since 2006 the Department of Education has funded student places on the Doctorate in Educational, Child and Adolescent Psychology (DECAP) course. The course is provided by Queen’s University Belfast, and it is funded through an approved three-year business case.Department officials are currently finalising the business case for the 2025/26 to 2027/28 period.  This business case will consider a range of options for the delivery of the course, including the bursary level.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people (i) eligible for the NHS Healthy Start programme; and (ii) currently receiving support through the programme.
answer_text: As of 23 December 2024, 10,266 people in Northern Ireland are receiving support from the scheme. This data is published monthly on the NHS Healthy Start website.Unfortunately, data is not currently available for the number of people eligible for the NHS Healthy Start scheme. This is due to an issue with the data provided to the NHS Business Services Authority, administering the scheme on our behalf.  This data is used to calculate uptake for the scheme. The current data issues do not prevent anyone from joining the scheme or continuing to access the scheme if they are eligible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he will support the Department of Education to deliver the commitment to the development of a Speech, Language and Communications Intervention Programme which will target 10,000 children over the next three academic years, as referenced in the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda.
answer_text: The development of a Speech, Language and Communications Intervention Programme was announced by the Minister for Education in his statement to the Assembly on 13 January 2025.   This work will be progressed through the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan which will be published in the coming weeks. Officials from Health and Education continue to work together on refining a system that will include clear and positive ambitions for our children with Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether costs have been modelled for the introduction of a subsidy scheme for childcare providers to support with staff salaries.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy (DfE) is leading a scoping exercise to develop a clearer picture of the business support requirements of the childcare sector and help inform the appropriate government response.  The cost pressures faced by providers, including those related to staff salaries will be considered as part of this work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when their next meeting is scheduled with the MLK Development Corporation.
answer_text: We met with the MLKDC Board in July 2024 and continue to work with Board on the way forward. Officials from our department are in regular contact with MLKDC and meet formally with the MLKDC senior management team on a quarterly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will provide a plan for how the £50 million Executive funding for childcare will be spent.
answer_text: The draft 2025/26 Budget is currently out for consultation.  Once the funding for Early Learning and Childcare has been finalised, I will bring forward proposals to my Executive colleagues for its allocation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department ensures proactive, consistent and effective communication in relation to disruption on the road network.
answer_text: My Department has approximately 200 CCTV cameras across Northern Ireland that allows my officials to observe road conditions/disruptions. We also work closely with the PSNI and they provide information on some collisions that are likely to impact the road network.Once an incident is confirmed on the road network and my officials are aware, road users are made aware of the incident by placing information on any of the variable message signs, if any are located in the area. Depending on the type of incident information is placed on the TrafficWatchNI website (which had almost 10 million page visits in 2024), X account (which has over 85,000 followers) or issuing emails to website subscribers (of which there are 1,686).Officials also work closely with media outlets to provide this information to the public. My Department also processes approximately 22,000 applications for road or lane closure applications each year.  Those planned roadworks that are expected to affect the public most are displayed on my Department’s TrafficWatchNI website.Given the volume of closures, communication with road users including elected representatives in relation to upcoming roadworks is focused on the busiest routes, primarily the 'A' Class network.  Communications used include use of road-side signs on the impacted route in advance of the works, as well as notification on TrafficWatchNI. In some cases, this notification is extended to some busier lower tier roads as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the date he will publish (i) the Special Educational Needs Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan; and (ii) the associated outcomes framework.
answer_text: The Special Educational Needs (SEN) Reform Agenda and associated Delivery Plan will be published in the coming weeks.Officials are working together on refining the outcomes framework that will include clear and positive ambitions for our children with SEN and set out how we will measure our progress towards meeting those.  I intend to bring this forward as an early action as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the review of Healthy Child, Healthy Future will be published.
answer_text: Work is ongoing to refresh the Healthy Child, Healthy Future framework.  It is envisaged that the revised framework will be published by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current number of health visitor job vacancies, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The information on Health and Social Care (HSC) vacancies actively being recruited by staff group and organisation is published by my Department on a quarterly basis.  The latest available information refers to 30 September 2024 and can be found at the following link:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/dhssps-statistics-and-research/workforce-statistics A breakdown of health visitor vacancies actively being recruited by HSC Trust as at 30 September 2024, is detailed in the table below:HSC Health Visitor Vacancies Actively Being Recruited as at 30 September 2024HSC TrustVacancies Actively Being RecruitedBelfast5Northern4South Eastern6Southern6Western2Total23Source: Information & Analysis Directorate, DoH

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the selection process for the experts on the International Ministerial Advisory Panel on Curriculum and Assessment in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the remuneration each will receive.
answer_text: I have appointed prominent individuals with a wealth of knowledge, experience and expertise in matters of education policy, particularly curriculum and assessment to the International Ministerial Advisory Panel via Direct Award Contracts. All candidates were assessed as suitable for appointment by officials. Panel members will receive a daily rate of £500 for their professional time and advice.  Members will only be remunerated for days worked. This the same level as the Chairs of the Independent Review of Education and the Education Underachievement Panel and less than the per diem rate for a Director in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when the trial of special educational needs classroom assistants within preschool education settings will commence.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 20501/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 24 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, further to his oral statement on special educational needs reform on 13 January 2025, to detail (i) when the bespoke regional programme for 2-3 year olds will be in place; and (ii) whether this will be available to all 2-3 year olds.
answer_text: I refer the member to AQW 20500/22-27. The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 24 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the practice of treating children with special educational needs and disabilities as supernumerary will end as part of his special educational needs reforms.
answer_text: Children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) are currently considered as supernumerary for admissions purposes, meaning a child who holds a statement can access the school most suitable for their needs regardless of whether places remain available through the open enrolment procedure. While this remains the existing practice, my officials are in the process of bringing proposals to me for my consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will continue beyond the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the answer to question AQW 20468/22-27.   The answer is contained in the Written Answers Booklet for week ending 17 January 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19439/22-27, how National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines are being followed in relation to hybrid closed loop for diabetics who have reliability issues with freestyle Libre sensors and can only use Dexcom sensors.
answer_text: The most appropriate glucose monitoring system for each individual is the responsibility of the Health and Social Care Trust and their clinicians to determine, in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  Continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the NI Drug Tariff can be provided.  Two new sensors (Freestyle Libre Plus® and Dexcom One Plus®) were recently added to the NI Drug Tariff.  The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 on Hybrid Closed Loop systems, has been endorsed as applicable to NI. My Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications, given the scale of investment needed.   Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made to support the integration of continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 19439/22-27, why the Dexcom G6 and G7 are not on the Drug Tariff list when the Dexcom one and one+ are on the list.
answer_text: There are many glucose monitoring systems available from various companies to support people living with diabetes. I am aware of the significant benefits these devices can provide to people living with diabetes.  The most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the Health and Social Care Trust and their clinicians to determine, in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  There is an application and assessment process for the inclusion of devices onto the NI Drug Tariff list - my Department cannot add devices to the NI Drug Tariff list. It is the manufacturers of Continuous Glucose Monitors who have to complete this process and consequently, provide access. The Business Services Organisation manage this function within Health and Social Care on behalf of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the occasions in which he has made a decision on a development proposal that is contrary to the recommendation of officials since the beginning of the mandate.
answer_text: I have taken decisions which have not followed the recommendations made to me by officials on three Development Proposals (DP).  These are  DP720 (proposed closure of Queen Elizabeth II Primary School), DP 728 (proposed transformation to controlled integrated status of Rathmore Primary School) and DP 727 (proposed transformation to controlled integrated status of Bangor Academy and Sixth Form College).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to address the number of children not being seen by a health visitor for their one year review.
answer_text: The most recent version of the “Healthy Child, Healthy Future (HCHF): Health Review Statistics for Northern Ireland 2023/24”, provides data in respect of the coverage and timeliness of all Health Visiting pre-school health contacts. The information contained within the report is retrospective and pertains to the 2023/24 year.  During the period covered by this report, Health Visiting services in some of our HSC Trusts faced significant workforce challenges, with a large number of unfilled vacancies. Work has been ongoing within Trusts to address this with workforce planning and recruitment exercises, and prioritisation of HCHF contacts as appropriate. Any decision to stand down any element of core health visiting contacts, as defined within the existing HCHF framework, will be subject to a risk assessment by the relevant HSC Trust. I remain committed to ensuring that every child is given the best start in life.  Driving this commitment is the work currently progressing on the refresh of the “Healthy Child, Healthy Future” framework, to ensure it reflects current research evidence and best practice.  I anticipate that this refresh will be completed by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why the Southern Health and Social Care Trust recorded 46 per cent of one year health reviews as no contact for 2023-24.
answer_text: Over recent years the Health Visiting Service in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust (SHSCT) area has experienced significant workforce challenges, with a large number of unfilled vacancies. As noted in the published statistics for 2023-24, this situation impacted on the Trust’s ability to fully deliver 1-year reviews as recommended in the Healthy Child Healthy Future policy.The Trust has worked to increase recruitment to its complement of health visitors in order to allow it to deliver the services required to be provided as part of the framework. Consequently, the Trust has been able to resume full provision of all the framework contact interventions.   Throughout this period, the Trust has taken action to assess, manage and mitigate the risks involved. It has also reviewed its Health Visiting caseload and provided assurance that, in any case where the 1-year review was not carried out, an appropriate in-person review has now taken place.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the findings of the Good Relations Indicators 2023 report, published in December 2024, which found that the percentage of 16-year-olds who think that relations between Protestants and Catholics are better now than they were five years ago has declined in the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Education whether the new Local IMPACT Teams will have health professionals as part of the team.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:The Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Local IMPACT Teams (LITs) model roll-out will initially involve restructuring the EAs existing separate SEND support services and for September 2025 this will not directly include health services.  However, the long-term vision for the model has always been to maximise local operational co-ordination with relevant health services and other non-SEND EA services.The geographic network of the LITs has been aligned to Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust areas to enable this in future and work is currently underway in terms of aligning the LIT operating model with those of the Regional Integrated Support for Education (RISE) offers within each Trust.  Work is also underway in terms of regionalising the EA's speech and language class provision in alignment with relevant Trust operating models and the LITs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of the 2025-2026 draft budget on his Department.
answer_text: This is a very difficult proposed outcome for DAERA given the significant challenges facing my Department.		On Resource DEL, my initial assessment is while I welcome the proposed Executive earmarking of the £332.5 million for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development that was previously earmarked by HM Treasury for these purposes, I am disappointed that there has not been an inflationary uplift in this funding.  However, I recognise the very constrained funding available to the Executive.				In relation to the proposed £15.7 million general allocation to my Department, this is significantly less than my £50.9 million bids.  This would mean that there is insufficient funding to cover DAERA’s statutory obligations such as bTB Compensation, contractual pressures on pay and running costs, and important new work on Climate Change and the Environmental Improvement Plan including Lough Neagh.  		On Capital DEL, I welcome the proposed Executive earmarked allocation of £12.3 million to DAERA for a just transition fund for agriculture.  However, the proposed £119.5 million total Capital DEL allocation is £7.1 million less than my Department’s £126.6 million inescapable bids and, while that is disappointing, it again reflects the wider availability of such funding for the Executive.This also means that my Department would not receive any funding to allocate to my £52.7 million high priority Capital DEL bids which include Lough Neagh and the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation programme		I will therefore have to make difficult decisions on what can be funded.  If no further funding is available for my Department at Final Budget stage I will be seeking additional Resource DEL and Capital DEL allocations at in-year Monitoring Rounds in 2025-26.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any guidelines in place for GPs to prescribe medication recommended by a private specialist consultant.
answer_text: The legacy Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) issued correspondence on a number of occasions to private providers and general practice, most recently in June 2021, regarding requests for HSC prescriptions following private consultation. The purpose of this correspondence was to remind both sectors of their clinical responsibilities in relation to prescribing of medications to patients under their care. In particular, the limitations with regards to requests to GPs for transfer of ongoing prescribing of medication which for the patient’s GP may not be clinically acceptable.  This guidance still applies.  	To further clarify appropriate arrangements, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) issued ‘Private (non-HSC) Prescribing Requests Guidance for Primary Care’ in September 2024.  The new guidance (attached) provides a reminder that the legal responsibility for prescribing lies with the prescriber who signs the prescription, and it is the responsibility of the individual prescriber to prescribe within their own level of competence and scope of practice.The prescription of any medication associated with private healthcare should be managed by the private provider. Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to prescribe, in instances where a GP considers that the medication being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) guidance on Good Medical Practice, then they may choose to prescribe.  Supplementary advice to GP practices, relating to pre and/or post treatment support in primary care to individuals procuring private healthcare services, was issued by my department 20 November 2024.  It advises that, where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that:The individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package;The individual is informed that they may have to pay for ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether TNC2011/2 Policy Statement on Tackling Violence and Abusive Behaviour against Teachers has been implemented.
answer_text: TNC 2011/2 Policy Statement on Tackling Violence and Abusive Behaviour against Teachers was agreed through the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) in 2011 and was issued to all schools. It is also published on the Department’s web site.As you are aware the Department of Education (DE) is not the employer of teachers.  Teachers are employed by the Board of Governors for each school setting, with human resource matters carried out in consultation with the relevant employing authority.The Education Authority and CCMS, along with the other sectoral bodies, are in the process of finalising Violence at Work Policy and Guidance in consultation with Trade Unions. It is anticipated that the policy and guidance will be issued in February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the budgetary pressures facing her Department in 2025-2026.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Women's Health Action Plan.
answer_text: The purpose of the Women’s Health Plan, which my Department is currently developing, is to highlight ongoing policy and service development initiatives, and to outline the actions and improvements that can be delivered over the next three years to improve health outcomes for women. The focus to date has been on mapping the work that is already under way across the health and social care system (including, for example improving maternity, gynae and menopause services, cervical screening and breast cancer services, and planning for a mother and baby unit) and identifying areas where further action is needed (for example, improving public health awareness around women’s health, providing more training and education for healthcare professionals on these issues, promoting healthy lifestyles and tackling health inequality). My Department will be hosting a number of engagement events commencing this month to listen and share insights & priorities with a diverse range of women and health professionals to further inform the development of the Action Plan.  The pace and extent of delivery will be determined by our available capacity and resources, which remain under extreme pressure. You may also be aware that my Department part-funded the Women’s Health Survey for Northern Ireland which launched on 11 November 2024.  The survey is being carried out by the School of Nursing & Midwifery at Queen’s University, Belfast, in partnership with Derry Well Women.  Its purpose is to hear directly from women about their specific health needs and experiences to help shape planning for women’s health services.  The survey will remain open into February 2025 and I would be grateful for your support in sharing the survey and encouraging as many women as possible to take this opportunity to share their views.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the publication of the Cervical Cytology Review reports in December 2024, whether he will agree to a statutory public inquiry.
answer_text: Following the publication of the two factual reports a detailed analysis of the findings of the Cervical Cytology Review Outcomes report will be commissioned by the PHA. It is expected that this analysis, supported by appropriate independent experts, will be completed in January 2025.  These reports will help inform my consideration of the next steps.  As Health Minister I remain committed to finding the most appropriate method to understand what happened and to learn lessons to ensure that our screening programmes deliver on behalf of our population. These reports will help inform my consideration of the next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any actions his Department has taken under goal 2.1 of the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy; and (ii) any evidence of progress on this goal.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided me with a response.Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children on the waiting list for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery, broken down by month in the last two years.
answer_text: As of 13th December 2024, 66 children are on the Percutaneous Gastrostomy Surgery waiting list.My officials are not in a position to provide procedure level waiting list information over time as this is not readily available and could only be obtained at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he is taking to address the paediatric waiting list for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: There are a number of actions ongoing between my officials and Belfast Health and Social Services Trust (BHSCT) staff to reduce the current waiting times for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) surgery and to find a sustainable solution to this complex issue. Actions include:Belfast Trust have commenced additional clinics and theatre sessions to help reduce paediatric waiting times and additional Paediatric Gastrostomy procedures have been approved through waiting list initiative funding. The Trust have carried out a number of additional weekend theatre sessions to help address the backlog and it is hoped that additional sessions will be available going forward dependent on theatre, bed and staffing capacity.  Following a clinical validation exercise it is estimated that around 25 children would be suitable to transfer to an NHS Provider in Great Britain (GB) to receive their surgery. Discussions are ongoing with a number of NHS paediatric providers. Capacity is being sought for the child’s complete pathway including a pre-assessment clinic for these complex children, theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and a follow up appointment. This is required to ensure continuity of care for the children and their parents. A small number of procedures were also carried out in the Ulster Hospital by Belfast Trust paediatric surgeons. This pathway is being reviewed to determine if this would offer a sustainable solution or provide a short-term solution to reduce lists. Discussions between the two units are ongoing.The Republic of Ireland (ROI) have confirmed that they do not have capacity at this time to carry out PEG Tube Surgery for children from NI due to their own pressures. Belfast Trust have also entered into consultation with Independent Sector (IS) Providers in Northern Ireland to explore if there is a provider in a position to safely deliver the necessary arrangements for the child’s complete pathway including a pre-assessment clinic, theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and a follow up appointment. The IS Providers have indicated that they are not in a position to provide this service, at this time. Whilst RBHSC has been facing increased winter pressures as a result of the enhanced winter planning they have been able to maintain their elective lists, which would include the PEG tube insertions. In previous years elective sessions were cancelled during winter pressure periods.There is significant work underway between the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeons, the Child Health Partnership and District General Hospital (DGH) surgical units to consider what surgery needs to be carried out in RBHSC and what can be carried out locally at DGHs. A number of elective theatre lists have already been moved to District General Hospitals, and this work in

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department will fund the aftercare following paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.
answer_text: Patient who have a paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery in RBHSC have access to theatre capacity, inpatient bed capacity (children stay between 48-72 hours), parental training to manage the peg tube, equipment and food, and follow up appointments to manage the children’s follow up care. This is required to ensure continuity of care for the children and their parents. However, there are only a small number of specialist nurses in RBHSC who provide this care and as such this can be a limiting factor when there is a backlog of patients receiving treatment. Officials have asked Belfast Health and Social Services Trust staff to consider options for addressing the current backlog of children waiting for paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery.  Once this is provided my officials from the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) will consider this plan as a matter of urgency.Wider discussions are also underway to consider the additional recurrent need for children’s services including the need for pre-assessment clinics for these complex children and other complex cases, the need for additional specialist nursing capacity in RBHSC and for the expansion of nursing roles in local hospital paediatric units.However, this will be challenging within the current nursing and financial constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools that have been formally consulted as part of developing the End to End Review of Special Educational Needs; (ii) the number Special Educational Needs Co-ordinators involved in its production; and (iii) the number of families and young people formally consulted.
answer_text: In informing the scrutiny report recommendations on the special educational needs (SEN) system, most recently the Independent Review of Education and Independent Review of SEN Services and Processes, extensive stakeholder engagement was conducted across the system.  The recommendations derived from these reports underpinned the design of the End to End (E2E) Review of SEN, highlighting the importance of integrating stakeholder insights into the reform process.  As part of the E2E Review of SEN, a programme of targeted stakeholder engagement was also completed.  At the outset, individuals with an interest in the Review were given the opportunity to register online and specify their area of interest.  Engagement events were also advertised on Department of Education (DE) and Education Authority (EA) social media channels. Officials conducted workshops for key stakeholder groups, including children and young people, to ascertain their views on the support provided to them; with schools to understand how they support children and young people with SEN and how the staff are supported; and with parents/carers about support and advice available, each group was also asked to identify improvements that could be made.  Individual school visits were also undertaken to discuss a number of issues and to find potential solutions from the E2E Review of SEN.  The majority of school visits were conducted with school leaders and SEN Co-ordinators (SENCos).  Officials also met separately with a cluster group of nine SENCos to discuss a number of proposals.  Two surveys were also conducted to gather views from key stakeholders, with 270 responses received.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 18006/22-27, to detail the number of referrals to the Education Authority's Educational Psychology Service from a designated medical officer that have been (i) taken forward; and (ii) rejected in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) 	The Education Authority (EA) has provided the data on referrals to Educational Psychology Services (EPS) made by Designated Medical Officers which include Speech and Language Therapists (SLTs), over the last five years:2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24TotalReferrals to EPS from designated Medical Officers8388098596505943,750(ii) 	The EA does not hold data on cases that have been rejected centrally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, as part of the Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme, whether schools applying for the outdoor covered sports spaces will have to demonstrate that these facilities will be open to the wider community where it is safe and possible to do so.
answer_text: The primary aim of the new curriculum-led capital investment programme is to invest in priority areas that will directly support, enable and enhance delivery of the curriculum to support high-quality learning and teaching. Where this investment would also benefit the wider community outside of school core usage, schools are encouraged to make their premises available as a community facility, where possible. The eligibility criteria for both the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces in primary schools and the programme for synthetic pitches and sports halls in the post-primary sector will be set out by my department in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the announcement that all post-primary and special schools will receive new desktop computers, (i) whether consideration was given as to when schools last updated desktop computers; and (ii) whether schools were consulted if they needed other hardware instead.
answer_text: Desktop devices are being procured to replace end of life devices in post-primary and special schools to ensure that students have access to secure, modern technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 16788/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 19171/22-27, (i) to detail the 11 Development Proposals awaiting his decision; and (ii) how long each proposal has been waiting for his decision to be made.
answer_text: I have taken decisions on Development Proposals that were with me for consideration. The decisions can be accessed on the Department’s website. Development Proposal Decisions 2025 | Department of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered a scheme where those with diabetes who are experienced hybrid closed loop (HCL) pump users could act as service ambassadors to provide managed peer support to service users that have just started using HCL pumps.
answer_text: The role of service users, working alongside the Diabetes Network, sharing lived experiences of diabetes, has set the direction of the Network’s efforts to improve diabetes treatment and care.  Indeed, in the setting up of the Diabetes Network, provision was made for a collective leadership platform, to support real change in improving outcomes for people living with diabetes.  The Regional Insulin Pump Service is a clinical model currently delivered in Northern Ireland to provide equity, reduce variability, and enhance patient outcomes.  People living with diabetes involved in the codesign of the Regional Insulin Pump service, reflected the need for ‘quality of life’ to be a key principle. Acceptance of this recommendation made Northern Ireland the first region among the four nations to use this.  The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Hybrid Closed Loop systems guidance states the support of a trained multidisciplinary team experienced in insulin pumps and continuous glucose monitoring is required.  In addition, the role of peer support is also being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether additional financial support and services for parents with multiples is being considered as part of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: The specific needs of parents with multiples will be considered as part of the development of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy. In recognition that implementing an ELC Strategy will take time, I have introduced a range of measures this year which have already reduced the costs of childcare for eligible parents, including those with multiples.   The Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme has been designed to ease the financial pressures on households and to help more parents remain in work, and the subsidy is available per child.The Sure Start programme operates a Progressive Universalism model which ensures that the individual needs of Sure Start children and families are identified and supported, including families with multiple births.  I have provided an additional £3.1m to Sure Start in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to upgrade blood sugar sensors used by those with diabetes to ensure they are hybrid closed loop capable.
answer_text: Continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the NI Drug Tariff can be provided, with the most appropriate system determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 on Hybrid Closed Loop systems, has been endorsed as applicable to NI.My Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered.Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered.Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any additional specific support being provided to parents with multiples, both pre-birth and post-birth.
answer_text: Support varies from Trust to Trust, therefore I have provided a response from each Trust below:  Belfast HSC Trust:The Royal Jubilee Maternity Service provides dedicated specialist Twins and Multiples Antenatal Clinics which are led by Consultant Obstetricians who specialise in multiple pregnancy care.  There is a dedicated antenatal twins and multiples specific antenatal education programme provided for women and their families.   The Service provides dedicated twins and multiples infant feeding advisory support in Maternity and Neonatal settings. South Eastern HSC TrustIn terms of antenatal care - there is a twin specific educational parent-craft evening every 3 months, these are in addition to all the other parent-craft and supports we have in place for every pregnant patient.From an antenatal perspective all parents of multiples are seen at the twins/multiples clinic in Maternity Out Patients Department, which is consultant led.  There are two midwives allocated to this clinic to provide continuity and these midwives provide an antenatal education class tailored to the needs of parents expecting multiples.  The classes are also supported by a consultant obstetrician, a member of the neonatal team and a representative from the Charity ‘Tiny Life’.  Parents are also encouraged to attend the other antenatal education classes breastfeeding workshops within the Trust to help them prepare for birth and the early postnatal period. Mothers are also directed to the ‘Twins Trust’, which is active in Northern Ireland and can provide ongoing support.  Postnatal hospital care is tailored to the individual needs of the babies and that of the mother.  Community midwives will offer postnatal visits as required on an individualised basis to offer advice and support.Northern HSC Trust:Northern Trust have a dedicated multiples clinic run by a specific team of staff including Obstetricians with a special interest in multiples, midwives and a dedicated sonographer who works alongside the clinic. Parents receive support from a team of staff who they see regularly and receive the benefits of continuity care, have early access to the clinic upon confirmation of a multiple pregnancy and have regular, specialised care in accordance with their pregnancy.  This clinic has benefited from working with the Twins Trust and successfully completed an audit to measure the clinic against the recommendations detailed in NICE guideline for multiples NG137.  In addition, there are antenatal education sessions which are specifically tailored to provide information for multiple pregnancies. This is coordinated by the antenatal education midwife alongside the midwife working in the clinic, with input from Tiny Life, a charity for premature babies.Southern HSC TrustThe Southern Health and Social Care Trust run six information evenings for all multiple pregnancy ladies pre-birth.  These face to face sessions are delivered by the inter disciplinary team, consisting of:SHSCT Midwife/ (Mu

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on any work being undertaken to address the issues that people are having in accessing GP appointments.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of access to GP services for patients.  In response to increasing patient demand GPs are utilising a mixed consultation model with telephone consultations, video and face to face consultations as a means of managing the rising demand on services within the existing workforce while retaining responsiveness to those patients who need the most urgent attention and care.As independent contractors, who provide primary medical services to their registered list of patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations, and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients.  This also means that decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including the use of technology, telephony and the appointment system, are determined locally by the GP practice within the constraints of their available workforce.Additional measures have been put in place to support primary care to adapt flexibly to increasing demand.  These include focusing on increasing capacity to meet the demand from patients through, for example, reinforcing the GP workforce and increasing the range of healthcare professionals working in primary care, investment in general practice and the implementation of new and innovative ways of working, including making greater use of technology and telephony.  In recent years the Department has made significant funding available to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and employ additional staff to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.As part of supporting work in this area, my Department has also established a GP Access Working Group to explore issues relating to access to services and develop a programme of work to help address these and improve the experience for patients.The Working Group includes representatives from General Practice and service users among its membership.  As part of this, work has commenced on developing guidance for practices in management of patient demand and optimising workflow within practices to maximise efficiency.  The Group is also considering how technology can help to deliver improvements to the process for accessing services for patients and practices alike.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the budget provided to the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team each year since its creation.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in September 2021. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT), within the Education Authority, was established in September 2022.The Department’s total funding for ABSIT for the 2022/23 financial year was £275,000. As a consequence of wider budgetary pressures across the Department, in financial year 2023/24, funding was reduced to £270,000. For the current financial year, 2024/25, funding has remained at £270,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the 19 Current Development Proposals, including when decisions on each will be taken.
answer_text: There are currently 21 Development Proposals (DPs) with my Department for consideration.  Eleven of these are awaiting my decision, two discontinuance proposals are being held due to forthcoming proposals for Transformation and the other eight are being progressed by my officials.As I am sure you will be aware, the assessment of any DP is a complex process and requires consideration of all the evidence to ensure decisions are taken in the best interests of all children and to ensure they have access to high quality education that is viable and sustainable in the long term.I am unable to provide a precise timeframe as to when decisions will be taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all pre-schools that will be part of the Pre-school Education Standardisation Programme have been given a timeline of when they will move to standardised hours.
answer_text: It is not possible to provide a timeline for individual settings at this stage, other than those that I have announced will transition to offer at least 22.5 hours pre-school education provision per week from September 2025.It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current number of vacancies for social workers in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust's Children's Disability Community Team.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust has indicated that there are currently 5 social work vacancies within the Children’s Disability Teams. The Trust has been actively trying to fill these posts but with very limited success. The majority of social work vacancies in Trusts are filled between June and August each year, when student social workers qualify from our local Universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children presenting to mainstream settings who are non-speaking in each of the last three years.
answer_text: This information is not held by the Department of Education (DE).

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their assessment of the number of jobs that could be created from the development of the Maze Long Kesh site.
answer_text: We recognise that the Maze Long Kesh site has potential to deliver significant employment opportunities, and to act as a catalyst for further development in the surrounding area. The potential number of jobs will depend on the detail of any proposals that emerge for the site.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a chairperson and full board will be appointed to the Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation.
answer_text: Officials are reviewing the options for appointment of a Chair and additional Board Members to the Maze/Long Kesh Development Corporation and we expect to receive updated advice in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department has provided schools with guidance on requests from private educational psychologists to observe pupils in schools and for teachers to complete paperwork for assessments.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) issued guidance to schools on the use of educational psychology private reports as part of the Special Educational Needs (SEN) & Medical Categories Training in 2018, which has been shared on request and at training sessions where required since then.The guidance notes that the school is not obliged to permit any private assessor into the school to undertake an observation, that this is at the discretion of the principal and each situation needs to be considered on an individual basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he has seen a draft of the Department of Education's End to End Review of Special Educational Needs report; and (ii) to what extent his Department has been involved in its production.
answer_text: My Department is supportive that the End-to-End Review is the key driver of transformation and cross-departmental collaboration for Special Educational Needs.A summary report and a draft delivery plan has been shared with officials. It will be presented for my consideration following a meeting of senior officials between both departments to finalise actions.Departmental and Public Health Agency officials have been fully embedded in several DE’s structures as part of the review and oversight of the policy interface. These structures have provided several opportunities to share information across departmental boundaries and inform the production of the report and delivery plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the work his Department is undertaking to support sports clubs in making access to sports and physical activity more affordable.
answer_text: Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, has responsibility for the development of sport across Northern Ireland.  I asked the Chief Executive of Sport NI for details of relevant programmes which they offer.  He has advised me as follows:“In 2023 Sport Northern Ireland (Sport NI) launched a Renewable Energy Fund (pilot scheme), a £1million pound National Lottery funded investment programme with two primary objectives:“To reduce the environmental impact of sports club across Northern Ireland.“Develop SNI’s understanding of the needs of the sports sector and how environmental interventions can impact the operations of sports clubs; the knowledge gained will inform how we invest in the future.“The fund also seeks to reduce carbon emissions, produce greener sports clubs, make savings on energy bills, and create a more financially sustainable club sector.  Although the programme does not stipulate how a club should use their savings, it is intended that should there be future iterations, savings be redirected into sports development.”“Sport NI recently launched the Creating Opportunities Crowdfunder small grants programme, which is available to local clubs. Clubs can apply for funding for any new and/or additional participation projects they wish to run within their club. The programme offers up to 50% match-funding support to a maximum of £5,000 from Sport NI through the Crowdfunder platform. Eligible costs include (but are not exclusive to) coaching costs, venue hire, workshops costs, equipment and capital costs required to deliver their participation project. “In 2024/25 the Crowdfunder programme has approved £141,015 of match funding to a total of 31 different approved projects.“Please also see web link for more information on Creating Opportunities Crowdfunder: http://www.sportni.net/funding/creating-opportunities/”The Chief Executive also advised me of the following programmes which Sport NI has supported with Exchequer funding under District Council Community Planning:“10 applications were received from Local Authorities (Ards & North Down didn’t apply). 6 applications at this stage have been deemed to be successful within the available budget to facilitate sport and physical activity opportunities. These are as follows;“Armagh Banbridge and Craigavon’s ‘Get Moving ABC’. The proposed project focusses on Walking Netball, Multi use games area programmes, a girls active programme and a local basketball pathway programme.“Mid Ulster’s Promoting Inclusive Sport and Physical Activity in Mid Ulster. The proposed project aims to develop a range of sport and physical activity projects such as swimming and multi activity for neurodivergent people along with several forest walks for older people.“Lisburn & Castlereagh’s Commit to Fit 2025 programme. This directly links sport and physical activity projects into 3 of the 5 themes within the Community Plan namely:“Children and Young People“Health and Well Being“Our Community“Antrim and Newtow

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department is taking to support school leaders with their workload.
answer_text: The teachers' Workload Agreement TNC 2024/2 was jointly developed and agreed by both the teaching trade unions (NITC) and Management Side of TNC.The agreement clarifies the legislative terms and conditions of teaching staff including working hours, teaching time, cover arrangements and supervision.The guidance outlined in the agreement has been developed to provide a framework for Principals and Boards of Governors to ensure the allocation and distribution of duties to all Principals, Vice Principals and Teachers is managed in a fair and reasonable manner that enables staff to observe their contractual requirements with sufficient time to enjoy a reasonable work/life balance.The Workload Agreement TNC 2024/2 is effective in providing clarification, advice and guidance on teachers' terms and conditions to Boards of Governors, Principals and Teachers.In April 2024 a joint commitment between Management Side and NITC was given to design a framework to address the recommendations contained in the 8 completed workforce reviews. A timetable for the implementation of those areas which can be progressed, both immediately and over a short to medium term has now been jointly agreed with the trade unions. The focus by both Management Side and the NITC is to determine the areas of work which, once revised and implemented, will have the potential to impact positively on working practices within the education sector.A programme of work has been prepared grouping the recommendations which are in progress and those that can be progressed with existing resources into themes, one of which is School Leaders.One of the original major areas of contention was the workload associated with inspections and through positive engagement between NITC and the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) the processes have now moved towards a position of greater ongoing professional support and dialogue with an aim of supporting school improvement.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether there have been any recent changes to security arrangements and protocols for guests and visitors to Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on maternity services in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust continue to provide the full range of Maternity care services for pregnant women.   Maternity Services are provided from the following: The Ulster Maternity Hospital provides Maternity Outpatients services, Emergency Obstetric Unit, an Alongside Midwifery Unit and Labour Ward.  The Lagan Valley and Downe Midwifery Led Units are bases for Community Midwives, but also provide both obstetric and midwifery led antenatal clinics.Community Midwifery care is also delivered from hubs in Bangor and Ards & Holywood (Greenway team).The total births in the Ulster Hospital in 2023/24 (as per latest published inpatient activity statistics) was 3,612 which equates to 18.2% of overall hospital births across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to provide hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943, Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes, has been endorsed as applicable in Northern Ireland. Subsequently, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications of this decision.  Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, are being considered. Given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered, as funding is required for devices as well as resources to upskill and recruit the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to remedy the new IT system to ensure that medication does not drop off a patient's prescription if it is not renewed within a five day period.
answer_text: The encompass pharmacy team have worked with the Antimicrobial Stewardship Team from across the region to agree how the encompass clinical decision support tool is configured. As recommended, the encompass system includes a default duration of five days for all antimicrobials.  The prescribing clinician can change this duration at the point of prescribing and any prescribed order can be modified at any time. A number of prompts has been built into the system to support clinicians to actively manage orders for antimicrobials within the first five days of treatment after 48, 72 and 96 hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any further action that can be taken to improve traffic congestion in Moira.
answer_text: My Department has previously introduced measures to improve traffic flow and road safety in Moira including the signalisation of the Main Street/Meeting Street junction, a road marking scheme to provide right turn pockets into local retail sites and a garage outlet between Backwood Road and the Old Kilmore Road junction.		We have also looked at a number of options to prevent traffic from queuing back to the M1 Westbound Motorway off-slip in the evening peak.  To date, the costs of these options have been considered too prohibitive to be progressed further at this time. 		The village’s future transport needs, being situated within the Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council area, will be considered as part of the new Eastern Transport Plan being developed by the Department.  The plan will take account of the growth ambitions of Lisburn and Castlereagh city and other councils in the plan catchment area and will consider potential transport interventions for Moira.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Northern Ireland will commence participation in the National Diabetes Audits, as agreed in the Diabetes Strategic Framework of November 2016.
answer_text: The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales.The Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  However, there have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit.With the full implementation of Encompass, currently being rolled out across all Health and Social Care Trusts, the provision of data that is both available and extractable for the region will be available.  This will support participation in the National Diabetes Audit in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will commit to introducing legislation for post-19 special educational needs provision in the 2022-2027 mandate.
answer_text: I want every young person to have the opportunity to access skills and training opportunities and fulfil their potential. As you know, I have tasked officials to review the position in relation to both the provision, and the relevant legislation, and to bring forward recommendations.  At that point I will be in a better position to confirm what can be delivered within this shortened mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the recommendation in the Independent Review of Education for a single early intervention and learner support agency.
answer_text: In my statement to the Assembly on 21 October 2024 I set out my response to the Independent Review of Education. Alongside my statement I also provided my Departments response to the report’s recommendations.In relation to facilitating learner centred interventions, under which a single early intervention and learner support agency was suggested, my Department accepts the need to review and improve service delivery.Education services will be reformed through the outworkings of the End-to-End Reviews of Special Educational Needs and School Improvement which focus on the development of high quality, cost-effective services. These reviews will shortly bring forward recommendations on how delivery models can be more responsive, agile and customer focused. Service delivery improvements will be achieved initially through re-configuration and re-focusing of services delivered by the Education Authority and other education bodies. Progress will be closely monitored and, if necessary, more fundamental structural change will be implemented.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the evaluation of the Physician Associate role will be concluded.
answer_text: My Department intends that this evaluation will conclude by the end of the current financial year.  However, this timeframe may now be complicated by the need to take into account any relevant learning from the independent review of PAs in England announced on 20 November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16786/22-27, to detail any additional actions that can be taken to improve safety for those walking between Moira and Moira Train Station.
answer_text: A section of footway from the Motorway Bridge to Moira Train Station was widened to accommodate commuters and pedestrians wishing to go to the station and then further extended by approximately 110 metres from the motorway bridge towards Moira.There are no further works planned at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on proposed capital works for Fort Hill Integrated College and Fort Hill Primary School and Nursery Unit.
answer_text: The proposed capital works for Fort Hill Integrated College (IC) and Fort Hill Integrated Primary School (IPS) and Nursery Unit are in the latter stages of design with the Spatial Coordination and Developed Design stage (RIBA Stage 3) having completed in May 2024.A planning application for the new build was submitted in August 2023, however no decision on the application has been taken at this time.Given the severe constraints on my Department’s capital budget, regrettably this project will be unable to progress to the Technical Design and Pre-tender stage (RIBA Stage 4) and to construction without additional funding being secured. I can assure you that I have and will continue to make the case for significant additional and sustained capital funding for my Department, to permit projects like Fort Hill IC and Fort Hill IPS and Nursery Unit to proceed to construction as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the first Climate Action Plan will contribute toward Northern Ireland meeting its emissions targets contained within the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
answer_text: My department must by regulations set the first three carbon budgets and the emissions reduction targets for 2030 and 2040.  We will deliver on our carbon budgets through the development of a series of Climate Action Plans which will set out the policies and proposals across all sectors to reduce greenhouse gas emissions during the associated five-year period.My department is leading collaboration with all other Departments to develop Northern Ireland’s first Climate Action Plan. Officials are in the process of finalising the draft document for consultation.  I am fully committed to delivering a Climate Action Plan that is transformational, evidence-based and informed by stakeholders.The carbon budget regulations were agreed by the Executive last week and I now aim to bring the draft Climate Action Plan to Executive colleagues as soon as possible seeking their approval to publish the Plan for a 16-week consultation, as required by legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the Education Authority Educational Psychology Service tracks the number of referrals made by Speech and Language Therapists for children of pre-school age; and (ii) the number of these referrals that have been (a) taken forward; and (b) rejected in the last five years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that the Educational Psychology Service records all referrals from Speech and Language Therapists (SLTs) under the category of ‘designated medical officer’.  It does not track the number of referrals made specifically by SLTs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether private pre-school providers that are part of the Pre-school Education Programme Standardisation have access to all Education Authority support that statutory providers have access to.
answer_text: The support needed to transition to a standardised session length varies from setting to setting, based on their particular circumstances and the Education Authority (EA) assessment of need. All funded Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP) providers will be included in the standardisation programme. This includes both statutory and non-statutory pre-school providers of all management types.  The EA has assessed the support required for all providers requesting a transition to a standardised session length from September 2025 that are currently listed for consultation and will provide this support if the proposed changes are approved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on outstanding capital projects for schools in Lagan Valley.
answer_text: The status of ongoing capital projects in the Lagan Valley constituency are as detailed below: School Name Funding ProgrammeStatus Dromore High SchoolMajor CapitalProcurement of Integrated Consultant Team ongoing. Friends’ SchoolMajor Capital On holdLisnagarvey High SchoolMajor Capital On hold Fort Hill Integrated College, Fort Hill Primary School and Nursery UnitMajor Capital Spatial co-ordination and developed design (RIBA Stage 3) completed.  Planning application in progress.Rowandale Integrated Primary SchoolFresh Start AgreementNew build 14 class primary school anticipated to complete in Summer 2025.Beechlawn SchoolSpecial Educational Needs (SEN) Investment Programme.The design proposed for the extension and refurbishment of the building is currently being reviewed, with a view to increasing the provision.Parkview School Special Educational Needs Investment Programme.A feasibility exercise has commented to explore options to provide a five-class accommodation block. Specialist Provision in Mainstream (SPiM)One of the strands of my SEN capital investment programme focuses on the creation of additional specialist provision classes in mainstream schools.  For the 2024/25 school year, 112 new SPiM classes have been created, four of which are in the Lagan Valley constituency.  These are at Carr Primary School (PS), Fort Hill Integrated PS, Old Warren PS and St Colman’s PS. In addition, there are several ongoing minor works capital projects that are too numerous to mention in the Lagan Valley constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has engaged with the Minister for Education in Ireland regarding ongoing funding of the Scoil Spreagtha programme.
answer_text: No, I have not engaged with the Minister for Education in Ireland on the Scoil Spreagtha programme. My Department currently does not provide any funding to this programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the need to re-establish the Northern Ireland Anti-Bullying Forum.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in September 2021. The Department determined that more specific support should be provided to schools and educational settings to support implementation of the Act and the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) was therefore established within the Education Authority (EA) in September 2022.Given that some of the Northern Ireland Anti-Bullying Forum’s (NIABF) activities duplicated the work of the EA’s Primary and Post Primary Behaviour Support and Provisions, including that of ABSIT, the Department decided to cease funding to NIABF from 31 March 2023 to ensure that there was no double funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether work is scheduled to repair the uneven pavements in Moira town centre.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as resurfacing and road maintenance. Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department's Structural Maintenance allocation; however regrettably for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all our roads and footways in a reasonable condition. Whilst there are many roads and footways such as Main Street, Moira, that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year are included in my Department’s annual report to Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council which is scheduled to be delivered on 18 November 2024.My officials will continue to monitor the footways in Moira town centre and all defects meeting current intervention levels will be recorded for repair in line with current limited-service policy guidelines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the support he is providing to statutory and non-statutory youth services.
answer_text: My Department is providing funding of circa £36m for the 2024/25 year to the Education Authority for the provision of statutory and non-statutory youth services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what statutory and non-statutory youth services he has visited or met to date since taking office.
answer_text: Since coming to office I have not visited any statutory youth services, however I have visited or met with the following youth service settings delivered by the community and voluntary sector:Uniformed Organisations - joint meeting with Girls Brigade, Northern Ireland, Boys Brigade Northern Ireland, Girl Guiding Ulster, Scouts Northern Ireland & Scouting Ireland;Youth Action (NI) – joint meeting with Boys and Girls Clubs Northern Ireland, BYTES, YMCA, Youth Initiatives & Youth Work Alliance;Resurgam Youth InitiativeLedley Hall Boys & Girls Club;Fóram na nÓG;Duke of Edinburgh;Girl Guiding Ulster;North Belfast Youth Providers– joint meeting with New Lodge Youth Centre, Ardoyne Youth Centre, Streetbeat, Shankill Womens Centre, Holy Family Youth Centre & Ashton Community Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what actions the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team are taking to support schools with anti-bullying measures.
answer_text: The Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 came into effect in September 2021. The Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority was established in September 2022 and provides support for schools, educational settings, young people and parents to help them understand the requirements of the Act. ABSIT has a focus on preventative education to embed cultures that eradicate bullying. The team also provides children, young people and parents/carers, with support to deal with specific incidents of bullying. In the last academic year September 2023 – August 2024, actions taken by ABSIT to support schools to address bullying type behaviour included:Responding to 289 referrals from parents, advocates, school and other sources. These referrals include tailored support and training for schools. Facilitating 9188 education setting individuals to complete the ABSIT Online Foundational Training which launched in January 2024. Co-developing, with 146 schools, the ABSIT School Leadership Training Programme.  This is a comprehensive professional development programme for Safeguarding, Social, Behavioural and Emotional Wellbeing (SBEW) pastoral leads and SENCOs/Learning Support Coordinators in schools. Producing Addressing Bullying in School Resources that included: Environmental Risk Reduction and Action Plan template; Bullying Concern Assessment Form; Addressing Bullying Behaviour Policy template; Addressing Bullying Behaviour Processes Flow Chart; and a Policy Checklist.Creating Rights Based Information and Guidance Leaflets which are available to children, young people and parents/carers with translations in multiple languages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he intends to establish an Early Years Partnership Board, chaired by himself and the Minister of Health.
answer_text: I am aware of the reference within the Independent Review of Education Report to the establishment of an Early Years Partnership Board.  While I agree that close cooperation between my department and the department of Health is essential to ensuring a coordinated and coherent support infrastructure for children in their early years and beyond, there are existing structures in place to drive this.  They include the recently formed Children and Young People’s Strategy Monitoring and Reporting Board, which includes DE and DOH representation at Deputy Permanent Secretary level.  I have no immediate plans to establish an additional board at this stage but will keep the existing arrangements under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he is in discussions with any Executive colleagues to fund the establishment of an early years and childcare professional body.
answer_text: I have had no discussions with Executive colleagues on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has sought cross-departmental funding to reinstate the school sports programme.
answer_text: The 2024-25 funding envelope that my Department has been allocated faces significant, unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.  Taking the current budgetary situation and other Departmental priorities into account, I have decided to await the outcome of the October monitoring round before making a decision regarding the possible reintroduction of the Sports Programme.  I have asked my officials to consider if there are other models for funding a programme which I will consider in due course, I have also asked them to engage with sporting bodies including Ulster Rugby, the Irish Football Association, the Ulster Gaelic Athletic Association and Ulster Hockey to explore a programme that might be taken forward in the next financial year should funding become available.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the business plan of the Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Education in relation to a potential subsidy scheme for early learning and childcare workers to help improve their salary.
